rows.plain: Fewer product containers should be crushed when the carton flaps are folded shut. This prevents damaged products and wasted containers.
rows.imp_rat: Product containers crushed as carton flaps close is a direct failure of the closing step central to cartoning, so relevance is very high; crushed containers become scrap and can leak, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Standard closing mechanisms are consistent once set, but dimensional variation and marginal fill heights still produce occasional crushing during flap closure. Flap-closing occurs at line speed and damage does not primarily manifest as lost time. Correct compression bar and flap-folder adjustment requires setup skill to avoid crushing tall or fragile containers. Crushed containers are scrapped product plus wasted carton, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons containing an incorrect sterile-unit quantity
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes that contain the wrong number of sterile products. This helps ensure customers receive the quantity shown on the box.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect sterile-unit quantity in a carton is a core content-correctness failure with severe dosing/supply and recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Count-detection sensors and end-of-line checkweighers reliably catch most quantity errors. Count verification is handled inline by machine sensors with negligible added time. Correct quantity depends on machine setup and sensor function more than operator skill. Incorrect counts cause rejects and complaints, justifying inline count controls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with coding or label content that does not match the approved configuration
rows.plain: Make sure every carton has the correct printed codes and label information for its approved setup. This prevents packages from being identified or used incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Coding or label content not matching approved configuration is a labeling-integrity failure central to correct release with severe regulatory/recall consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Automated code-inspection and first-off checks make label-content mismatches well controlled. Code and label content checks occur at start-up and periodically without significant time burden. Verification of coding relies on operator checks and increasingly automated code inspection. Incorrect coding leads to costly holds and rework, so verification is prioritized.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of discharged cartons released downstream before required inspection is completed
rows.plain: Keep cartons from moving to the next step until the required inspection is finished. This helps prevent unchecked or faulty cartons from being sent onward.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing cartons downstream before required inspection completes bypasses the verification step of the job (high relevance) and allows unverified, potentially defective product to escape (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Machine interlocks that gate discharge on inspection completion are standard and dependable, with only rare bypass or sensor faults. Interlocks hold cartons until inspection completes, adding negligible delay to the flow. Configuring the release interlock requires setup knowledge but demands little operator vigilance afterward. Preventing premature release avoids downstream waste, and interlock hardware is a modest, well-justified cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton dimensions force or bind sterile units during insertion
rows.plain: Make sure the carton is not so tight that it pushes against or damages the germ-free units during insertion. This helps keep the units intact and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Carton dimensions forcing or binding units during insertion is a fit failure central to safe insertion, severe because binding can damage containers.
rows.sat_rat: Correct tooling largely prevents binding, but dimensional variation still forces units inconsistently. Binding during insertion causes immediate jams and stoppages. Preventing bind depends on correct format-part selection by setup techs. Forced insertion damages units and cartons, a recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons with erection, labeling, or insertion deviations that pass unrecognized
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that have problems with being assembled, labeled, or filled but are allowed through without anyone noticing. Finding these problems prevents incorrect cartons from continuing.
rows.imp_rat: Erection/labeling/insertion deviations passing unrecognized is a detection escape moderately central to release with severe nonconformance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection reduces but does not eliminate deviations that pass unrecognized, a recurring pain. Unrecognized deviations by definition are not caught in time. Recognizing subtle erection/labeling/insertion deviations depends heavily on operator experience. Unrecognized deviations lead to downstream scrap and investigation costs.

rows.source_job: Drain residual fluids from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual fluids escaping into the surrounding sterile fill-finish facility during drainage
rows.plain: Prevent leftover liquid from leaking into the surrounding clean facility while it is drained from the machine. This helps keep the area clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Residual fluids escaping into a sterile fill-finish facility during drainage is somewhat peripheral to cartoning mechanics (moderate relevance) but risks batch contamination and regulatory failure with severe consequences (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Containment methods exist but drainage in a sterile environment still risks recurring escape onto controlled floors. Contained drainage inside a sterile suite requires slow, careful staging to avoid escape. Preventing fluid escape into a sterile fill-finish room demands cleanroom discipline and drainage know-how. Containment trays, sterile-compatible absorbents, and cleanup add meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sealing material entering the carton and contacting product containers
rows.plain: Keep pieces of sealing material from falling into the carton and touching the product containers. This helps protect the containers from foreign material.
rows.imp_rat: Sealing material shedding into the carton and contacting product containers directly undermines the packaging job's protective purpose, so relevance is high; contaminated product reaching consumers is a severe quality and compliance failure.
rows.sat_rat: Adhesive and film application is fairly well controlled but stray glue strings and trim debris entering cartons still occur intermittently without reliable automatic detection. Detecting stray sealing material rarely adds process time when controls work, but rework does when it fails. Preventing glue strings or film scraps entering cartons depends partly on setup skill and maintenance of applicators. Contaminated product is scrapped, so failures carry direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with sterile-unit quantities or component arrangements outside the defined secondary configuration
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that contain the wrong number of sterile units or have their parts arranged incorrectly. This helps ensure every carton matches the approved packing design.
rows.imp_rat: Quantities or arrangements outside the defined secondary configuration is a content-correctness failure central to the job with severe nonconformance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Setup validation and inspection make configuration errors uncommon, though arrangement defects still slip. Configuration verification is largely inline with modest delay. Correct configuration setup relies on skilled changeover technicians. Out-of-configuration cartons cause rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury from unexpected machine movement or stored-energy release during fault repair
rows.plain: Prevent workers from being injured by sudden machine movement or the release of stored power while fixing a fault.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury from unexpected movement or stored-energy release during fault repair is central to safe maintenance (relevance) and severe with potential for serious harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature, regulated LOTO practice makes protection during fault repair highly consistent, with residual failures mostly from procedural shortcuts. LOTO adds procedural time before repair but this is universally built into maintenance planning. Lockout/tagout is standard trained practice that any qualified technician is expected to execute without deep expertise. Locks, tags and hasps are low-cost and widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that installed guards leave hazardous areas of the cartoning machinery accessible
rows.plain: Make sure the protective covers do not leave dangerous moving parts of the box-packing machine within reach.
rows.imp_rat: Guards leaving hazardous areas accessible directly undermines physical safeguarding (relevance) and can result in contact injuries with high-speed carton mechanisms (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized guarding designs and risk assessments reliably enclose most hazards, though awkward access points occasionally slip through. Guard fit-up and gap checks during preparation are done efficiently against documented layouts. Assessing residual access requires trained safety personnel applying reach and gap standards, a manageable but real skill demand. Fixed and interlocked guarding is a standard included cost, though retrofits to close gaps can add expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of sterile units being misoriented or incompletely seated within cartons
rows.plain: Products should be placed in cartons facing the right way and sitting fully in place. This helps keep the packages consistent and prevents packing errors.
rows.imp_rat: Misoriented or incompletely seated units is a direct insertion-quality failure central to the CFJ, severe due to potential damage and nonconformance.
rows.sat_rat: Guides, sensors, and reject stations handle most misseating but occasional escapes persist. Seating verification is inline and does not add meaningful time. Proper seating relies on machine setup and format tooling rather than operator skill. Misoriented units cause rejects and jams that waste material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which demonstrated sustained throughput falls below the required throughput under intended production conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment can consistently produce close to the required amount under normal working conditions. This shows whether it is suitable for the intended job.
rows.imp_rat: Sustained throughput falling below required rate strikes at the very purpose of the cartoning machine, so relevance is high; severity is meaningful via lost output but is an economic rather than safety consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers quote nameplate speeds while sustained throughput under intended conditions varies, so shortfalls are a recurring and imperfectly guarded surprise. Throughput demonstration via FAT/SAT is a scheduled step but repeat trials when rates fall short add delay. Interpreting rated vs. sustained throughput under real product conditions requires experienced process engineers who understand OEE deductions. Underperformance discovered late forces costly rework or line rebalancing, and validation runs consume material and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile-product packaging requirements omitted from evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the criteria used to compare solutions include all important requirements for packaging sterile products. This helps prevent choosing a solution that could put the product at risk.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical sterile-product requirements from evaluation criteria means defects go unchecked; moderately central but severe because escaping defects reach patients.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement capture today depends on human recall and fragmented sources, so critical omissions recur despite review gates. Assembling complete sterile-product requirement sets pulls from many documents and takes considerable elapsed time. Knowing which sterile-packaging requirements are critical enough to include leans heavily on regulatory and QA expertise that few individuals hold. Omitted critical requirements are inexpensive to prevent but very costly when they force late redesign.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of products damaged during cartoning when incoming dimensions or weights vary
rows.plain: Reduce damage to products when the incoming items differ in size or weight. The packing machine should handle these normal differences without harming the products.
rows.imp_rat: Product damage when incoming dimensions or weights vary tests the machine's ability to do its core job under real-world variation, so relevance is high; damage is scrap but generally recoverable via adjustment, giving moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Most cartoners are set for nominal dimensions and lack adaptive handling, so variation in incoming product size or weight reliably produces elevated damage — a poorly served pain. Adapting to varying incoming dimensions often forces line slowdowns, but the core loss is defects not delay. Compensating for input variation demands considerable operator skill and constant adjustment, a heavy vigilance burden. Input variation drives elevated scrap and rework costs that are hard to contain.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of carton blanks carrying particulate or microbial contamination after magazine loading
rows.plain: Keep dirt and germs off the flat carton pieces as they are loaded into the machine. Clean carton pieces help protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Carton blanks carrying particulate or microbial contamination after loading is moderately central to sterile packaging, with high severity given microbial ingress into product-contact packaging.
rows.sat_rat: There is little inline particulate or microbial detection on blanks, so this need is largely unaddressed by standard cartoner practice. Contamination is rarely detected in real time, so any check occurs long after loading. Preventing contamination depends on handling discipline and hygiene practices rather than machine safeguards. Contaminated blanks risk product recalls, a high-cost exposure with minimal inline mitigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons left partially open or insecurely closed after cartoning
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes that are left partly open or not securely closed after packing. Securely closed boxes help protect what is inside.
rows.imp_rat: Partially open or insecurely closed cartons is a direct closure failure central to protection with severe integrity and sterility-exposure consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Closure sensors reliably detect open/insecure cartons, with rare escapes. Open-flap detection is inline. Automated sensing reduces operator dependence. Insecure closures cause rejects and complaints.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons missing required label content
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that are missing required information on their labels. Complete labels help people identify and handle the cartons correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons missing required label content is a labeling-compliance failure central to release with severe regulatory and patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Vision reliably detects missing content when the reference is correct, though reference errors escape. Content verification is inline via OCR/vision. Automated content checks reduce manual proofreading load. Missing label content causes rejects and recall risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified carton arrangement damages a sterile unit or its label during insertion
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the planned carton arrangement damages a germ-free item or its label when the item is put into the carton. The arrangement should protect both the item and its information.
rows.imp_rat: A carton arrangement that damages a unit or label during insertion threatens integrity; relevant to safe insertion and severe when it breaches product.
rows.sat_rat: Mature cartoner tooling handles gentle insertion reliably for validated formats, with damage mainly on marginal or new configurations. Insertion damage checks are handled during setup qualification without ongoing time penalty. Mechanical tooling and format parts are engineered by specialists, reducing reliance on operator finesse during running. Damaged units and labels are discarded, but well-designed insertion keeps this loss low in steady state.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cartons containing a product variant that does not match the intended carton
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that contain the wrong version of a product. This helps ensure each carton has the product it is supposed to contain.
rows.imp_rat: A carton containing the wrong product variant is a fundamental mispack that defeats the packaging job, so relevance is high; wrong-variant packs pose labeling, allergen, or dosing risks that can trigger recalls, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode and vision checks catch most variant mismatches, but changeover setup errors and unscanned SKUs leave a residual mismatch rate. Automated variant verification adds no meaningful cycle time when configured. Vision and code-reading systems reduce dependence on operators visually distinguishing similar variants. Vision/scanner systems carry moderate cost that is standard on mixed-variant lines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that serialization criteria allow insertion conditions that compromise sterile-barrier integrity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that rules for giving packages unique identifying information allow handling that damages their protective germ-free seal. The rules should protect the package while it is being identified and placed.
rows.imp_rat: If serialization criteria permit sterility-compromising insertion conditions, it indirectly threatens the barrier; moderately relevant but severe in outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization and sterility disciplines are often specified separately, so conflicting conditions are caught inconsistently rather than by a robust integrated practice. Checking that serialization criteria don't force barrier-compromising insertion is done during design reviews without significant time cost. Bridging serialization requirements with sterile-barrier constraints demands scarce cross-disciplinary expertise that the status quo does not fully supply. A barrier compromise reaching product is extremely costly, so latent exposure is high even if occurrences are infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton seals reopening during downstream conveyance
rows.plain: Reduce the number of carton seals that come open while the cartons move through the rest of the process. This helps keep the products protected inside.
rows.imp_rat: Seals reopening during downstream conveyance defeats the durability of the cartoning outcome (high relevance) and exposes product to contamination, damage, or loss after the machine (fairly high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Adhesive and compression systems are established, but glue quality, temperature drift, and compression dwell variation still cause intermittent seal reopening. Seal reopening typically manifests downstream after the machine cycle, so detection is delayed beyond the cartoner itself. Achieving durable seals depends on operator skill in setting glue temperature, compression, and dwell. Reopened seals cause downstream jams and rejected product, and glue-system variability keeps this cost recurring.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of sustained compression on sterile units during machine stoppages
rows.plain: Keep sterile products from being squeezed for a long time when the machine stops. This helps protect the products from damage during a stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Sustained compression during stoppages can crush primary containers; moderately relevant to handling protection and severe due to potential sterility breach.
rows.sat_rat: Few machines automatically relieve compression on stop, so outcomes depend on chance duration of the stoppage. Sustained compression accrues silently during stoppages with no timer prompting operators to relieve it. Recognizing that stopped units are being crushed requires attentive judgement not consistently applied. Crushed sterile units during stoppages become scrap, a real but intermittent loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of using an obsolete specification revision to configure the next carton format
rows.plain: Use the current instructions when setting up the next carton size, rather than an outdated version. This helps prevent the changeover from being set up incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Using an obsolete spec revision to configure a format is a configuration-control failure central to correct output with severe compliance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Document control systems largely prevent obsolete-revision use, though manual copies still slip through. Checking revision currency is quick when document control works. Low skill—mostly a matter of verifying revision status. Using an obsolete revision can scrap a whole run.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons crushed, torn, or deformed during cartoning
rows.plain: Prevent cartons from being crushed, torn, or misshapen while they are being packed. This keeps the packages usable and protects their contents.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons crushed, torn, or deformed during cartoning is a direct handling-quality failure central to protection with severe integrity consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Vision and ejection reliably remove deformed cartons, though machine wear causes intermittent damage. Damaged-carton detection is inline. Machine setup skill reduces mechanical damage rates. Crushed/torn cartons are scrapped, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons leaving the production run with incomplete closure
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes that leave the production run without being fully closed. This helps protect the products during handling and transport.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons leaving with incomplete closure directly fails the seal/finish outcome of cartoning (high relevance) and can spoil, spill, or compromise product integrity downstream (fairly high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closure sensors and mechanical guides are mature, yet stock variation and tooling wear still produce occasional incomplete closures. Closure verification occurs inline so incomplete closures are flagged within the cycle. Closure tooling adjustment needs a trained setter, but routine operation is largely hands-off. Incompletely closed cartons cause rejects and rework, mitigated well by closure detection but not fully eliminated.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cartons containing products in the wrong position or orientation
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes in which products are placed incorrectly or facing the wrong way. This helps ensure products are packed properly and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Wrong position/orientation of products in cartons is central to producing correct packs (high relevance), though consequences are typically rework or aesthetic/functional flaws rather than catastrophic harm (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Orienting feeders and guides work well for stable products, but position and orientation errors persist for irregular or unstable items where tooling cannot fully constrain placement. Orientation control is handled at line speed and is not primarily a time problem. Correct orientation depends on skilled setup of orienting tooling and feeders for each product. Mis-oriented product usually means rework or scrap rather than throughput loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of accepted cartons that are incompletely erected, unclosed, crushed, or torn
rows.plain: Reduce the number of accepted boxes that are not fully put together, not closed, crushed, or torn. This helps keep products protected and presentable.
rows.imp_rat: Incompletely erected, unclosed, crushed, or torn accepted cartons is a core erection/closure failure with severe protection and appearance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Erection and closure inspection reliably rejects most defective cartons, though borderline defects occasionally pass. Vision and ejection handle crushed/torn cartons inline immediately. Detection is automated, reducing operator dependence. Rejected cartons and blanks are scrapped, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with illegible label text or machine-readable content
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes whose printed words or scannable markings cannot be read. Clear labels help people identify and handle the product correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Illegible label text or machine-readable content breaks identification/traceability; central to compliant release with severe recall and dispensing consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Code grading and OCV reliably catch illegible content, making this well controlled. Barcode grading and OCR run inline instantly. Automated grading removes reliance on operator readability judgement. Illegible codes cause rejects and downstream scanning failures with cost impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between operator recognition of an imminent hazard and emergency-stop actuation
rows.plain: Press the emergency stop as soon as an operator notices an immediate danger. Acting quickly can reduce the chance or severity of harm.
rows.imp_rat: Minimizing time from hazard recognition to e-stop actuation is important for effective intervention (relevance) and reduces injury severity, though physical safeguards remain the primary protection (severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no mature automatic hazard-detection layer in typical cartoners, so the human-in-the-loop delay makes response time inconsistent and unpredictable. The delay between hazard recognition and actuation is dominated by unaided human reaction time, which standard hardware placement improves only marginally. Fast recognition depends on trained attentiveness and situational awareness, which vary widely between operators. Improving response mainly involves training and ergonomic layout rather than significant expenditure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine settings that do not match the approved batch setup when readiness is confirmed
rows.plain: When the machine is ready, make sure its settings match the approved setup for the batch. This helps ensure the cartons are made correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Machine settings not matching approved batch setup at readiness confirmation is core to producing conforming cartons, with serious consequences of running an entire batch incorrectly before detection.
rows.sat_rat: Servo-recipe systems restore settings consistently, but manual mechanical adjustments still escape automatic verification at times. Automated readiness confirmation checks settings at run start without significant delay. Judging whether every setting matches the approved setup requires experienced setter oversight. Mismatched settings produce defective cartons and rework that recipe recall reduces meaningfully.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from the onset of a carton formation, filling, or sealing deviation to operator recognition
rows.plain: Notice as quickly as possible when cartons are being formed, filled, or sealed incorrectly. Early notice allows the operator to correct the problem sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Fast operator recognition of formation, filling, or sealing deviations is central to keeping the cartoning run producing good output (high relevance); delayed recognition means accumulating scrap and downtime, a serious but recoverable consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor triggers are consistent for hard faults, but slow-developing deviations are recognized inconsistently and often late. Inline sensing shortens deviation-to-recognition to seconds where instrumented, but manual monitoring still lags on subtle deviations. Early recognition of subtle formation or sealing drift still depends on an experienced operator's eye. Faster recognition limits the volume of defective cartons produced, containing scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a closed carton reopens during downstream handling
rows.plain: Make sure a closed carton stays shut while it is being handled later. This helps protect the items inside from falling out or being exposed.
rows.imp_rat: A closed carton reopening downstream defeats the closure job's purpose, exposing units with severe integrity and sterility consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Hot-melt glue and tuck-lock closures are mature and hold well when set correctly, with occasional pop-open failures. Reopened cartons are found downstream, causing rework delays. Reliable closure depends on correct glue/tuck setup by skilled staff. Reopened cartons cause rework and rejects, moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton jams that stop the scheduled production run
rows.plain: Reduce carton jams that interrupt the planned production run.
rows.imp_rat: Carton jams that stop the scheduled run directly defeat the machine's core job of continuous carton production (very high relevance); each stoppage causes lost throughput but is typically cleared and recovered (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern cartoners with servo control and gentle handling reduce jams substantially, but jams remain a routine run interruption. Jams stop the run and clearing them consumes noticeable downtime despite quick-access designs. Clearing and preventing recurring jams draws on operator troubleshooting skill. Jam-induced stoppages waste production time and sometimes product, a recurring cost only partly reduced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering sterile product-contact areas while the machine is idle
rows.plain: Keep germs and other unwanted material from getting into the parts that touch the product while the machine is not running. This helps keep the product safe when production resumes.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering sterile product-contact areas while idle is somewhat peripheral to active cartoning but severity is very high because undetected idle-state ingress compromises the next sterile run.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring and protective covers keep idle contamination fairly well controlled, with breaches occurring mainly during transitions in and out of idle. Covering openings and maintaining idle-state controls is quick. Maintaining aseptic integrity while idle relies on routine but attentive practice. Covers, sterile barriers and environmental monitoring are modest ongoing costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of restarting cartoning before the interrupting condition is fully corrected
rows.plain: Do not restart boxing products until whatever stopped the machine has been completely fixed. This helps prevent the process from starting again while it is still unsafe or incorrect.
rows.imp_rat: Restarting before the condition is corrected risks propagating defects/damage; moderately central to safe recovery and severe in potential consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Restart interlocks help but operators can override or misjudge readiness, so premature restarts still occur. Verifying full correction before restart delays resuming production. Judging whether a condition is fully corrected before restart requires experienced judgement. Premature restart scraps more material, an avoidable recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that serialization criteria permit duplicate sterile-unit identifiers within the identification scope
rows.plain: Make sure the rules for assigning serial numbers cannot give two sterile units the same identifier. This allows each unit to be tracked separately.
rows.imp_rat: Serialization criteria permitting duplicate identifiers corrupts unique traceability; moderately central and severe due to compliance and recall-tracking failure.
rows.sat_rat: Uniqueness enforcement within an identification scope is a mature, well-solved capability of serialization systems. Duplicate-prevention is enforced at number generation with no runtime cost. Serialization number management systems handle uniqueness automatically, requiring minimal user skill. Duplicate identifiers would cause costly data conflicts, but systems make this rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator selects an emergency-stop control that does not arrest the hazardous motion
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that someone presses an emergency-stop control that does not stop the dangerous movement. This helps protect people when an emergency occurs.
rows.imp_rat: Operator selecting an e-stop that does not arrest motion is a serious human-factors gap that delays hazard control (relevance) and produces severe injury when the wrong control is used in an emergency (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Most well-engineered machines wire all local e-stops to a common stop group so any button works, but on multi-zone integrated lines mismatched coverage still occasionally leaves an operator pressing an ineffective control. Reaching a functional stop is instantaneous once the correct control is pressed, so time is not the limiting factor. Under stress an operator may misjudge which of several e-stops on a line covers the specific hazard zone, so some cognitive reliance remains despite grouping conventions. Providing multiple correctly-zoned e-stops adds wiring and device cost but is standard on well-designed lines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of erecting a carton format that does not match the batch specification
rows.plain: Set up the carton style that matches the production run's requirements. Using the wrong carton could lead to packing or labeling problems.
rows.imp_rat: Erecting a format not matching the batch spec is a wrong-configuration failure central to correct packaging with severe compliance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Setup checklists and format-part controls make format verification fairly consistent, though human verification still fails occasionally. Wrong-format detection at startup is quick when checklists are followed. Verifying carton format against batch spec relies on operator attention and reading skill. Wrong-format runs waste materials and time, but are usually caught early.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required material, dimensional, closure, or tamper-evidence attributes missing from carton specifications
rows.plain: Reduce missing requirements in carton specifications, including the materials, size, closure, and evidence that the carton has not been opened. This helps ensure the packaging meets safety and legal requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Missing material/dimensional/closure/tamper attributes from specs is an upstream completeness gap moderately central to the job, severe if tamper-evidence is lost.
rows.sat_rat: Spec templates capture most attributes, yet gaps in closure or tamper-evidence detail still recur. Compiling complete carton attribute specifications is a documentation-heavy task that takes real effort. Knowing every required material, dimensional, closure, and tamper-evidence attribute demands specification expertise. Missing attributes trigger rework and delay but the specification effort itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of contamination introduced into the sterile fill-finish area while pneumatic components are open
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of dirt or germs brought into the clean filling area while air-powered parts are open. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the sterile fill-finish area while pneumatic components are open is somewhat tangential to the mechanical cartoning job (moderate relevance) but carries catastrophic consequences for product sterility and patient safety (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Open pneumatic components expose internal surfaces and oil mist to the sterile area, and current containment practices only partly control this, leaving notable residual risk. Opening pneumatic components in an aseptic area forces added containment and re-sanitization time. Servicing open pneumatic parts without contaminating the sterile field requires above-average aseptic discipline. Portable containment and requalification after opening lines are costly per event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator cannot reach the emergency-stop control without entering the hazard zone
rows.plain: Make sure the worker can press the emergency-stop button without having to enter the dangerous area. This allows the machine to be stopped quickly during an emergency.
rows.imp_rat: Requiring entry into the hazard zone to reach the e-stop critically hampers timely response (relevance) and severely raises injury risk because the operator must approach the danger to stop it (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice mandates accessible e-stops at each workstation, but on large or retrofitted cartoners some hazard points still lack a control within reach without approaching the danger. Correctly placed controls let the operator actuate without an approach delay, supporting fast response. Proper placement removes any need for the operator to know a special reach path, requiring no skill. Adding pull-cords, extra buttons, and perimeter devices is inexpensive relative to the machine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the emergency-stop control disengages before the hazard is cleared
rows.plain: Once the emergency stop is pressed, it should stay active until the danger has passed. This helps prevent the machine from starting again too soon.
rows.imp_rat: An e-stop that disengages before the hazard clears exposes personnel prematurely, making it very relevant to safe intervention and severe because injury can occur during re-energization.
rows.sat_rat: Positive-latching, deliberate-reset e-stop hardware per ISO 13850 makes premature disengagement before hazard clearance very uncommon in standard installations. Latching e-stop devices hold the stop condition indefinitely, so there is no time pressure on the operator to keep the hazard suppressed. The mechanical latch-and-twist-release design means no operator judgement is needed to keep the control engaged. Latching e-stop buttons are cheap, standardized components carrying negligible incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between criterion weighting and patient-safety and compliance risks
rows.plain: Make sure the importance given to each judging factor reflects the risks to patient safety and compliance with required rules. This helps decision-makers focus on what matters most.
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned criterion weighting versus safety/compliance risks is an upstream evaluation issue peripheral to running the machine but severe if it deprioritizes safety.
rows.sat_rat: Weighting stays subjective and inconsistent, so alignment to actual risk is unreliable. Aligning criterion weights to risk requires risk-workshop time that is often abbreviated. Correctly weighting criteria against patient-safety and compliance risk demands scarce risk-analysis judgement. Misweighting has no direct material cost but drives poor selection decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for erection, insertion, closure, and labeling
rows.plain: Make the rules for judging whether items were put together, inserted, closed, and labeled correctly as clear as possible. Clear rules help people make consistent decisions about whether the packaging works.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance criteria for the key steps undermines consistent judgment; moderately central and severe if it causes wrong accept/reject decisions.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguity leads to inconsistent acceptance decisions that undermine repeatable outcomes. Drafting unambiguous acceptance criteria across four operations is a slow, iterative wording exercise. Writing crisp, testable criteria requires rare technical-writing plus process expertise, and ambiguity persists where that skill is thin. Ambiguous criteria cause disputes and rework rather than direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material entering glue, tape, adhesive, or carton blank supplies during replenishment
rows.plain: Keep dirt and other unwanted material out of glue, tape, adhesive, and carton supplies while they are being added. Clean supplies help the packaging work properly and protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material entering glue, tape, or blank supplies during replenishment is moderately relevant to consumable integrity, with serious consequences of contaminated adhesives affecting seal safety and product.
rows.sat_rat: Covered hoppers and filtered adhesive systems help marginally, but replenishment remains an open, largely unguarded contamination pathway. Foreign-material intrusion during replenishment is typically noticed only after downstream problems arise. Avoiding contamination in supplies rests on operator care and housekeeping, with no automatic safeguard. Foreign material in glue or blanks can spoil output and trigger recalls, an expensive and poorly contained risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of accepted cartons with missing or incorrect secondary components
rows.plain: Reduce the number of accepted boxes that are missing required parts or contain the wrong parts. This helps ensure customers receive complete and correct packages.
rows.imp_rat: Accepted cartons with missing or incorrect secondary components is a content-completeness escape with severe compliance and safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Detection is reliable for presence but incorrect-component identity is harder to catch, leaving a residual gap. Inline presence/verification checks add little delay. Automation reduces dependence on operator verification of components. Missing/incorrect components drive rejects and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of mismatch between expected quality states and the active secondary-package configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the outer packaging being used matches the quality condition expected for the products. This helps prevent the wrong packaging setup from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between expected quality states and active configuration risks wrong disposition; moderately central and severe if nonconforming product is accepted.
rows.sat_rat: State-configuration alignment is partly manual and error-prone, a recurring gap. Reconciling quality states with active configuration is often manual and lagging. Requires QA judgement to match states to configurations. Mismatches cause holds and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons missing required secondary components
rows.plain: Reduce the number of boxes that are missing required extra packaging parts. This helps ensure each package is complete and protects the product as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Missing required secondary components is a completeness failure central to correct secondary packaging with severe compliance/usability consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Presence detection reliably catches most missing components, with occasional undetected omissions. Missing-component detection via sensors is inline and fast. Sensor presence checks reduce reliance on manual verification. Missing secondary components cause rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required safety functions left unverified after functional checks
rows.plain: Make sure every required safety feature is checked and confirmed to work. Any safety feature left unchecked could fail without being noticed.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving required safety functions unverified after checks means unconfirmed safeguards (relevance) and can allow undetected failures leading to injury (severity), though it is one step removed from the hazard itself.
rows.sat_rat: Structured checklists and CMMS task lists make full coverage the norm, though a function occasionally goes unverified when the list is outdated or a step is skipped. Running the full battery of required safety-function checks is time-consuming but scheduled into planned maintenance windows. Knowing the complete set of required safety functions demands training and up-to-date reference to the machine's safety validation, which competent techs generally have. Comprehensive checklists cost little to maintain and reduce expensive rework or incident exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of carton-derived particulate released during erection
rows.plain: Keep the amount of dust and tiny carton pieces released while boxes are being opened and shaped as low as possible. This helps keep the work area and products clean.
rows.imp_rat: Carton-derived particulate during erection is a contamination risk to sterile units; moderately central to clean cartoning and severe if particulate reaches product.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on enclosure guarding and vacuum extraction that only partly control shedding, so contamination risk remains an inconsistent, unsolved pain. Particulate release happens continuously during erection and cannot be quickly addressed once cartons run, so operators tolerate it in real time. Reducing carton dust depends largely on carton-blank quality and machine tooling rather than operator skill, leaving little the executor can do. Chronic particulate drives cleaning cycles and occasional rejected units, a persistent low-level material and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between registered market configurations and approved production specifications
rows.plain: Keep the product versions registered for sale consistent with the approved instructions for making them. This helps ensure the products are made according to the accepted requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between registered market configs and approved specs risk wrong-market release; moderately central and severe due to regulatory consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Document control catches many discrepancies but cross-market spec conflicts persist. Reconciling registered vs approved specs is a periodic manual review. Requires regulatory/QA expertise to catch discrepancies. Spec discrepancies risk costly compliance failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile-unit identifiers become unreadable during insertion or carton erection
rows.plain: Keep the identifying marks on sterile items clear and readable while the items are inserted or placed into cartons. This helps ensure each item can still be correctly tracked.
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable identifiers after insertion/erection break traceability; moderately central and severe because they impede serialization and recall capability.
rows.sat_rat: Inline print-and-verify with grade cameras is a mature standard practice that keeps unreadable identifiers uncommon in production. Readability verification is done inline by vision systems without slowing the line. Automated code-grading offloads the judgement of readability from operators. Unreadable identifiers cause rejects and rework, but grading catches most before they propagate, limiting cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of labeled sterile units inserted in the wrong position or orientation while flow is established
rows.plain: Place fewer labeled sterile units in the wrong spot or facing the wrong way while materials are first being moved through the process. This helps prevent handling and production errors.
rows.imp_rat: Units inserted in wrong position/orientation while flow is established is an insertion-quality failure central to the CFJ with severe usability consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Tooling constrains orientation but early-flow mispositioning is frequent before stabilization. Correcting insertion orientation while establishing flow costs setup time. Getting position/orientation right during flow establishment needs finesse. Wrong-position inserts scrap trial units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of operating modes and access points omitted from safety-system verification
rows.plain: Check every way the system can operate and every place people can enter or reach in when testing the safety system. This reduces the chance that an unsafe situation is missed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting operating modes or access points from verification is moderately relevant as a coverage gap and severe when an unverified mode conceals a dangerous fault, though administrative in nature.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage gaps are a recognized weak point because rarely-used modes and secondary access points are commonly omitted from verification. Exhaustively cycling every mode and access point during preparation is time-consuming and often abbreviated under schedule pressure. Knowing which modes and access points must be tested demands experienced commissioning judgement that varies by person. Thorough coverage adds labor hours but no major material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of flap-folding jams halting carton flow
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times folded carton flaps get stuck and stop the cartons from moving. This keeps cartons flowing smoothly for closing and sealing.
rows.imp_rat: Flap-folding jams halting carton flow strike at the core carton-forming function of the machine (high relevance); they halt production and generate scrap but are recoverable interruptions (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-engineered folding sections are reliable for consistent stock, but stiffness and crease variation still trigger periodic folding jams. Flap-folding jams halt flow and require operator intervention, adding downtime. Preventing folding jams needs skilled setup of plows, rails, and timing. Each halt wastes throughput and occasionally damages cartons, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with missing, incorrect, or extra configuration components
rows.plain: Make sure every carton has the correct parts, with nothing missing or added by mistake. This helps ensure the package is assembled as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Missing, incorrect, or extra configuration components is a content-completeness failure central to correct secondary packaging with severe compliance impact.
rows.sat_rat: Inline detection of missing leaflets/components is common but occasional escapes still occur. Component presence checks are integrated into the run with minimal delay. Ensuring all inserts and components are present requires operator attention during setup and running. Missing or extra components trigger rework and material loss, driving inspection effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons providing inadequate sterile-unit clearance or restraint
rows.plain: Make sure cartons hold sterile units securely and leave enough room around them. This helps prevent the units from being damaged or losing their sterility during handling.
rows.imp_rat: Inadequate clearance or restraint risks unit movement/damage in transit; moderately central to fit correctness and severe if it leads to damage.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional checks and samples catch most clearance issues but tolerance stack-ups still produce inadequate cartons at times. Checking clearance and restraint across carton designs requires fit trials that take time to complete. Specifying adequate clearance and restraint draws on dimensional and materials expertise. Inadequate clearance leads to rejected cartons and rework, a recurring material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between evaluation criteria and required sterile-unit, carton, label, and secondary-packaging configurations
rows.plain: Make sure the rules used to judge possible solutions match the required product, carton, label, and packaging arrangements. This helps ensure the chosen solution fits what must actually be packed.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched evaluation criteria against required configurations means verification checks the wrong things; moderately central to the job and severe because bad units can be accepted.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on checklists and expert review that still leave criteria misaligned with actual configurations often enough to cause downstream surprises. Reconciling evaluation criteria against every configuration variant is a slow, manual cross-referencing exercise that lengthens the specification cycle. Catching mismatches demands seasoned packaging engineers who deeply understand sterile-unit, carton, and label interactions, a scarce expertise. Missed mismatches surface late as requalification and rework, but the direct evaluation activity itself is relatively cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of replenishments involving a consumable that does not match the active production batch
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times workers add a supply that is not meant for the production batch currently being made. This helps prevent mix-ups and protects the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Replenishing with a consumable not matching the active batch is relevant to run integrity, with moderately serious consequences of contaminated or mislabeled cartons requiring rework.
rows.sat_rat: Few lines have scan-verified replenishment, so wrong-consumable errors during refill remain a common, poorly guarded failure. Replenishment checks are quick but happen mid-run when attention is divided. Verifying a consumable matches the active batch relies almost entirely on operator vigilance during refills. A mismatched consumable can contaminate a whole run, a costly error only loosely controlled today.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in determining whether observed safety-function responses meet approved acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make it easier to tell clearly whether the safety features responded in an acceptable way. This helps prevent uncertain or disputed safety checks.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in judging safety-function acceptance criteria is moderately central to reliable qualification, and its consequences are serious because an undetected safety failure can injure operators and invalidate validation.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance criteria are often qualitative or under-specified, so different technicians reach different pass/fail conclusions, making outcomes inconsistent. Deliberating over whether a borderline response passes can stall a maintenance window. Judging conformance against acceptance criteria demands experienced interpretation, and ambiguous criteria push heavy reliance on scarce senior judgement. Clearer written acceptance criteria are cheap to produce yet frequently absent or vague in practice.

rows.source_job: Recover reusable components from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from unexpected machine movement or stored-energy release during component extraction
rows.plain: Prevent the technician from being hurt by unexpected machine movement or force released from the machine while removing parts. The machine must be made safe before parts are taken out.
rows.imp_rat: Injury from unexpected movement or stored-energy release during component extraction is a maintenance hazard not central to cartoning throughput, so relevance is moderate; crush or impact injuries make severity very high.
rows.sat_rat: Blocking and de-energization procedures reduce incidents, but disassembly of unfamiliar machines still produces surprise movements. Careful component extraction with movement restraint slows the teardown noticeably. Anticipating stored-energy release and unexpected movement during extraction leans heavily on experienced technician judgement. Rigging, blocking, and restraint gear are moderate costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cartons loaded with an incorrect product quantity
rows.plain: Put the correct number of products in every box, so customers receive the quantity they were promised.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons loaded with an incorrect product quantity is a core count-integrity failure of the cartoning job, so relevance is high; short or over counts trigger customer complaints, regulatory issues, and recalls, giving fairly high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inline counting and checkweighing catch most miscounts reliably, though small or nested items still occasionally evade detection. Count verification is automated inline and does not slow the run when functioning. Checkweighers and count sensors remove reliance on operator counting skill. Sensors and checkweighers are a standard, justified cost on quantity-critical lines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with erection or closure characteristics outside defined tolerances
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that are opened or closed in ways outside the allowed limits. This helps ensure cartons are formed and sealed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Erection or closure characteristics outside tolerance is a direct quality failure of the core sub-jobs with severe protection consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection catches many tolerance excursions but subtle drift within limits is under-monitored. Tolerance checks on erection/closure are inline where instrumented. Interpreting tolerance drift relies on technician judgement. Out-of-tolerance cartons cause rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of rejected output from different runs becoming commingled before disposition
rows.plain: Keep rejected products from different production runs separate until a decision is made about them. This prevents them from being mixed up or handled together by mistake.
rows.imp_rat: Commingling rejected output from different runs before disposition confuses reconciliation; moderately central and severe for traceability/disposition integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Reject bins and labeling exist but manual segregation of rejects across runs is inconsistently enforced. Keeping rejects from different runs separated adds handling time. Requires disciplined labeling and quarantine habits that vary by operator. Commingled rejects can force broader disposition scrap, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile-unit exposure to handling conditions outside approved limits during cartoning
rows.plain: Shorten the time a sterile item is handled in conditions that are outside the approved safe limits while it is being packed into a carton. This helps protect it from becoming contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to handling conditions outside approved limits threatens stability; moderately central and severe if it degrades the sterile product.
rows.sat_rat: Exposure limits are monitored but jams and slowdowns can extend exposure unpredictably. Limiting exposure time depends on line speed and prompt handling that are only partly controlled. Managing exposure duration relies on operator pacing and awareness of limits. Excess exposure can spoil units, an avoidable loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to unexpected machine motion during diagnostic checks
rows.plain: Prevent the machine from moving unexpectedly while the technician checks what is wrong. This reduces the risk of injury during troubleshooting.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to unexpected machine motion during diagnostics is a servicing-safety concern peripheral to producing cartons, so relevance is moderate; being caught by moving mechanisms causes serious injury, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Because faults are frequently diagnosed with power on, exposure to unexpected motion during diagnostics remains a persistent, only partly mitigated risk. Diagnostics often require the machine live, so protective setup and cautious jogging add time without full isolation. Safe live troubleshooting depends heavily on the technician's judgement about what may move, a scarce and error-prone skill. Reduced-speed/jog modes and light curtains exist but are underused, and adding safe-diagnostic modes carries real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons using a format that does not match the approved packaging configuration
rows.plain: Use only cartons that match the approved package design and size. This helps ensure the product is packed and labeled in the intended way.
rows.imp_rat: Wrong carton format versus approved configuration undermines correct secondary packaging; relevant to the job and severe if released nonconforming.
rows.sat_rat: Setup verification against approved specs is a mature control that reliably catches format errors. Confirming approved format at setup is a routine, time-bounded step. Format selection follows documented setup sheets so moderate skill suffices. Wrong-format cartons waste material and cause rework but are usually caught at setup.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with flaps remaining partially open after sealing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons whose flaps are still partly open after sealing. This helps keep the packages closed and protects what is inside.
rows.imp_rat: Flaps remaining partially open after sealing is a direct closure-quality defect central to the job (high relevance), typically causing rework, rejects, or presentation issues (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Folding and sealing mechanisms handle standard stock well, though flap spring-back and crease variation cause intermittent partial openings. Flap-position sensing detects partial openings in real time at the sealing station. Fine-tuning folding rails and plows for reliable flap closure remains a skill-dependent setup task. Partially open flaps generate scrap and rework that inline detection reduces but does not remove.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous energy remaining in the cartoning machine during decommissioning
rows.plain: Make sure no dangerous electrical, mechanical, or stored pressure energy remains in the box-packing machine when it is taken out of service. This helps protect people working on or near it.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous energy remaining during decommissioning is less central to routine cartoning operation (relevance) but severe because uncontrolled stored energy can cause serious injury to dismantling workers (severity).
rows.sat_rat: OSHA/regulatory LOTO practice is mature and widely deployed, so hazardous energy rarely remains where the procedure is followed. Lockout/tagout de-energization procedures are routine and add only modest time to the decommissioning schedule. Trained maintenance staff and qualified electricians routinely perform energy isolation under established LOTO programs. Isolation hardware and procedures are inexpensive and already budgeted into standard decommissioning work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of erecting a carton blank that does not match the current sterile-unit format
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of putting together a carton that is the wrong size or shape for the product being packaged. The correct carton helps protect and properly contain the sterile product.
rows.imp_rat: Erecting a blank not matching the current format disrupts the core erection job and can cause damage or wrong packaging, a serious but often setup-caught error.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode/format checks and job specifications catch most mismatches but visually similar blanks still slip through occasionally. Format verification adds setup time but is a routine gate, not a bottleneck. Correct blank-to-format matching still leans on operator attention and recognition of subtle carton differences. A mismatched blank run wastes materials and forces rework if caught late.

rows.stk: Environmental Health and Safety Specialist / Maintenance Technician
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that personnel are exposed to hazardous substances while decontaminating the machine
rows.plain: Prevent workers from being exposed to harmful substances while cleaning the machine before it is moved. This helps make the transfer safer.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to hazardous substances during decontamination is a maintenance-safety concern outside the running job (low relevance), but chemical/biological exposure is a severe, potentially irreversible health consequence (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination SOPs and PPE reduce exposure well, but manual work still leaves recurring exposure risk. Decontamination is a lengthy manual scrubbing and wipe-down process before release. Safe handling of hazardous cleaning agents and pharma residues requires trained, attentive personnel in proper PPE. PPE, cleaning agents, and validated procedures add substantial cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of erection, insertion, closure, or labeling criteria with undefined acceptance thresholds
rows.plain: Set clear pass-or-fail limits for putting packages together, inserting products, closing packages, and adding labels. This makes it clear when each step has been done correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Undefined acceptance thresholds is a criteria-completeness gap moderately central to consistent judgment, with moderate consequences that setup usually surfaces.
rows.sat_rat: Many criteria still lack quantified thresholds, so pass/fail judgements vary and outcomes are inconsistent. Defining acceptance thresholds for every criterion requires data-gathering and negotiation that lengthens setup. Setting defensible thresholds needs statistical and process knowledge held by few. Undefined thresholds cause inspection disputes and rework rather than raw material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous machinery motion exposing the technician during functional checks
rows.plain: The machine should not move dangerously and put the technician at risk during checks. This helps the technician test the safety features without being hurt.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous motion exposing the technician during functional checks is a servicing-safety error tangential to core carton production, so relevance is moderate; the resulting strike or entrapment injuries make severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Running checks in reduced-speed or guarded modes helps, but functional testing inherently exposes techs to motion, leaving recurring residual risk. Functional checks require running the machine, so safe-operating setup adds modest time. Positioning oneself clear of hazardous motion while observing function relies on trained situational awareness that varies. Guarding and hold-to-run controls are commonly fitted at low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons that fail erection or closure specifications
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that fail to form correctly or close as required. This helps keep packages secure and ready for shipping.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons failing erection or closure specs is a direct quality failure of the erection/closure sub-job with severe protection consequences when released.
rows.sat_rat: Erection/closure inspection reliably catches failures, though marginal closures occasionally pass. Closure inspection is inline and immediate. Detection is automated; setup skill affects rates. Failed closures scrap cartons and product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected machine movement exposing the technician to injury during calibration
rows.plain: Prevent the machine from moving unexpectedly while the technician is checking and adjusting it. This helps protect the technician from injury.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected machine movement during calibration touches the setup work needed to run the job (moderate relevance), and a technician injury is a severe, life-altering consequence when it occurs (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Reduced-speed and hold-to-run practices reduce risk, yet calibration often needs motion present so unexpected movement remains a real hazard. Calibration in a controlled or jog mode adds setup time but fits within planned tasks. Safe calibration near live mechanisms depends on the technician's care and familiarity with the axis being tuned. Calibration fixtures and safe-mode controls are moderate-cost and generally available.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous fluids or adhesives being released during draining, staging, or transfer from the cartoning machine
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous liquids or glue from being released while they are drained, moved, or prepared for disposal. This helps protect workers and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Release of hazardous fluids or adhesives during draining/transfer is a servicing hazard, not central to running the carton line, so relevance is moderate; chemical exposure or spills cause serious harm and cleanup, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Manual draining with containment works but drips, cured adhesive, and awkward drain points cause frequent minor releases. Draining and transferring fluids and adhesives is a slow, careful manual operation during disposal. Clean draining of viscous adhesives and process fluids relies on operator technique and knowledge of drain points. Spill containment, absorbents, and proper disposal containers add real material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between approved carton specifications and cartoning-machine setup instructions
rows.plain: Make sure the approved box details match the instructions for setting up the box-packing machine. This avoids confusion and reduces the time needed to switch the machine between jobs.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between approved specs and setup instructions is an upstream configuration-integrity issue; moderately central and severe if it drives wrong setup.
rows.sat_rat: Approved setup sheets usually align with specs but stale or conflicting documents still appear. Resolving spec-versus-instruction conflicts stalls setup. Detecting and reconciling conflicts requires experienced judgement. Conflicts cause rework and wasted materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected machine motion after the operator initiates shutdown
rows.plain: Make sure the machine stops moving as expected after the operator presses the stop control. This helps prevent people or objects from being caught by continuing motion.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected machine motion after shutdown is a safety-adjacent control issue rather than the core cartoning throughput job, so relevance is moderate; however, when it occurs an operator can be struck by moving parts, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Stored energy in springs, gravity, or pneumatics can still cause residual motion after shutdown, though servo-braked modern machines handle this well in most cases. Controlled stop functions bring motion to rest promptly after shutdown initiation without meaningful delay. Stored-energy release is largely engineered in, requiring little operator awareness of residual motion. Brakes, servo controls, and energy-dissipation features are standard cost items on current machines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoning-machine settings configured outside specified tolerances for the next carton format
rows.plain: Fewer machine settings for the next box size should be outside the allowed range. This helps the machine make the new boxes correctly during the changeover.
rows.imp_rat: Machine settings configured outside tolerance for the next format risks defective erection/insertion; moderately central and severe if it damages product.
rows.sat_rat: Setup sheets and digital setpoint recall improve consistency, but manual-adjust machines still drift out of tolerance. Bringing settings within tolerance can take iterative trials. Dialing settings to tolerance is skill-intensive adjustment work. Out-of-tolerance settings produce scrap trial cartons.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that setup verification uses cartons, labels, leaflets, or sterile units from the wrong format
rows.plain: Check the setup using only the cartons, labels, instruction sheets, and sterile items meant for the chosen product or package. This prevents the wrong materials from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Verifying setup with wrong-format materials invalidates the setup check; moderately central and severe because it can approve an incorrect configuration.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance procedures reduce this but wrong-format samples occasionally enter verification. Confirming verification uses right-format samples adds a check step. Depends on disciplined material control rather than expertise. Using wrong-format samples invalidates verification and wastes materials.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of judging machinery as compatible when its carton-format, throughput, or line-interface requirements do not match the sterile fill-finish operation
rows.plain: Avoid deciding that a used or repaired machine will work when its package sizes, production speed, or connections do not fit the sterile product-filling operation. This helps prevent buying equipment that cannot work safely or effectively with the existing process.
rows.imp_rat: Misjudging machinery compatibility with carton-format/throughput/line-interface needs is a selection-phase error affecting whether the job can be done at all, so relevance is meaningful; a mismatch to a sterile fill-finish line causes costly rework and downtime, giving fairly high severity.
rows.sat_rat: URS-driven checklists and FAT protocols catch many mismatches, yet subtle format or interface incompatibilities still slip through into commissioning fairly often. Compatibility checks against carton-format and line-interface specs are routine during vendor selection but iterating on incomplete data can stretch the acquisition timeline. Correctly judging format, throughput, and interface fit for sterile fill-finish demands seasoned packaging engineers who are not always available on the buying team. The cost of a wrong compatibility call is enormous, but pre-purchase due-diligence effort itself is modest relative to machine price.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inspection activity introduces contamination into the sterile fill-finish area
rows.plain: Prevent checking the equipment for wear from bringing dirt or germs into the sterile filling area. This helps keep the products free of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection-introduced contamination into the sterile area is a specialized side activity relative to core cartoning throughput (lower relevance), yet compromised sterility is catastrophic for compliance and patient safety (very high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because inspection is minimally invasive, established gowning and behavior SOPs keep contamination fairly well controlled, with lapses from touch and particle shedding. Gowning and controlled entry for inspection add time but are routine. Inspecting without contaminating requires standard aseptic-behavior training that most sterile-area staff hold. Inspection is low-material-cost, mainly gowning consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected actuator movement caused by residual pneumatic pressure during service
rows.plain: Prevent machine parts from moving unexpectedly because pressurized air remains in the system during servicing. This helps protect the person doing the work.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pneumatic pressure causing actuator movement during service is a maintenance-phase hazard (moderate relevance), but the resulting crush/strike injury is severe (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Where dump valves and bleed procedures exist residual-pressure movement is controlled, but trapped-air release remains a frequently overlooked failure mode. Bleeding pneumatic pressure adds a quick venting step before service. Knowing that accumulators and trapped air can drive actuators after isolation requires specific pneumatic awareness some techs lack. Exhaust/dump valves are cheap and often built into the machine's isolation block.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to escalate an interruption outside routine correction limits
rows.plain: Make sure problems that cannot be fixed through normal steps are reported to the right person instead of being left unresolved.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to escalate an interruption beyond routine limits is a process-control gap moderately relevant to the job, severe if it allows uncontrolled recovery.
rows.sat_rat: SOP escalation triggers exist but depend on operator recognition, so escalation is inconsistently applied. Escalation timing suffers when operators hesitate, delaying proper response. Knowing when an interruption exceeds routine limits requires trained judgement that varies among operators. Failure to escalate can compound into larger losses, an indirect but real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that installed change parts do not match the approved carton format
rows.plain: Make sure the installed replacement parts fit the approved box design. This prevents setup problems and packaging errors.
rows.imp_rat: Change parts not matching approved carton format is a setup error affecting erection quality; moderately relevant and severe because it can yield nonconforming cartons.
rows.sat_rat: Part labeling, kitting and checklists reduce mismatches but human misidentification remains a recurring gap. Verifying installed change parts against the approved format takes measurable setup time. Recognizing correct change parts depends heavily on operator familiarity and labeling discipline. Wrong parts cause scrap and downtime when discovered during trials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous mechanical, pneumatic, or electrical energy during inspection
rows.plain: The technician should avoid contact with dangerous moving parts, pressurized air, or electricity while checking the equipment for wear. This helps prevent serious injury.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous mechanical, pneumatic, or electrical energy during inspection is a maintenance-safety error tangential to core cartoning output, so relevance is moderate; the potential for electrocution or crushing makes severity very high.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-source energy isolation is well-drilled, so exposure during inspection is consistently controlled aside from missed secondary energy sources. Energy isolation before inspection is a brief, standardized step. Recognizing pneumatic, electrical and mechanical energy sources is core trained competence for maintenance staff. Isolation hardware and verification meters are inexpensive and already on hand.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from stored energy, sharp edges, or unexpected assembly movement during wear-part replacement
rows.plain: The technician should be protected from trapped pressure or movement, sharp edges, and parts shifting unexpectedly while replacing worn parts. This reduces the chance of cuts, crushing, or other injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Technician injury from stored energy or sharp edges during wear-part replacement is a maintenance-safety concern peripheral to producing cartons, so relevance is moderate; the consequence is direct human injury, so severity is very high.
rows.sat_rat: Established LOTO plus PPE practice reliably controls stored energy and sharp-edge hazards during part replacement, with occasional lapses on residual spring or gravity energy. De-energizing and blocking assemblies before wear-part swaps is a routine pre-task step with predictable duration. Standard PPE and LOTO training cover this without requiring specialist skill. Guards, gloves and blocking devices are cheap and standard-issue.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of completed packaging records being unavailable for review
rows.plain: Make sure the completed packaging records can be found and reviewed. This provides proof of what happened during the run.
