rows.imp_rat: Unavailable packaging records is a records/compliance gap peripheral to physical cartoning but severe because it can block release or trigger findings.
rows.sat_rat: Established EBR and paper batch-record practices make records reliably retrievable, with occasional gaps from manual entry. Records are usually available promptly when electronic batch systems are in place. Maintaining complete records requires trained documentation discipline from operators. Missing records cause costly batch holds and rework of documentation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to hazardous machine motion during correction
rows.plain: Keep people away from dangerous moving machine parts while fixing an interruption. This helps prevent serious injury during the correction.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to hazardous motion during correction is peripheral to the packaging output but severe because of serious injury potential.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked guards and stop functions reliably limit hazardous-motion exposure during correction. Safe correction procedures add some time but are standard. Following guarded-correction rules is trainable and not expert-dependent. Personnel injuries are costly but infrequent with current controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in parent-child assignment rules for labeled sterile units
rows.plain: Make the rules for linking labeled sterile units to their larger groups clear. This helps people know exactly which items belong together and can be traced together.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in parent-child assignment rules undermines correct aggregation; moderately central and severe because it can corrupt serialization hierarchies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard templates cover common hierarchies well, yet ambiguity persists for partial and mixed configurations, leaving recurring gaps. Clarifying parent-child rules is a one-time specification activity that seldom delays throughput. Interpreting hierarchy rules for atypical configurations still leans on experienced packaging engineers, limiting how much the status quo de-skills this. Ambiguous rules discovered late force respecification and validation cost, a recurring but manageable expense.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of registration deviation from the target under intended production conditions
rows.plain: Keep the equipment’s alignment as close as possible to the intended position during normal production. This helps products and parts line up correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Registration deviation under normal production conditions degrades placement/print accuracy central to correct cartons (high relevance), but usually yields fixable quality drift rather than severe harm (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Registration control systems help, yet deviation under real speed and material variation remains a persistent tuning challenge. Dialing in registration under production conditions takes repeated trial runs and adjustment. Minimizing registration deviation depends on experienced technicians reading marks and tuning servos. Adjustment consumes some scrap material and technician hours but no major spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of incompatibility between serialization criteria and cartoning-machine insertion, carton-erection, and sequencing constraints
rows.plain: Set the rules for assigning unique numbers to products so they work with the machine’s limits for putting products into cartons, making cartons, and ordering them. This prevents the numbering rules from slowing down or disrupting packing.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibility between serialization criteria and machine constraints is a configuration-fit issue; moderately relevant and severe if it forces bad insertion or halts serialization.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard integrated check, so incompatibilities recur and are handled reactively rather than prevented. Reconciling serialization criteria with machine insertion and sequencing constraints often surfaces late, adding rework cycles. It requires simultaneous fluency in serialization schemes and cartoner mechanics, a rare combination the status quo relies on individuals to hold. Late-discovered incompatibilities force re-engineering and re-validation, a notable recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to uncontrolled motion or stored energy during changeover
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a worker is hurt by unexpected movement or energy that has not been safely released during equipment changeover. The equipment should be made safe before work begins.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to uncontrolled motion/stored energy during changeover is peripheral to the packaging job itself but severe due to serious personnel-injury risk.
rows.sat_rat: Guarding, interlocks and LOTO are mature and widely deployed, making exposure to stored energy reliably controlled. Lockout procedures add time to changeover but are routinely applied. Following energy-isolation steps is standardized and trainable rather than expert-dependent. Safety incidents are costly but rare under established procedures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with label placement, condition, or adhesion outside defined tolerances
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons whose labels are in the wrong place, damaged, or not sticking properly. Proper labels help the cartons meet quality requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Label placement, condition, or adhesion outside tolerance is a labeling-quality issue moderately central to release with severe consequences if labels detach or become illegible.
rows.sat_rat: Vision catches placement well but adhesion/condition defects are harder to detect consistently. Adhesion and placement checks are inline where equipped. Detecting adhesion/condition issues still partly depends on operator observation. Poor adhesion causes rejects and field complaints.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the required association between each labeled sterile unit and its carton identity
rows.plain: Make the required link between each labeled sterile unit and its matching carton completely clear. This helps prevent units from being put in the wrong cartons.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in unit-to-carton identity association undermines correct pairing; moderately relevant and severe since it can propagate into mislabeling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard parent-child aggregation practice covers most cases well, but residual ambiguity in edge cases still slips through and remains a recurring source of misassociation. Defining unit-to-carton association rules is largely front-loaded during specification and does not add ongoing delay to the recommendation work. Mature serialization/aggregation frameworks (GS1, EPCIS) give the influencer standardized concepts, reducing the interpretive skill needed to specify these associations. Ambiguity that survives into production drives costly rework and deviation investigations, so the status quo carries real latent cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which carton labeling falls outside approved placement boundaries
rows.plain: Keep labels on the cartons within the approved areas. This helps ensure the labels are easy to find and correctly placed.
rows.imp_rat: Labeling outside approved placement boundaries is a labeling-quality issue moderately central to compliant release with severe consequences if legibility/regulatory breach results.
rows.sat_rat: Vision systems reliably detect placement outside tolerance, though marginal cases persist. Vision placement checks run inline without slowing throughput. Automated placement inspection lowers reliance on visual operator judgement. Out-of-boundary labels cause rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the specified unit quantity exceeds the usable capacity of an erected carton
rows.plain: Make sure the required number of units can fit inside a prepared carton. This prevents overfilling or damage during packing.
rows.imp_rat: Specified quantity exceeding usable carton capacity forces overfill/damage; moderately central to fit correctness and severe if it damages units.
rows.sat_rat: Physical capacity limits are well understood and routinely verified, making overcapacity errors uncommon. Capacity checks are quick geometric comparisons done during specification. Comparing unit count to carton capacity is straightforward and needs little specialist skill. Overpacking errors caught early cost little; if missed they cause scrap, but they are usually caught.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of floor anchors loosening during high-speed cartoning operation
rows.plain: Keep the bolts holding the machine to the floor from coming loose during fast box-packing work. This keeps the machine stable and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Floor anchors loosening during high-speed operation affects sustained running of the machine (moderate relevance), and can cause misalignment, damage, or a safety incident if unchecked (fairly high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Engineered anchoring per vibration specs is well understood, though high-speed dynamic loads occasionally loosen improperly installed anchors. Proper anchor installation and torque checks add modest time during setup. Correct anchor selection and torqueing follows established methods within reach of trained millwrights. Chemical or mechanical anchors are inexpensive relative to consequences of loosening.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cartoning machine misalignment after floor anchoring
rows.plain: Keep the packing machine properly lined up after it is fastened to the floor. This helps it operate correctly and reduces wear or damage.
rows.imp_rat: Machine misalignment after anchoring undermines reliable, accurate cartoning across the run (fairly high relevance), leading to persistent defects and downtime rather than catastrophic harm (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard leveling and laser-alignment practice reliably delivers acceptable alignment, though anchoring can still induce small residual misalignment. Leveling and alignment after anchoring is an iterative step that consumes noticeable setup time. Achieving alignment relies on skilled use of levels and lasers by experienced fitters. Shims and precision instruments are low cost within the overall install.

rows.source_job: Isolate energy sources on the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the machine being re-energized by another party before removal
rows.plain: Prevent someone else from turning the machine back on before it has been removed or made safe. This protects the people working on it.
rows.imp_rat: Re-energization by another party before lockout removal is a specific lockout/tagout gap not central to the cartoning function, keeping relevance modest; when it happens a worker can be killed or maimed, so severity is very high.
rows.sat_rat: Group-lockout and tagging conventions reliably prevent re-energization, though communication lapses across shifts occasionally allow it. Applying tags and locks is quick and does not materially delay the removal. Lockout discipline is basic trained practice requiring little specialized skill. Locks and tags are trivially cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation in closure alignment from specification
rows.plain: Keep the carton closure lined up as closely as required. Proper alignment helps the carton close securely and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Closure alignment deviation affects carton closure quality; relevant to a job sub-step with moderate consequences unless it reaches integrity loss.
rows.sat_rat: Vision and mechanical guides make closure alignment fairly consistent on well-maintained machines, but drift over a run still produces defects. Closure misalignment is usually caught at inspection stations without major time loss. Correct closure setup requires skilled changeover technicians to dial in flap folders and glue. Misaligned closures cause rejected cartons and rework, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of synchronization mismatch among carton erection, sterile-unit insertion, and carton closure during setup
rows.plain: Keep carton forming, placing the sterile product inside, and closing the carton in step with one another during setup. Good timing between these actions helps prevent interruptions and mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Synchronization mismatch among erection/insertion/closure at setup risks misinsertion; moderately central with moderate consequences usually caught before production.
rows.sat_rat: Servo phasing and cam timing give repeatable sync once set, though setup-phase mismatch is common initially. Achieving synchronization across stations can consume significant setup time. Phasing erection, insertion and closure is highly skill-dependent tuning. Sync mismatch during setup wastes trial materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton blanks outside dimensional or material specifications at cartoning
rows.plain: Use only carton pieces that have the right size, shape, and material when putting products into cartons. This helps the cartons protect and hold the products properly.
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-spec carton blanks at cartoning cause erection/closure defects; moderately central to quality with moderate consequences usually caught at erection.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming inspection catches gross defects but subtle dimensional variation still reaches the machine. Incoming material inspection is a receiving-stage step, not inline. Depends on QC sampling skill for dimensional checks. Out-of-spec blanks cause jams and scrap costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the condition interrupting carton flow
rows.plain: Make it less likely that the cause of a stoppage in carton movement will be identified incorrectly. Correctly identifying the problem helps the worker choose the right response.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the interrupting condition is a diagnostic ambiguity moderately relevant to correct recovery, with moderate consequences unless it drives unsafe restart.
rows.sat_rat: Basic fault codes help but many conditions still require judgement, making identification inconsistent. Misidentifying the interrupting condition prolongs diagnosis and downtime. Correctly diagnosing what stopped carton flow depends heavily on operator experience. Misdiagnosis wastes labor and can scrap material during wrong corrections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation in erected-carton geometry from the current carton specification
rows.plain: Keep the shape of opened cartons as close as possible to the approved carton design. This matters because misshapen cartons may not handle, stack, or fit correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Erected-carton geometry deviation affects carton quality; relevant to the erection sub-job with moderate consequences unless it drives closure or insertion failure.
rows.sat_rat: Adjustable guides and forming plates produce consistent geometry once set, but drift and blank variation cause deviations. Achieving square geometry can require multiple timing adjustments. Getting erected-carton geometry to spec is a core skilled adjustment task. Out-of-spec geometry scraps cartons during trials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty that observed cartoning results are repeatable across the intended operating envelope
rows.plain: Make sure the packaging results would stay consistent under all the normal operating conditions the equipment is expected to handle. This gives buyers confidence that the results are not a one-time success.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that results are repeatable across the operating envelope is a qualification-confidence concern; moderately relevant and moderately severe for validation.
rows.sat_rat: Standard DOE and repeatability protocols exist but envelope edges are routinely under-sampled, leaving genuine uncertainty about repeatability. Establishing repeatability across an operating envelope requires many runs that stretch qualification timelines, and today this is a slow, iterative effort. Judging whether observed results generalize across the envelope demands seasoned validation engineers who can design representative run matrices. Extensive repeat runs consume machine time, materials, and sterile units, making envelope coverage expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of synchronization loss between carton transport and sterile-unit insertion
rows.plain: Keep carton movement and the insertion of germ-free units properly timed together. This helps the line run smoothly without missed or mistimed insertions.
rows.imp_rat: Synchronization loss between transport and insertion risks misfeed/misinsertion; moderately central to reliable insertion with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Encoder-based synchronization is reliable in steady state but occasional loss persists. Sync loss during running forces stoppages, costing time. Restoring transport-insertion sync requires trained intervention. Lost sync jams and scraps materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required change parts do not fit the intended carton format
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the parts needed for a changeover do not fit the intended box size or style. This helps avoid delays when switching production.
rows.imp_rat: Change parts not fitting the intended format is largely a setup fit issue; moderately relevant and typically caught before running, with moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated change-part kits per format usually ensure fit, though wear and mixed kits cause intermittent problems. Discovering a part does not fit mid-setup causes disruptive delays. Judging fit requires experienced operators who know the machine and format tolerances. Ill-fitting parts risk damage to parts or machine and wasted setup effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from unexpected movement of cartoning machinery during lubrication
rows.plain: Prevent the packaging machine from moving unexpectedly while a technician applies lubricant. This helps protect the technician from being caught or struck.
rows.imp_rat: Injury from unexpected movement during lubrication concerns maintenance rather than the production job itself (low relevance), but the bodily-harm outcome is severe when it happens (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because lubrication is typically done de-energized, unexpected-movement injury is fairly well controlled, with lapses only when techs grease running equipment for convenience. Lubrication is usually a shut-down task, so isolating first costs little extra time. Basic lockout before greasing points is a low-skill, well-understood routine. Isolation for lube tasks requires no special expenditure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of test-batch cartons formed with dimensional or structural defects
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons in the test batch that are misshapen or not strong enough. Properly formed cartons help protect the product and move through the line smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Test-batch cartons with dimensional or structural defects is relevant to validating forming quality, but severity is moderate since defects are detected during the test batch before production release.
rows.sat_rat: Forming sections are well engineered so most test cartons form correctly, with occasional dimensional defects during initial tuning. Forming trial cartons and inspecting dimensions adds bounded setup time. Correct forming setup relies on technician adjustment of forming tooling and knowledge of blank behavior. Rejected malformed cartons are a small material cost during preparation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of test-batch cartons sealed incompletely or out of alignment
rows.plain: Reduce the number of test cartons that are not fully sealed or whose seals are out of place. Proper sealing helps keep the product inside and protected.
rows.imp_rat: Test-batch cartons sealed incompletely or misaligned is relevant to seal quality validation, but severity is moderate as defects are caught in the test batch rather than shipped.
rows.sat_rat: Standard test-batch sealing checks catch most defects, but incomplete or misaligned seals still appear during initial setup. Running and inspecting test-batch seals adds setup time proportional to the number of trial cartons. Achieving complete, aligned seals depends on technician adjustment skill with glue or heat and compression. Scrapped test cartons and adhesive are minor consumable costs.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of verification cartons whose closures remain outside specification after closing-mechanism adjustment
rows.plain: Make sure the cartons used for checking have closures that meet the required standard after the closing parts are adjusted. This shows that the machine is closing cartons properly.
rows.imp_rat: Verification carton closures out of specification after adjustment is relevant to closure quality, but severity is moderate since these are caught during verification before full production.
rows.sat_rat: Established closing-adjustment routines usually bring closures into spec, though out-of-spec verification cartons still occur before settling in. Adjusting closing mechanisms and re-checking closures involves iterative trial runs. Correct closure setup requires a technician's feel for the closing mechanism and carton stock. Verification cartons and glue consumed during adjustment are modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected movement of drive components during adjustment
rows.plain: Prevent drive parts from moving unexpectedly while they are being adjusted. This helps protect the worker's hands and body from injury.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected drive-component movement during adjustment is a setup/maintenance hazard rather than core throughput (moderate relevance), with a serious injury potential when it occurs (fairly high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard isolation and mechanical restraint control most drive movement during adjustment, though back-driving and stored torque still cause occasional surprises. Locking or blocking drive components before adjustment is a short added step. Preventing drift of servo or spring-loaded drives during adjustment needs mechanical understanding that competent techs possess. Mechanical blocks and brake engagement cost little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of personnel injuries during disconnection, handling, loading, or unloading of the cartoning machine
rows.plain: Prevent people from being injured while disconnecting, moving, loading, or unloading the cartoning machine. This protects workers while the machine is being given away.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injuries during disconnection/handling/loading are logistics-adjacent to the core cartoning function (low relevance), yet any such injury is a severe, irreversible consequence (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Documented handling procedures keep injury counts low, though the multi-step cycle leaves recurring pinch and strain incidents. Safe handling across disconnect-through-unload adds planned time but no chronic delay. Established safe-handling training covers the full disconnect-to-unload cycle competently. PPE, rigging, and crew costs are routine and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury during disconnection, handling, or loading of the machine for return
rows.plain: Prevent people from being hurt while disconnecting, moving, or loading the machine for return. This helps ensure the machine can be shipped back safely.
rows.imp_rat: Injury during disconnection/handling for return is peripheral to the CFJ of cartoning products (relevance low, a return event is rare and outside normal production), but a personnel injury carries severe, potentially irreversible human-harm consequences (severity high).
rows.sat_rat: Mature material-handling practice makes handling injuries uncommon where lift plans are followed. Rigging and loading for return follow planned lift sequences that take predictable time. Safe heavy-machine handling depends on trained riggers and lift planners who are generally available. Cranes, forklifts, and rigging crews are significant but standard budgeted costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required cartoning-machine settings with missing or conflicting values for the next carton format
rows.plain: Reduce the number of machine settings needed for the next box size that are missing or do not agree with one another. This helps the machine be changed over correctly and efficiently.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or conflicting setting values is a setup-data completeness gap moderately relevant to correct configuration, with moderate consequences typically caught at setup.
rows.sat_rat: Complete parameter records reduce gaps but conflicting or missing values still surface on older formats. Chasing missing setting values delays completion of setup. Filling gaps in setting values relies on operator experience. Guessed values cause scrap and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of variation in erected-carton position at transfer to sterile-unit insertion
rows.plain: Keep each opened carton in the same position when it is handed over for putting in the clean product. This helps the product enter the carton correctly and smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in erected-carton position at transfer risks misinsertion; moderately relevant to reliable insertion with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Guides and servo transfers hold position well but variation from blank quality remains. Reducing transfer-position variation takes iterative tuning time. Controlling erected-carton position at transfer is skilled adjustment. Position variation causes mis-inserts and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of synchronization failures between carton movement and sterile-unit arrival during startup
rows.plain: Prevent the cartons and the clean product units from getting out of step when the process starts. This helps each product unit arrive at the carton at the right time.
rows.imp_rat: Synchronization failures at startup risk misinsertion or jams; moderately relevant to reliable operation with moderate, largely recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Modern electronic camming reduces startup sync failures but they still recur during ramp-up. Startup sync failures extend the time to stable running. Diagnosing timing between carton and unit arrival needs skill. Failed sync at startup produces scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of incompatibility between the specified carton hierarchy and required downstream case or pallet relationships
rows.plain: Make sure the planned levels of boxes, larger cases, and pallets fit together with the relationships needed later in the process. This helps products be grouped and handled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibility of carton hierarchy with downstream case/pallet relationships is a design/aggregation concern peripheral to insertion, with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregation standards cover common case/pallet structures well, but bespoke downstream relationships still produce recurring conflicts. Aligning carton hierarchy with case/pallet relationships is a specification-time task with little runtime delay. End-to-end aggregation design requires supply-chain and serialization knowledge that remains specialist work. Downstream mismatches trigger repackaging or rejected shipments, a significant occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury during hands-on cartoning-machine training
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a worker will be hurt while learning to use the machine that puts products into boxes. Safe training helps operators learn the machine without unnecessary risk.
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury during hands-on training is a peripheral onboarding-safety issue rather than a core cartoning function, so relevance is modest; a training injury still means real harm and liability, keeping severity fairly high.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout practices and supervision reduce incidents, but hands-on exposure to live mechanisms keeps residual injury risk present. Safe hands-on training takes longer than simply running the machine, and time pressure erodes precautions. Injury avoidance depends heavily on the trainer's vigilance and the trainee's inexperience, a skill-dependent and error-prone situation. Dedicated supervised training time has a labor cost but requires little extra equipment.

rows.source_job: Remove machine anchors and foundation fixtures
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician injury from sudden fastener release or fragmented material during cartoning-machine fixture removal
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a technician will be hurt when a fastener suddenly comes loose or broken pieces fly during fixture removal. Careful removal helps protect the technician from unexpected impacts.
rows.imp_rat: Injury from sudden fastener release or fragmented material during fixture removal is a narrow maintenance hazard largely separate from cartoning output, so relevance is modest; flying debris can cause eye or impact injury, giving fairly high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Correct tooling and PPE prevent most injuries, but seized or spring-loaded fasteners still occasionally fragment or fly. Fixture removal is done deliberately, adding modest time to protect against fastener release. Knowing which fasteners hold tension and how to release them safely is experience-dependent finesse. PPE and proper tools are low-cost and generally available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging concealed utilities while penetrating the plant floor
rows.plain: Secure the machine to the floor without hitting hidden pipes, wires, or other services underneath. Damaging them could cause leaks, outages, or other hazards.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging concealed utilities while penetrating the plant floor is an installation-phase construction issue almost unrelated to the cartoning CFJ, so relevance is low; striking a live line or gas main can be catastrophic, keeping severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Inaccurate as-built records and imperfect scanning mean concealed-utility strikes remain a real, recurring risk during anchoring. Locating and marking concealed utilities before floor penetration adds delay that is sometimes skipped. Reliable detection requires scanning expertise and interpretation of often-incomplete facility drawings. Utility scanning and potholing cost money, and a strike causes expensive damage and downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of worsening a partial obstruction when advancing the affected mechanism
rows.plain: Avoid making a partial blockage worse while moving the affected machine part. This helps prevent further damage or a more serious stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Worsening a partial obstruction by forcing the mechanism is a diagnostic-handling error rather than a central job function (relevance), but it can cause mechanical damage and extended repair (severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is little machine safeguard against overdriving into an obstruction, so outcomes vary widely with operator care. Cautious manual advancing to clear a partial obstruction is slow and iterative. Avoiding worsening a jam while advancing depends almost entirely on the technician's feel and tactile judgement, which is scarce and inconsistent. A worsened obstruction can bend tooling or tear cartons, adding material and repair cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the production stoppage caused by difficulty accessing or removing jammed cartons or products
rows.plain: Get production running again quickly when cartons or products become stuck, so the stoppage causes less delay.
rows.imp_rat: Difficulty accessing jammed cartons prolongs stoppage, relevant to keeping the cartoning job running (moderate relevance), but the impact is lost time rather than lasting harm (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Machine designs vary widely in accessibility and many older cartoners have poor access, so this remains a chronic, inconsistent pain. Reaching deep-seated jammed cartons often means opening guards, removing tooling and probing by hand, which extends stoppages well beyond the jam itself. Access to buried jams relies heavily on operator familiarity with the machine's internal geometry, making it error-prone for less-trained staff. Extended access time and possible product loss during retrieval add real cost per incident.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the root cause of a mechanical, electrical, pneumatic, or control fault
rows.plain: Correctly identify what caused a machine problem, whether it involves moving parts, wiring, air pressure, or controls. Finding the real cause helps workers make the right repair.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the root cause of a fault prolongs downtime and risks incorrect fixes, giving moderate relevance to sustained operation, but it is a diagnostic/maintenance error with recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic outcomes vary widely with the individual technician, and intermittent or interacting faults are frequently misattributed. Root-cause diagnosis across mechanical, electrical, pneumatic, and control domains can consume large, unpredictable troubleshooting time. Correctly pinpointing a cross-domain fault demands scarce, experienced multi-disciplinary technicians whose judgement is hard to replace or standardize. Misdiagnosis wastes parts, labor, and production time, making this an expensive failure mode.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of line blockage caused by a carton jam during rejection
rows.plain: Clear a jammed carton as quickly as possible when defective cartons are being removed. This keeps the production line from being stopped for long.
rows.imp_rat: Carton jam during rejection blocking the line is a recurring interruption to the cartoning job (moderate relevance); the blockage duration causes throughput loss but is recoverable (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Jams recur unpredictably at the rejection point and current guarding/sensor practice rarely prevents them, so blockage is a frequent nuisance. Carton jams at the rejection station still require an operator to stop the line, locate the jam and manually clear it, which routinely eats several minutes of downtime per event. Clearing a reject-zone jam demands an experienced operator who knows the machine's chokepoints, so newer staff take noticeably longer. The lost throughput and occasional scrapped cartons during clearing carry a modest but recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the actual source of a cartoning machine jam or fault is incorrectly identified
rows.plain: Correctly identify what is really causing a jam or other problem in the machine that puts products into boxes. This helps workers fix the right part instead of guessing.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the true jam/fault source leads to wrong repairs and prolonged downtime, which matters to keeping cartons running (relevance), and wrong fixes can waste time and recur (severity); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Fault codes flag where a sensor tripped, not why, so the actual source is frequently misidentified on complex jams. Locating the true fault source can consume significant diagnostic time when the machine points to a symptom not a cause. Correct root-cause identification still leans heavily on experienced technicians reading between coarse fault codes, a scarce and error-prone judgement. Misdiagnosis wastes parts and labor chasing the wrong cause.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of shutdown interruption caused by cartons jamming during clearing
rows.plain: Shorten the time the machine must be stopped when cartons get stuck and the blockage is being removed. This helps production resume sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons jamming during clearing extends shutdown, moderately central to sustained operation (relevance), with consequences limited to added downtime (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: The recurrence of jams during the clearing process itself is common and poorly mitigated by current tooling. Secondary jams occurring while clearing the first jam compound the shutdown and prolong the interruption unpredictably. Avoiding cascade jams during clearing takes practiced technique that only seasoned operators reliably apply. Repeated re-jamming during clearing wastes labor time and can damage additional cartons.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeated stoppages caused by jammed material remaining after the cartoning machine is reset
rows.plain: Prevent cartons or other material left behind after a reset from causing the machine to stop again. This reduces repeated interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Leftover jammed material causing repeated stoppages after reset directly harms continuous cartoning throughput (relevance) and repeated stoppages compound downtime (severity); constrained to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Machines readily reset with residual material still present, so recurring stoppages are a common, poorly guarded pain. Repeated stoppages from leftover debris multiply downtime as the machine trips again after each reset. Thorough clearing before reset relies on operator diligence to spot every fragment. Repeat trips and wasted cartons add cumulative cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an update interruption leaves the machine controls unable to start
rows.plain: Make it less likely that an interruption while updating the machine's control software will leave the machine unable to start. The machine should still be ready to run after the update.
rows.imp_rat: An update interruption leaving controls unable to start is a rare event outside normal cartoning (lower relevance), but it can render the machine inoperable and cause major downtime (higher severity); capped by ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Few sites have rollback or UPS-protected update workflows, so interruptions can readily leave controls unbootable. A bricked controller after a botched update causes extended downtime awaiting reflash or vendor support. Recovering an interrupted update needs specialist recovery procedures beyond routine maintenance skill. Emergency vendor intervention and lost production make interrupted updates expensive when they occur.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of developing machine faults going unrecognized before failure
rows.plain: Make it less likely that a machine problem will go unnoticed until the machine breaks down. Finding problems early can help prevent a failure.
rows.imp_rat: Developing faults going unrecognized before failure moderately affects sustained cartoning reliability (relevance), and an unexpected failure can halt production (severity), but it is a predictive-maintenance concern kept within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Most maintenance is still reactive or calendar-based, so developing faults commonly go unrecognized until failure. Undetected developing faults give no advance warning, so failures strike without lead time to plan. Recognizing incipient failure signatures demands experienced operators or condition-monitoring expertise that is often absent. Missed early warnings turn into costly unplanned breakdowns and collateral damage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of diagnoses that identify the wrong component or setting as the fault source
rows.plain: Make it less likely that a diagnosis blames the wrong part or setting for a machine problem. Correctly finding the source helps repairs happen faster and prevents unnecessary changes.
rows.imp_rat: Diagnoses that fault the wrong component waste repair effort and extend stoppages, relevant to uptime, but the consequence is corrected on the next diagnostic pass rather than causing lasting harm.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong diagnostic aids, a meaningful share of diagnoses still name the wrong part or setting, especially on complex control faults. Chasing the wrong component adds rework loops and extends downtime. Distinguishing the true faulty component from a symptomatic one relies on deep experience and structured troubleshooting that many technicians lack. Wrong-component diagnoses cause needless part replacement and swapped-in-vain labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel being deployed to the wrong product run or packaging configuration
rows.plain: Assign people to the correct product run and packaging setup. This helps prevent work from being done on the wrong product or with the wrong equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Deploying personnel to the wrong run is an administrative staffing error peripheral to the machine's insertion job, with moderate and recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Shift briefings and qualification matrices make wrong-run deployment fairly controllable though manual. Assigning personnel to the right run is a scheduling task done ahead of shift. Correct deployment depends on the overseer's knowledge of qualifications and current schedule. Misdeployment wastes labor and can delay runs but rarely destroys product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering incompatibilities among labeled-unit geometry, carton geometry, and cartoning-machine limits after configuration recommendation
rows.plain: Avoid recommending a setup before checking that the product units, boxes, and packing machine fit together and within the machine’s limits. This helps prevent unexpected changes and delays when switching products.
rows.imp_rat: Discovering geometry/limit incompatibilities after recommendation is a design-rework timing issue peripheral to running production, with moderate consequences.
rows.sat_rat: There is no reliable predictive check in the status quo, so incompatibilities are commonly found late rather than prevented. Discovering geometry/machine-limit incompatibilities after recommendation forces time-consuming rework loops. Foreseeing these clashes requires deep combined knowledge of unit geometry, carton design, and machine envelope that few hold. Late discovery wastes engineering effort and can require tooling changes, driving real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of test-batch cartons loaded with incorrect contents, count, or orientation
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect contents, count, or orientation in test-batch cartons matters to output quality, but because these are test batches the defective units are caught before commercial release, limiting both centrality and consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Loading verification catches errors, but incorrect contents, count, or orientation remain a frequent test-batch issue before settings converge. Verifying content, count, and orientation across a test batch adds inspection time. Correct loading setup depends on aligning product handling and feed systems, requiring careful technician judgement. Mis-loaded test cartons waste product and packaging in modest amounts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required machine control functions inaccessible to authorized personnel
rows.imp_rat: Required control functions being inaccessible to authorized personnel can block adjustments or safe operation of the cartoner, which touches the job directly; still, it is an access-provisioning issue usually resolved quickly, so relevance and severity remain moderate under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based HMI access is a mature feature that reliably keeps required functions available to authorized personnel, with rare misconfiguration. Verifying authorized access to control functions is a quick confirmation step during preparation. Configuring access levels follows the HMI's standard role scheme, requiring modest skill. Access configuration is a software task with negligible cost.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a calibrated detection sensor classifies a known reference incorrectly
rows.imp_rat: A calibrated detection sensor misclassifying a known reference undermines reject reliability, but it surfaces during calibration verification where it can be corrected, capping both proxies within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration procedures make sensors mostly dependable, yet misclassification of known references still occurs with drift or poor setup. Calibrating detection sensors against reference samples takes a defined but non-trivial setup step. Proper sensor calibration and reference handling require technician familiarity with the detection system. Reference standards and calibration effort are low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of loading stoppages caused by product or carton jams during insertion
rows.imp_rat: Loading stoppages from jams during insertion are highly relevant to the throughput core job, but each jam is a minor, quickly-cleared interruption with low per-event severity. Kept within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Servo-driven loaders and product control have improved insertion reliability, yet product or carton variation still causes recurrent loading jams. Insertion jams stop loading and demand clearing time before the run resumes. Reducing loading jams relies on operator adjustment of loaders and product staging. Stoppages lose output and can crush product, a recurring but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton stock failing to feed or erect correctly during the run
rows.imp_rat: Carton stock failing to feed or erect during the run is a core failure of the cartoning function (relevance) with direct output loss (severity), but ranked below batch 1 so held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vacuum pick and erecting mechanisms are mature but still misfeed with stock or humidity variation, leaving steady gaps. Feed or erecting failures interrupt the run and cost recovery time each occurrence. Consistent feeding depends on correct magazine and vacuum setup that experienced operators manage adequately. Failed feeds waste carton blanks and reduce throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons closing incorrectly because of variation in stock thickness, stiffness, or crease quality
rows.imp_rat: Cartons closing incorrectly from stock variation is directly relevant to producing a sealed carton, but individual mis-closures are typically detected and rejected with modest consequence, keeping severity low.
rows.sat_rat: Machines are tuned for a nominal stock spec, so lot-to-lot board variation consistently defeats fixed settings and causes recurring closure faults. Closing errors from stock variation surface within the cycle but recur unpredictably across a run. Compensating for varying stock thickness, stiffness, and crease quality demands considerable setter expertise. Incorrect closures from stock variability generate ongoing scrap that is hard to eliminate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons or product units trapped or damaged during the pause
rows.imp_rat: Cartons/product trapped or damaged during a pause directly wastes product and affects cartoning yield (moderate relevance and severity), though the loss is bounded and recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Modern servo-driven cartoners with controlled decel reduce trapped/damaged units, though a residual number are still caught mid-transfer. A controlled pause is reasonably quick but any trapped units must still be inspected and removed, adding some time. Judging which paused cartons are salvageable versus damaged requires operator judgement. Cartons and product crushed or trapped during a pause are direct scrap, a tangible material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of material accumulated because linked production stages continue feeding the paused cartoning stage
rows.imp_rat: Material piling up from unpaused upstream stages when cartoning halts causes waste and cleanup but is a line-coordination issue peripheral to the cartoning core job (relevance); consequences are contained spillage and rework (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cascade-stop and accumulation-conveyor practice covers most cases but overflow still occurs when timing is imperfect. Upstream stops trigger reasonably fast but not instantly across linked stages. Line integration handles backpressure automatically once configured, needing modest setup skill. Accumulated material from lagging upstream stops is a recurring waste, only partly mitigated by accumulation tables and cascade stops.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton-blank starvation at the erecting mechanism during continuous operation
rows.imp_rat: Blank starvation at the erecter during continuous operation stops carton production (relevance) but is typically a recoverable feed gap with limited lasting harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Low-level detection and extended magazines make continuous feeding fairly dependable with occasional lapses during busy periods. Low-magazine sensors give warning so refills happen before starvation stops the run. Refilling the magazine is a routine low-skill task once alerted. Starvation stops cost throughput but consume little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of multiple carton blanks advancing into the erecting mechanism in a single feed cycle
rows.imp_rat: Double-feeding blanks into the erecter is central to correct carton formation (relevance) and causes jams and scrap when it occurs (severity); constrained to 4.5 average.
rows.sat_rat: Thickness and double-sheet detectors are standard and fairly effective, though static and warped stock still cause recurring double feeds. Double-feed detection stops the machine quickly, limiting lost time per event. Double-blank correction is straightforward once detected, needing little skill. Double feeds waste blanks and can jam, but detection limits the damage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of damaged or warped carton blanks jamming between the magazine and erecting mechanism
rows.imp_rat: Warped blanks jamming between magazine and erecter interrupts feeding, relevant to throughput (relevance), though usually a clearable stoppage rather than severe damage (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Machines have little defense against warped incoming stock, so blank-quality variation reliably produces jams. Warped-blank jams between magazine and erector stop the line and take time to clear. Screening and loading good stock relies on operator attentiveness rather than machine safeguards. Damaged blanks waste material and cause downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons outside approved quality specifications after a parameter adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Cartons outside quality specs after a parameter adjustment matter to the output-quality aspect of the cartoning job (relevance), but such rejects are typically caught and scrapped rather than causing irreversible harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Without inline quality feedback, some out-of-spec cartons routinely pass before the adjustment settles. Out-of-spec cartons after an adjustment require inspection and rework time. Judging when output returns to spec relies on operator inspection skill. Off-spec cartons are direct material and product waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a parameter adjustment causes a carton jam during the production run
rows.imp_rat: A parameter adjustment causing a carton jam during a run directly interrupts the cartoning job (moderate relevance), but jams are typically recoverable stoppages rather than catastrophic events (bounded severity), so it stays under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Guarded parameter ranges and recipe limits reduce risk but live adjustments still occasionally induce jams. A jam from a mid-run parameter change halts production and costs recovery time. Making safe adjustments during a run relies on operator judgement about margins, an error-prone demand. Resulting jams waste cartons and product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting an incorrect parameter value when approved setting limits are unclear or conflicting
rows.imp_rat: Selecting incorrect parameter values from unclear setting limits affects cartoning quality (moderate relevance) and can produce defective output requiring rework (moderate severity), but is bounded by review steps.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management on newer HMIs helps, but conflicting or undocumented approved limits still cause wrong-value selection fairly often. Hunting for the correct approved setting when limits are unclear wastes changeover time. Selecting the right parameter under conflicting guidance leans heavily on operator expertise and memory. A wrong setting can produce scrap runs, but the error itself is mainly a correctness issue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton jams caused by guide or mandrel misalignment during restart
rows.imp_rat: Guide or mandrel misalignment jamming cartons at restart is central to reliable erecting (relevance) and produces immediate jams and damaged blanks (severity); kept at the 4.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Tool-less changeover and setpoint memory help on newer machines but alignment drift at restart still causes recurring jams. Realigning guides or mandrels at restart adds setup time before a stable run resumes. Correct guide and mandrel alignment demands trained setup judgement that is error-prone under pressure. Misalignment jams waste cartons and cause downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety interlocks that remain unsatisfied when readiness is checked
rows.imp_rat: Unsatisfied safety interlocks at readiness check delay start-up and touch safety, giving moderate relevance, but they are caught at readiness verification before harm occurs, limiting severity.
rows.sat_rat: Safety-rated interlock circuits with positive-guided contacts are highly consistent and rarely fail to detect an open guard, making this a best-served need. Interlock readiness checks are near-instant on modern PLC-driven cartoners so time loss is minimal. Standard HMI fault screens flag unsatisfied interlocks by name, requiring little operator judgement to interpret. Interlock hardware and safety-rated controls are a mature, inexpensive baseline included on virtually all machines.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of machine mechanisms colliding during incremental advancement for timing verification
rows.imp_rat: Mechanisms colliding during incremental timing verification is an infrequent setup-mode event (relevance) but can cause serious mechanical damage when it happens (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Soft-limit and interference-detection features on modern controls reduce but do not eliminate collision risk during manual jogging. Careful jogging for timing verification is slow but rarely the main time sink. Avoiding collisions during incremental advance depends on the technician knowing clearance limits, a moderate skill demand. A collision during jog can damage costly tooling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of upstream product continuing to enter the cartoning machine after shutdown begins
rows.imp_rat: Upstream product continuing to enter after shutdown causes accumulation and rework, moderately relevant to orderly operation, but consequences are recoverable spillage rather than catastrophic, so severity is modest.
rows.sat_rat: Momentum and buffer content mean product still enters after a stop begins unless line-wide sequencing is well tuned, leaving recurring gaps. Shutdown signals propagate quickly but residual product already in the infeed still passes through before stopping. Coordinated stops rely on integrated line control that operators generally trust without deep expertise. Photo-eye and encoder-based stop logic is standard and low-cost on integrated lines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of intended product-carton combinations the candidate machinery cannot handle within specification
rows.imp_rat: Product-carton combinations the machine cannot handle within spec limits the machine's fitness (relevance), yet this is a selection/capability gap with contained consequences (severity), so it stays capped.
rows.sat_rat: FAT and sample runs cover the main combinations reliably, yet edge-case carton/product pairings are sometimes untested until production. Testing every product-carton combination within spec extends evaluation and sample-prep time. Judging whether each combination stays within spec needs packaging technologists familiar with the carton range. Uncovered combinations mean later tooling or change parts costs, but sampling representative combinations limits this.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory product-protection, traceability, reject-control, and cleanability requirements the candidate machinery does not satisfy
rows.imp_rat: Unsatisfied product-protection and cleanability requirements in candidate machinery matter to fitness-for-purpose, but this is a selection-stage gap rather than a runtime cartoning error, limiting relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Standard URS and traceability matrices systematically capture most mandatory requirements, so gaps are the exception rather than the norm. Requirement-by-requirement verification of product-protection, traceability, reject and cleanability features is time-consuming during evaluation. Mapping mandatory requirements to machine features needs cross-functional GMP and validation knowledge. Missing a mandatory feature forces retrofits at high cost, but structured URS lowers that risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of adjusted drive components remaining outside specified tension or alignment tolerances
rows.imp_rat: Drive-component tension/alignment out of tolerance affects mechanical reliability of cartoning (moderate relevance), but consequences are usually gradual wear or minor misfeeds rather than catastrophic (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Tension gauges, laser alignment tools, and specs make in-tolerance drive setup achievable, though by-feel adjustment still leaves some components out of spec. Tensioning and aligning drive components with proper tools is a routine, time-bounded task. Correct belt/chain tension and alignment depends on technician technique, though tension gauges reduce the guesswork. Misalignment causes premature wear and downtime, but correct setup costs little.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material being introduced into the product or packaging path during repair
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material entering the product/packaging path during repair directly threatens product safety, so severity is meaningful; but repair-time contamination is an occasional maintenance-context event rather than central to the routine cartoning job, capping relevance. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Tool-accountability and post-repair inspection catch much foreign material, but intrusive repairs still occasionally leave debris in the product or packaging path. Foreign-material checks, tool accountability and post-repair verification add meaningful time to repairs. Preventing loose fasteners, swarf or wipes from entering the product path relies on disciplined, careful technicians. Tool-control kits and FOD checks are cheap relative to a contaminated-batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machine unavailability from fault detection through restoration to specified operating condition
rows.imp_rat: Total downtime from detection to restoration directly determines cartoning availability (relevance) and lost production accumulates (severity), but it ranks below batch 1 and is capped at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Spares availability, CMMS dispatch, and diagnostic tools compress downtime somewhat, but unpredictable fault complexity keeps restoration times inconsistent. Total downtime from detection to restoration is the core loss here and remains highly variable and often long. Restoration speed hinges on having skilled technicians available promptly, which is frequently the bottleneck. Every hour of unavailability is lost production, making this a large cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of repaired faults recurring after the cartoning machine returns to production
rows.imp_rat: Recurring repaired faults directly undermine cartoning uptime (relevance), and repeated failures on the same defect erode confidence and cause repeated downtime (severity); capped at 4.5 average per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Fixes that address symptoms rather than root cause let a notable fraction of faults recur after return to production. Recurring faults force repeat interventions that eat cumulative downtime. Preventing recurrence requires the technician to fix the underlying cause, not just the symptom, which demands strong diagnostic skill. Repeat failures multiply parts, labor, and lost-production cost.

rows.source_job: Isolate energy sources on the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unintended shutdowns to adjacent sterile fill-finish systems caused by machine isolation
rows.imp_rat: Unintended shutdown of adjacent sterile fill-finish systems is a spillover effect peripheral to cartoning itself (low relevance), but disrupting sterile processes carries high consequence (higher severity); average kept at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery procedures exist but are ad hoc for the disposal context, so downtime duration is inconsistent and often longer than planned. When isolation does trip an adjacent sterile system, restart and requalification of that system is slow and the status quo offers few shortcuts. Rapid diagnosis of what tripped and how to safely restore demands scarce senior operators who may not be on hand during a disposal job. Extended sterile-system downtime carries steep batch-loss and idle-capacity costs.

rows.source_job: Drain residual fluids from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the maintenance technician being exposed to retained operating fluids during drainage
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to retained fluids during drainage is a maintenance-safety concern peripheral to the core cartoning function (low relevance), though exposure can cause real personal harm (moderate-to-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: PPE and drain-point procedures reduce exposure but splashes and drips during manual drainage still occur regularly. Careful drainage to protect the technician adds time to the fluid-removal step. Avoiding exposure to retained operating fluids depends on the technician's technique and PPE use. PPE and containment are low-to-moderate, routine costs.

rows.source_job: Disconnect the machine from facility utilities
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that facility-side utility terminations remain energized, pressurized, leaking, or exposed after cartoning machine disconnection
rows.imp_rat: Energized or pressurized facility utility terminations after disconnection pose real personnel-safety hazards (high severity), but this is a decommissioning-context error remote from the routine cartoning job.
rows.sat_rat: Facility terminations are usually secured, but left-energized or leaking stubs still occur when handoff is unclear. Making facility-side terminations safe is a defined, moderately quick step at disconnection. Capping and de-energizing facility utility stubs is standard facilities-trade work. Caps, blanks, and lockouts are inexpensive.

rows.source_job: Disconnect the machine from facility utilities
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that utility contents or disconnection debris contaminate the sterile fill-finish area during cartoning machine disconnection
rows.imp_rat: Utility contents or debris contaminating a sterile fill-finish area during disconnection is severe when it happens, but disconnection is a rare teardown event far from the routine cartoning job, so relevance is low.
rows.sat_rat: Utility line contents and disconnection debris still occasionally contaminate the sterile area despite containment efforts. Capturing utility contents and debris during disconnection in a sterile suite requires slow, careful work. Clean disconnection without contaminating the sterile area needs cleanroom-aware technique. Caps, containment, and cleanup materials add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying lubricant incompatible with a chain, cam, bearing, or sterile manufacturing requirement
rows.imp_rat: Applying incompatible lubricant has moderate relevance to machine health and sterile requirements, and consequences can include contamination or component wear (severity), but it is bounded under the ceiling by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Food-grade lube specs, labeled dispensers, and lubrication schedules make incompatible-lubricant errors fairly rare in disciplined shops, though mix-ups still occur. Selecting the specified lubricant is a quick step when lubrication charts are available. Correct lubricant choice is largely proceduralized via lube charts, lowering the skill demand. Wrong lubricant can ruin components or product, but correct-lubricant cost is minor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that backed-up control data are corrupted or unreadable when recovery is needed
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted or unreadable control-data backups are peripheral to the routine cartoning job (lower relevance), but when recovery is needed the inability to restore is quite damaging (moderate severity); held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are commonly written and forgotten without periodic test-restores, so corruption is frequently discovered only when recovery is actually needed. Verifying backup integrity adds little time when done, but restore-time discovery of corruption forces long unplanned downtime. Detecting silent corruption requires disciplined checksum or test-restore practice that most maintenance crews lack. Extra storage and verification runs are cheap relative to the cost of a failed recovery, so cost is rarely the blocker.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a backup for the wrong machine or controller during recovery
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a backup for the wrong machine or controller during recovery can push incorrect settings onto a live cartoner, causing misfeeds or downtime; however it only bites during the relatively rare recovery event, keeping relevance to routine cartoning modest even though severity when it occurs is meaningful.
rows.sat_rat: Naming conventions help but multi-machine fleets with similar controllers make mis-selection a real recurring risk. Restoring the wrong machine's backup wastes the whole recovery window and forces a redo. Correct selection depends on the technician recognizing machine/controller identifiers rather than an enforced match. A misapplied backup can damage settings and cause scrap, though direct cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the current approved backup is unavailable when recovery is needed
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable approved backup at recovery time is an infrequent, off-line-cycle concern (lower relevance), yet its occurrence severely hampers restoration (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Approved-version control exists in better-run plants but ad hoc file naming and offline media mean the right backup is often missing when needed. Locating the current approved backup can waste hours when versions are scattered across USB drives and laptops. Knowing which copy is the approved one relies on tribal knowledge rather than an enforced repository. Redundant storage is inexpensive, so unavailability stems from process gaps rather than resource limits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transferring contaminants from soiled areas to cleaned product contact zones during cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Transferring contaminants from soiled areas to cleaned product-contact zones during cleaning threatens product safety directly, but it is a cleaning-procedure error rather than a core cartoning function, moderating relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning SOPs and color-coded tooling substantially reduce cross-contamination, but manual cleaning still leaves recurring transfer risk between soiled and clean zones. Directional cleaning sequences and changeover of tools between zones add real time to cleaning cycles. Avoiding clean-to-dirty cross-transfer depends on the technician rigorously following zoning and sequence discipline. Dedicated cleaning tools, wipes, and validated agents carry recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing particulates or residues onto cleaned machinery surfaces or packaged product during wear-part replacement
rows.imp_rat: Introducing particulates onto cleaned surfaces or product during wear-part replacement can contaminate output (moderate-to-high severity), yet it occurs only during maintenance windows, making it less central to the core job's ongoing execution.
rows.sat_rat: SOPs, cleanroom garbing, and post-swap inspection catch most contamination, but manual handling of greasy wear parts near product zones still leaves recurring stray-particulate incidents. Post-replacement wipe-down and inspection add modest time to a wear-part swap but are routinely built into the maintenance window. Avoiding particulate transfer during physical part swaps depends heavily on technician diligence and hygiene discipline rather than any foolproof tooling. Cleaning consumables, wipes, and any scrapped contaminated product are a minor but recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a wear component with the wrong dimensions, material, or specification
rows.imp_rat: Installing a wrong-spec wear component is a maintenance-quality error somewhat removed from the running job (relevance) but can cause premature failure or damage (severity).
rows.sat_rat: BOMs, labeled spare kits, and part-number checks catch most mismatches, though occasional wrong-material or lookalike parts still slip through. Verifying part number and spec before install adds little time when documentation is at hand. Correct part selection relies on the technician cross-checking specs, though catalogs and kitted parts reduce the judgement needed. Wrong-spec installs waste the part and the labor and can damage the machine, but kitting curbs this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment of replaced belts, guides, rollers, or tooling
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment of replaced belts, guides, rollers, or tooling directly affects carton forming and transport quality, so it is fairly central; but such alignment is typically caught and corrected during commissioning, so realized severity stays limited under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard fixtures, dial indicators, and OEM procedures make alignment largely repeatable when followed, though errors still slip through. Precise alignment of belts and tooling is time-consuming but a routine, planned part of the job. Good alignment leans heavily on the fitter's experience, gauges, and feel, making outcomes technician-dependent. Misalignment causes premature wear and jams, adding modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of adjusted timing, alignment, and format parameters that remain outside specified tolerances
rows.imp_rat: Adjusted timing, alignment, and format parameters staying outside tolerance is fairly central to cartoning quality (relevance), and out-of-tolerance parameters cause misformed cartons and downstream defects (moderate severity); capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Gauges, setup sheets, and format documentation keep most parameters in tolerance, though manual adjustment leaves a residual out-of-spec fraction. Measuring and confirming timing, alignment, and format parameters adds bounded verification time. Hitting tolerance on many adjustable parameters relies on technician finesse and measurement discipline. Out-of-tolerance parameters cause rejects, but the adjustment cost itself is small.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering the controlled packaging area during calibration activities
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering the controlled packaging area during calibration carry real product-integrity risk, but calibration is an intermittent setup activity rather than the central packaging function.
rows.sat_rat: Access controls, gowning, and defined calibration procedures handle most cases, but bringing instruments and hands into the controlled zone remains a recurring contamination vector. Gowning, tool cleaning, and area re-verification during calibration add predictable but manageable time. Keeping contaminants out during calibration relies on trained technicians following cleanroom protocol rather than passive engineering controls. Costs are limited to consumables and occasional re-cleaning of the area.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a sensor or encoder remains outside its specified tolerance after recalibration
rows.imp_rat: A sensor or encoder staying out of tolerance after recalibration is a maintenance-accuracy error (relevance) that can cause mistimed operation and defects if undetected (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibration procedures with post-check verification keep most sensors in tolerance, with residual drift or setup error uncommon. Post-calibration verification checks add routine, bounded time. Bringing a sensor into tolerance is largely proceduralized, reducing the skill needed once the method is defined. Calibration labor and reference-standard upkeep are modest recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the controlled manufacturing area during calibration
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination into the controlled manufacturing area during calibration echoes item 6 with slightly broader scope; consequential for product integrity but tied to occasional calibration, not the ongoing job.
rows.sat_rat: Mature GMP contamination-control practice covers most calibration activity, though manual instrument introduction still produces occasional breaches. Controlled entry and line-clearance steps for calibration are standard and add only routine time. Preventing manufacturing-area contamination during calibration leans on procedural discipline and behavioral compliance from technicians. Minor consumable and downtime costs accompany the required contamination controls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of validated machine parameters and production recipes altered or lost during the update
rows.imp_rat: Validated parameters/recipes altered or lost during an update is not part of normal running (lower relevance), but losing validated settings has serious quality and revalidation consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Updates frequently overwrite or reset validated parameters without a robust export/reimport step, so drift and loss are common. Re-entering lost validated parameters after an update consumes significant re-commissioning time. Preserving recipes through firmware updates demands controls expertise that on-site maintenance staff seldom have. Lost validated recipes trigger re-validation and possible scrap, a meaningful but occasional expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incompatibilities between specified machinery interfaces and existing line controls, material flows, utilities, and physical constraints
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibilities between machinery interfaces and existing line controls, utilities, and constraints matter to integration success (relevance), but these are design-phase issues resolvable before harm (severity), capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Site-integration reviews and P&ID/layout checks catch most physical and utility conflicts, but some interface issues still emerge at install. Interface and utility compatibility studies are a normal but time-consuming pre-installation activity. Reconciling controls, material flow, utilities and physical constraints needs multidisciplinary line-integration engineers. Late-discovered incompatibilities drive expensive integration rework, though layout reviews mitigate this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking supplier noncompliance with regulatory requirements applicable to cartoning machinery in sterile fill-finish
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking supplier regulatory noncompliance can create serious compliance exposure (high severity), but it is a procurement-vetting error upstream of machine operation, so its relevance to running the job is limited.
rows.sat_rat: Established supplier-qualification and audit programs in regulated pharma make overlooked noncompliance uncommon, though not eliminated. Regulatory compliance review is a defined gate but chasing supplier documentation can be slow. Verifying supplier regulatory compliance for sterile fill-finish requires specialized QA/regulatory expertise that mature pharma buyers usually have in-house. Compliance audits add cost, but the audit process is well-established and predictable in spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartoning activity is attributed to the wrong batch or lot
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that packaging work is recorded as belonging to the wrong production group. Correct records are needed to trace what happened to each group of products.
rows.imp_rat: Wrong-batch attribution is peripheral to the physical cartoning job (low relevance) but has serious compliance/recall consequences when it occurs (moderate severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record headers and scanning reduce misattribution, though manual steps still allow errors. Wrong-batch attribution is a documentation error corrected slowly during review. Correct batch attribution depends on operator entry discipline. Misattribution causes costly record investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of blocked discharge preventing completed cartons from leaving the machine
rows.plain: Keep completed boxes from being stuck at the machine for as little time as possible. This helps the line keep moving instead of backing up.
rows.imp_rat: Blocked discharge delays are throughput/availability issues peripheral to correct, safe cartoning, with modest and recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation conveyors and full-line sensors detect blockage and stop feed reliably, limiting how long discharge stays blocked. Blocked discharge stops the machine and is felt immediately as downtime until cleared. Clearing a discharge blockage is a routine operator task requiring modest skill. Downtime consumes labor and capacity, though little material is lost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from target cartoning throughput during operation
rows.plain: Keep the carton-packing speed close to the planned rate during operation.
rows.imp_rat: Throughput deviation is an efficiency/productivity concern peripheral to correct, safe cartoning; consequences are largely economic and recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Modern cartoners with servo control and speed monitoring hold rate reasonably well, though minor stops still pull throughput off target. Throughput deviation directly manifests as lost run time, and operators monitor rate meters to react quickly. Holding target throughput requires experienced operators who anticipate feed and jam behavior. Off-target throughput wastes machine capacity and labor hours but rarely scraps material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prior-run cartons, inserts, or sterile units remain after line clearance
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that boxes, instruction sheets, or germ-free units from the previous run are left behind after the line is cleared.
rows.imp_rat: Residual prior-run units after clearance is a mix-up/contamination risk (moderate severity) but is a clearance-procedure concern peripheral to core erection/insertion (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Documented line-clearance checks are standard but rely on visual inspection that occasionally misses items. Line clearance to remove prior-run items adds changeover time. Thorough clearance depends on operator diligence rather than special skill. Residual prior-run items risk costly mix-up rejections.
