rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that machine configuration does not match the approved product and carton format at startup
rows.plain: Make sure the machine setup matches the approved product and carton type before starting. This helps prevent the wrong settings from damaging products or causing packaging problems.
rows.imp_rat: Config mismatch at startup can produce wrong-format packaging (moderate relevance) with meaningful rework/compliance consequences (moderate severity), but ranked below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and setup verification make configuration matching fairly reliable, with residual manual-check failures. Startup configuration checks add setup time but are routine. Verifying config against approved format requires trained setup judgement. Wrong configuration at startup wastes startup material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prior-run cartons, leaflets, labels, or sterile units remain in the cartoning machine after line clearance
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that boxes, instruction sheets, labels, or germ-free units from the previous run remain inside the packing machine after it is cleared.
rows.imp_rat: Residual prior-run components after line clearance is a mix-up/contamination risk (moderate severity) but a clearance-procedure matter peripheral to core erection (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Documented line-clearance procedures are mature and effective, though hidden residuals sometimes remain. Line clearance is a defined but time-consuming step. Thorough clearance depends on operator diligence more than expertise. Residual prior-run material risks mix-ups and rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that hazardous motion remains accessible because a guard or interlock is bypassed, disabled, or misaligned
rows.plain: Make sure guards and safety devices have not been bypassed, turned off, or put out of position so that dangerous moving parts can still be reached. This helps protect people from moving machinery.
rows.imp_rat: A bypassed guard/interlock is a safety hazard with high severity if it occurs, but it is peripheral to the core packaging job function (low relevance). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interlock systems and safety checks are mature and reliable, though deliberate bypasses remain a residual hazard. Guard/interlock checks add brief confirmation time. Recognizing a bypassed or misaligned guard needs safety awareness. Little material cost, but injury cost is severe.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an emergency stop fails to halt hazardous cartoning-machine motion during confirmation checks
rows.plain: Make sure the emergency stop actually stops dangerous machine movement during safety checks. This helps protect people if the machine must be stopped quickly.
rows.imp_rat: E-stop failure to halt hazardous motion is safety-critical (high severity) yet tangential to erecting/inserting cartons (low relevance). Capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: E-stop circuits are highly reliable and routinely verified, so failures are rare. E-stop confirmation is a quick standard check. Testing e-stops requires minimal skill. Negligible material cost; failure cost is safety-critical.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exposure to electrical, pneumatic, thermal, or vacuum hazards from incorrect or leaking connections
rows.plain: Prevent people from being exposed to electrical power, pressurized air, heat, or suction because connections are wrong or leaking. This helps make sure the equipment is safely connected before use.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to electrical/pneumatic/thermal hazards is high-severity for personnel but peripheral to the packaging job itself (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized connectors and inspection routines make exposure uncommon but leaks still occur. Connection checks add modest setup time. Identifying leaking or wrong connections needs trained technicians. Leaks waste energy/media and risk injury cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of carton blanks failing to open fully during erection
rows.plain: Reduce the number of flat carton pieces that do not open all the way when being made into boxes. This helps the boxes form correctly and hold the product securely.
rows.imp_rat: Blanks failing to open fully halt erection (moderate relevance to the job) but the effect is a stoppage or reject, not irreversible harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vacuum openers work well but blank quality and suction wear cause recurring open failures. Failed openings force stoppages, adding time. Diagnosing suction/opening issues needs mechanical knowledge. Blanks that fail to open are scrapped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of erected cartons with flaps projecting into the sterile-unit insertion path
rows.plain: Make sure the folded carton flaps do not stick into the path where the sterile container is inserted. This prevents the carton from blocking or damaging the container.
rows.imp_rat: Flaps projecting into the insertion path directly block sterile-unit insertion (moderate relevance), but consequences are typically a jam or reject rather than lasting harm (low-moderate severity). Capped under 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Flap guides and openers usually clear the path but marginal setups leave intruding flaps. Adjusting flap clearance adds tuning time during setup. Setting flap folders to clear the insertion path is a skilled adjustment. Flaps in the path cause jams and scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile units contacting unintended surfaces while material flow is adjusted
rows.plain: Keep sterile parts from touching the wrong surfaces while the material flow is being changed. This helps prevent contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile units contacting unintended surfaces risks sterility breach (moderate severity) but arises during flow adjustment, peripheral to routine insertion (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Guided paths limit contact but adjustment phases still risk incidental touching. Avoiding contact during flow adjustment costs careful setup time. Preventing unintended surface contact needs skilled path setup. Contact damages sterile units, causing scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterile units with compromised container integrity after machine clearing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of germ-free containers whose seals or packaging are damaged after a machine stoppage is cleared. This helps keep the product protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Compromised container integrity after machine clearing is a sterility-breach risk (moderate severity) but concerns clearance handling somewhat outside core erection (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Inspection after clearing catches most damage but subtle integrity loss can be missed. Post-clearing integrity checks add time. Assessing container integrity after clearing needs trained inspection. Compromised units after clearing are high-value scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cartons, labels, leaflets, or sterile units from different formats being mixed during stoppage recovery
rows.plain: Prevent packages, labels, instruction sheets, or sterile items for different product types from getting mixed up while restarting after a stoppage. This helps ensure the right materials stay with the right product.
rows.imp_rat: Mixing formats during stoppage recovery is a mix-up risk with moderate severity but is a recovery-procedure concern peripheral to core erection/insertion (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Segregation practices reduce mixing but stoppage recovery is an error-prone moment. Recovering from stoppage without mixing costs handling time. Preventing format mixing during recovery needs careful control. Mixed formats scrap materials and risk defects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton blanks, leaflets, labels, or sterile units for the wrong format are consumed during setup
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that packaging pieces or sterile products meant for another product size or design are used during setup. This avoids waste and prevents the wrong materials from entering production.
rows.imp_rat: Consuming wrong-format materials during setup is mostly a waste/cost issue with low relevance to the core insertion job and modest, recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Line clearance and staging controls limit mix-ups but setup-phase consumption of wrong materials still occurs. Consuming wrong-format materials during setup wastes time reclearing and restarting. Preventing wrong-material consumption relies on operator vigilance during staging. Consumed wrong-format blanks, labels, leaflets and sterile units are direct scrap losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that rejection-verification samples mix with production output during startup
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that samples used to check rejected items get mixed with regular production output when the equipment starts up.
rows.imp_rat: Verification samples mixing with production output is a mix-up risk (moderate severity) but a startup-procedure concern peripheral to core erection (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Sample segregation practices are standard but startup mixing occasionally happens. Segregating reject samples adds minor handling time. Requires procedural discipline, not deep skill. Mixing samples with output risks contamination cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton erection or closure defects escape verification before full production
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that poorly formed or sealed cartons are missed before regular production begins. Early checks help prevent a large number of faulty packages.
rows.imp_rat: Defects escaping verification before full production is relevant to output quality (moderate) with moderate severity if bad cartons propagate; ranked below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Inline and manual inspection catch most defects but subtle closure faults still escape. Verification before full production adds a controlled time gate. Judging erection/closure defects requires trained inspection. Escaped defects cause downstream rejects and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartons collapse or open under distribution loads
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that cartons cave in or come open while being shipped. Strong, secure cartons help protect the products inside.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons collapsing under distribution loads threatens product protection (moderate severity) but concerns downstream distribution, peripheral to the machine's core task (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Load testing standards make collapse a well-managed and predictable risk. Compression and load testing to verify integrity is a standard but time-consuming step. Structural carton design against loads is a mature engineering competency. Collapse causes damage and rework; over-design wastes material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton surfaces abrading or puncturing sterile barriers during distribution
rows.plain: Have fewer cartons scrape against or puncture the protective packaging around sterile products while being shipped. This helps keep the products protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Carton surfaces abrading/puncturing sterile barriers in distribution is a sterility-breach risk (moderate severity) but is a distribution-performance concern (low-moderate relevance to the machine job).
rows.sat_rat: Testing catches many abrasion risks but real distribution conditions still cause occasional barrier contact damage. Evaluating abrasion/puncture risk requires distribution simulation adding time. Predicting surface abrasion needs materials and packaging-dynamics expertise. Abrasion-caused barrier damage generates scrap, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that damaged cartons escape detection before distribution
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that damaged cartons are missed before products are shipped. Finding damage early helps keep products protected and prevents unsuitable packages from reaching distribution.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged cartons escaping detection before distribution risks unprotected product reaching market (moderate severity) but detection is somewhat peripheral to core erection (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Manual and automated inspection catch most but not all damaged cartons before shipment. Inspecting for damaged cartons before distribution adds line inspection time. Detecting subtle carton damage relies on inspector attention and vision systems of varying capability. Escaped damaged cartons cause complaints and returns, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton material fails protection requirements during handling and distribution
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that packaging material cannot protect the product while it is being handled or shipped. The material should keep the product protected without being used unnecessarily.
rows.imp_rat: Carton failing protection requirements in distribution risks product/sterility damage (moderate severity) but is a material-performance concern somewhat outside erection/insertion (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Material qualification is mature so protection failures are relatively uncommon but not impossible. Confirming material protection performance requires transit and environmental testing time. Selecting protective materials uses well-established packaging-materials expertise. Under-protective material causes damage losses; over-spec adds material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required label content becomes obscured on erected cartons
rows.plain: Make sure the required information on a label can still be seen after the box is assembled. This matters so people can read the information they need.
rows.imp_rat: Obscured label content on erected cartons undermines readability (moderate relevance) with moderate severity; kept under the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Erected-sample reviews catch most obscuring but panel-fold interactions occasionally hide content. Checking that content stays visible on erected cartons requires fit-and-fold reviews adding modest time. Predicting obscured content needs design-review attention rather than rare expertise. Obscured content causes rework, not major material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of carton labels that are incorrect, misplaced, detached, or unreadable under expected conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the number of carton labels that are wrong, in the wrong place, come off, or cannot be read in normal use. This helps people identify the packaged product correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect/unreadable carton labels bear on identity and readability (moderate relevance) with meaningful patient/compliance severity, but ranked below batch 1 so held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Label specs and inspection are mature but detachment or unreadability under real conditions still occurs occasionally. Assessing label placement and durability under expected conditions requires targeted testing time. Specifying robust label performance draws on adhesion and print expertise that is fairly established. Bad labels cause rejects and rework, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of cartons that open, deform, or lose structural integrity under expected conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the number of cartons that open, bend out of shape, or lose their strength during normal use. This helps the cartons continue protecting the product.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons losing structural integrity under expected conditions directly undermines the secondary-packaging job (moderate relevance) with meaningful protection severity, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard testing controls most integrity loss but edge conditions still cause occasional failures. Verifying integrity under expected conditions requires environmental and handling trials. Designing cartons that hold integrity uses established structural expertise. Cartons that open or deform generate scrap and complaints, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protocol scenarios fail to detect damage to labeled sterile units or their sterile barriers during insertion and carton handling
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that planned tests fail to find damage to labeled germ-free units or to the protective packaging that keeps them germ-free while they are inserted and packed.
rows.imp_rat: Protocol scenarios failing to detect barrier damage risks undetected sterility breach (moderate severity) but is a protocol-design concern peripheral to routine insertion (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Barrier damage from handling is a known critical risk that standard visual protocols only partially detect. Adding sterile-barrier damage checks to protocols extends inspection time. Detecting subtle barrier damage requires specialized inspection skill and equipment. Integrity testing consumes units destructively and adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that diagnostic handling compromises sterile unit identity, segregation, or package integrity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that investigating a problem damages the identity, separation, or sealed packaging of sterile products. Careful handling keeps products correctly identified, apart from one another, and protected.
rows.imp_rat: Diagnostic handling compromising unit identity or package integrity risks sterility/identity breach (moderate severity) but arises in diagnostics, peripheral to the routine job (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Segregation and identity controls are well-established in sterile operations, keeping compromise relatively rare. Controlling diagnostic handling to protect identity and integrity slows diagnostic work. Careful aseptic and segregation handling during diagnostics requires trained personnel. Handling controls and potential unit loss add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that insertion compromises labeled sterile-unit package integrity during verification testing
rows.plain: Make sure inserting something during testing does not damage the labeled sterile package. This helps confirm that the package can still protect its contents after testing.
rows.imp_rat: Insertion compromising package integrity in verification risks sterility breach (moderate severity) but occurs in verification testing, peripheral to production output (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Insertion forces can damage packages and detection is imperfect, leaving a meaningful reliability gap. Verifying insertion doesn't compromise integrity adds test and inspection time. Assessing insertion-induced integrity loss requires specialized integrity-test skill. Destructive integrity testing consumes units and adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of documented parameter values that differ from the machine's as-installed settings
rows.imp_rat: Documented parameter values differing from as-installed settings weakens traceability and complicates future troubleshooting, but the machine can still run cartoning correctly in the moment, so relevance to the CFJ is moderate and severity is bounded to eventual maintenance confusion.
rows.sat_rat: Documented values frequently diverge from actual settings because manual transcription and late tweaks are rarely reconciled. Transcribing as-installed parameters into documentation is a manual, time-consuming step often deferred. Accurate documentation requires care to read and record actual machine settings, an error-prone manual task. The cost is mostly clerical labor, low but easily skimped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between the operator initiating the pause and the cartoning machinery reaching a stopped state
rows.imp_rat: Delay between initiating a pause and reaching stopped state matters for control and safety (moderate relevance), but a short lag typically has limited consequences (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled-stop behavior is a well-established, dependable feature on current cartoning machinery. Modern servo drives bring the machine to a controlled stop within a cycle or two of the pause command, so this delay is short. The stop is automated and requires no special operator skill once the button is pressed. The brief stopping interval consumes negligible resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unclear machinery status preventing confirmation that the cycle is paused
rows.imp_rat: Unclear machinery status blocking confirmation of a paused cycle is a moderate operational concern (relevance), with consequences of confusion or unsafe assumptions that are usually recoverable (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard HMIs with clear state indication and stack lights make paused status confirmable most of the time, though poorly designed interfaces still cause occasional uncertainty. Checking whether the cycle is truly paused usually happens quickly via the HMI or indicator lights. Interpreting mixed status signals can require operator experience when displays are ambiguous. Ambiguous status rarely causes direct spend beyond occasional caution-related delay.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between observing an output-quality deviation and completing the necessary parameter adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Time between observing a quality deviation and completing the adjustment has moderate relevance to limiting defective output and moderate severity via accumulated scrap, kept within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Detection is typically periodic manual inspection rather than continuous, so response time is inconsistent and often lengthy. The lag from spotting a quality deviation to completing the fix is largely manual and often slow, running defective output in the interim. Reacting quickly depends on the operator noticing and knowing the right parameter to move. Every minute of delay accumulates off-spec cartons and lost material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cartoning machine starvation caused by intermittent upstream product flow
rows.imp_rat: Starvation from intermittent upstream product flow is largely an upstream-coordination problem peripheral to the cartoning mechanism itself (relevance), with recoverable idle-time consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation buffers smooth intermittent upstream flow reasonably well but cannot fully prevent starvation during longer upstream disruptions. Upstream flow gaps idle the cartoner, wasting run time until product resumes. Buffer management is largely automatic, needing little skill. Starvation itself wastes little material but reduces line efficiency.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required fill, seal, and condition attributes that cannot be assessed during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Fill, seal, and condition attributes that cannot be assessed during inspection limits verification quality (moderate relevance), with consequences of undetected defects that are partially caught elsewhere (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Inline vision and checkweighers cover many fill/seal attributes, but some conditions still cannot be fully verified during the run. Some attributes require destructive or offline checks that slow full assessment. Assessing seal integrity or fill by hand requires trained inspection technique. Unassessable attributes risk defect escapes but the immediate cost is defect-driven.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the inspector applies incorrect or outdated acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: An inspector applying outdated acceptance criteria is a specific inspection-step error (lower relevance to core forming/sealing job), but wrong criteria can pass defective cartons or reject good ones (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled-document practices exist but reliance on paper specs and human recall means outdated criteria are still occasionally applied. Verifying which acceptance criteria apply can slow inspection when documents are scattered. Applying the correct current criteria depends on the inspector's training and diligence. Using outdated criteria risks scrapping good product or passing bad, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree the mechanism travels beyond the intended incremental position during jogging
rows.imp_rat: Overtravel beyond the intended jog position is a service-mode precision error (relevance) that can cause collision or mispositioning damage when it occurs (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Servo inch/jog with defined step sizes gives fairly repeatable incremental moves, so overshoot is uncommon on modern machines. Overshoot during jog forces repeated corrective jogs, adding minor time. Fine incremental positioning depends on operator throttle control, a modest manual skill. Overshoot rarely causes material loss unless it drives into tooling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of in-process cartons left inside the machine when standby is reached
rows.imp_rat: In-process cartons left inside the machine at standby creates rework and clearing effort (moderate relevance), but the consequence is limited scrap and restart delay (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Some machines have empty-out or clear-cycle routines, but many leave partially processed cartons inside at standby, so results are inconsistent. Clearing in-process cartons at standby adds startup delay when the line resumes. Operators must know where product tends to strand, which is learned rather than automated. Stranded in-process cartons are frequently scrapped, driving material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of moving components binding or seizing when the machine returns to service
rows.imp_rat: Components binding or seizing on return to service moderately affects restart reliability (relevance) and can stop the line (severity), but as a post-service issue it stays under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual bar-over and low-speed jog checks reliably catch binding before startup in most maintenance routines. Checking free movement before return to service adds only a brief manual-turn step. Verifying components move freely is a straightforward hand-check requiring little special skill. A seized component on startup can cause costly damage, but the preventive check is free.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of shared production-line utilities or controls being unintentionally disabled during decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Unintentionally disabling shared line utilities/controls during decommissioning is a one-time transition error (low relevance to ongoing cartoning) with potentially serious knock-on consequences (severity), held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Management-of-change and isolation procedures catch most cases, but interconnected PLC and network dependencies still produce occasional surprise disables. Verifying that no shared controls are disabled lengthens the decommissioning sequence but is a routine part of change control. Distinguishing dedicated from shared controls requires controls-engineering expertise that is not always present on a disposal crew. An unintended disable of line controls causes costly downtime, motivating careful practice.

rows.stk: Sustainability Officer / Environmental Health and Safety Specialist
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing hazardous residues from decommissioned cartoning machine components during sorting and transfer
rows.imp_rat: Releasing hazardous residues from decommissioned components during sorting is an end-of-life disposal concern with real safety consequences, but it is peripheral to the running cartoning job, so relevance is low.
rows.sat_rat: Residue release during sorting is inconsistently controlled, so contamination during transfer recurs. Sorting components while containing residues slows down the transfer stage. Recognizing which decommissioned parts hold hazardous residue requires informed judgement. Hazardous-waste segregation and disposal containers add cost.

rows.source_job: Disconnect the machine from facility utilities
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a shared utility feed serving adjacent sterile fill-finish equipment is severed during cartoning machine disconnection
rows.imp_rat: Severing a shared utility feed to adjacent sterile equipment during disconnection is peripheral to routine cartoning (low relevance) but potentially severe for neighboring sterile processes; capped by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout-tagout and permit-to-work practices exist and mostly prevent severance, yet stale drawings and undocumented cross-connections leave a real recurring failure risk. Tracing a shared utility feed to adjacent sterile fill-finish equipment adds time to the disconnection plan, but experienced pharma facilities budget for it. Correctly identifying which feeds are shared demands scarce facilities-engineering judgement and up-to-date P&ID knowledge that is often incomplete. A severed shared feed can idle a whole sterile suite, so the financial exposure is large but usually avoided.

rows.source_job: Remove machine anchors and foundation fixtures
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fixture-removal dust and debris entering adjacent controlled areas of the sterile fill-finish facility
rows.imp_rat: Fixture-removal dust entering adjacent controlled areas is peripheral to the cartoning function itself (low relevance) but carries elevated severity due to sterile-facility contamination risk; still held at or below the 4.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Dust and debris from fixture removal frequently migrate into adjacent controlled areas despite containment attempts. Dust and debris control during fixture removal adds masking and cleanup time. Containing removal dust in a controlled sterile area requires cleanroom containment discipline. Barriers, HEPA vacuums, and containment enclosures add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of worn or damaged mechanical, pneumatic, and electrical components that remain undetected during inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected worn/damaged mechanical, pneumatic, or electrical components during inspection has moderate relevance to keeping the machine running and moderate severity via eventual breakdowns, held under 4.5 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Checklist-based visual inspection catches obvious wear but consistently misses a meaningful percentage of hidden or early-stage damage. Thorough inspection of all mechanical, pneumatic, and electrical components takes significant time that is often abbreviated. Detecting subtle wear or damage depends on an inspector's trained eye and thoroughness, which varies greatly between individuals. Missed defects lead to downstream failures and waste, but inspection itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that tubing, fittings, or valve connections are restored incorrectly after service
rows.imp_rat: Tubing/fittings/valve connections restored incorrectly after service is a maintenance-restoration error of moderate relevance to reliable operation and moderate severity via leaks or malfunction, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Labeled lines, quick-connects, and P&ID references keep most restorations correct, though occasional swapped or under-torqued connections persist. Reconnecting tubing and fittings correctly is quick when routing is documented or marked. Correct pneumatic and fluid reassembly relies on the technician's familiarity and following routing diagrams. A misconnection can cause leaks or damage, but correct reassembly costs nothing extra.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of mistimed machine elements damaging cartons or product during verification runs
rows.imp_rat: Mistimed elements damaging cartons or product during verification runs has moderate relevance to setup quality and moderate severity via damaged output, kept within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Dry runs and reduced-speed verification catch most mistiming before production, though phasing errors still occasionally crush cartons or product. Verification runs with dummy or scrap product add controlled setup time before full production. Timing verification relies on the technician correctly interpreting motion and phasing during trial runs. Damaged cartons or product during trials are contained waste rather than a large cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that calibration results are invalid because reference values are incorrect or untraceable
rows.imp_rat: Invalid calibration from incorrect or untraceable references is an administrative/quality-assurance error somewhat removed from the running job (relevance), but bad references can propagate systematic errors (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established metrology programs with certified traceable standards make invalid reference values rare where a calibration system is in place. Using traceable standards is a standard step that adds negligible time. Traceability is largely a documentation-discipline task rather than a skill-intensive one. Maintaining certified, traceable reference standards carries real but well-understood calibration-program cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved incompatibilities between candidate machinery controls architecture and existing packaging line controls
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved controls-architecture incompatibilities with the existing line have moderate relevance to integration and moderate severity via commissioning delays, held at or below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Controls integration remains a frequent friction point, with legacy-to-new architecture gaps often only fully understood during integration. Resolving controls-architecture mismatches before purchase requires iterative technical exchange that adds time. Reconciling candidate controls architecture with existing line controls demands scarce automation and integration specialists. Unresolved controls incompatibilities force costly middleware or retrofits during commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable pharmaceutical and sterile fill-finish compliance requirements omitted from the machinery specification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting pharma/sterile compliance requirements from the specification is a documentation-stage error with downstream compliance risk, but it is far upstream of the core cartoning job, so relevance is low.
rows.sat_rat: Template-based URS practice covers common requirements well, but omissions of less-obvious sterile requirements still recur. Building a complete compliance-laden specification takes considerable up-front time before quoting. Knowing the full set of pharmaceutical and sterile fill-finish requirements to specify depends heavily on scarce regulatory expertise. Omissions surface as change orders and requalification costs later, though good specs contain this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between specified throughput and expected production demand, product mix, and line constraints
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between specified throughput and actual demand/product mix affects capacity planning, but it is a specification-fit issue rather than a direct cartoning-execution error, keeping both proxies moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Demand forecasts are inherently uncertain and product mix shifts, so misalignment between spec and real demand is common. Aligning specified throughput to demand and product mix requires forecasting cycles that can lengthen planning. Translating production demand and product-mix scenarios into a throughput spec demands experienced planners and line engineers. Over- or under-sizing throughput wastes capital or capacity, a significant but recognized cost trade-off.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that replenishment introduces contamination onto packaging materials or into the cartoning area
rows.imp_rat: Replenishment introducing contamination is a moderate-severity sterility risk but is a material-handling concern outside core erection/insertion (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Procedures for clean material introduction exist but manual replenishment remains an inconsistent contamination pathway. Contamination from replenishment isn't detected quickly during the act. Clean replenishment depends on careful operator technique and gowning discipline. Introduced contamination can scrap material and units, an expensive risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of prior-run fragments or residue remaining in cartoning-machine material paths after clearance
rows.imp_rat: Residual fragments/residue in material paths is a contamination/mix-up risk (moderate severity) but a clearance concern peripheral to core erection (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning routines reduce residue but fragments in hidden paths persist. Thorough residue removal from paths is time-consuming. Finding hidden fragments depends on operator diligence. Residue causes later jams and scrap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of change parts or fasteners left loose, misaligned, or unsecured after setup
rows.imp_rat: Loose/unsecured change parts after setup can cause machine damage or defects (moderate severity) but is a maintenance-quality concern (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Torque checks and checklists help but loose or unsecured parts still slip through. Securing all fasteners adds a verification step. Ensuring nothing is left loose depends on operator thoroughness. Loose parts cause damage and downtime, costly to recover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of erected cartons with torn panels, crushed edges, or unintended creases
rows.imp_rat: Torn/crushed/creased erected cartons is a common erection defect (moderate relevance) usually yielding rejects rather than severe harm (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Handling tuning reduces damage but crushing and creasing recur during setup. Torn/crushed cartons force re-runs, costing some time. Reducing panel damage needs tuning skill on guides and tension. Damaged cartons are scrap material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of sterile-unit insertion-position drift during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Insertion-position drift during the run is relevant to placement quality (moderate) but usually yields correctable placement errors (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Servo positioning holds well but thermal and wear drift over a run persists. Monitoring insertion drift during run adds oversight but not stoppage. Detecting gradual position drift requires attentive skill. Drift causes progressive rejects.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of cartons with flaps or closures outside specified tolerances
rows.imp_rat: Flaps/closures out of tolerance affects carton closure quality (moderate relevance) but usually yields correctable defects rather than catastrophic outcomes (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Closure tooling is repeatable when set but tolerance drift recurs. Bringing flaps/closures to tolerance takes iterative adjustment. Closure tuning is a classic skill-dependent task. Out-of-tolerance closures scrap cartons.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a restart collision caused by cartons or sterile units left in the machine path
rows.imp_rat: Restart collision from residual items in the path directly disrupts erection/insertion (moderate relevance) with low-moderate severity (jam/machine damage, recoverable).
rows.sat_rat: Pre-restart path checks are standard but leftover items occasionally cause collisions. Checking for path obstructions before restart is quick. Low skill; visual path inspection. A restart collision damages parts, a costly event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with erection or sterile-unit insertion defects after restart
rows.imp_rat: Erection/insertion defects after restart is relevant to output quality (moderate) with low-moderate severity since typically caught and reworked.
rows.sat_rat: Restart procedures limit defects but the restart moment concentrates errors. Defects after restart force re-inspection time. Managing clean restarts requires experienced operators. Post-restart defects scrap cartons and units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of labeled sterile units scrapped because container integrity is compromised during insertion trials
rows.imp_rat: Units scrapped for compromised integrity during insertion trials touches insertion quality (low-moderate relevance) with moderate severity (sterility + waste).
rows.sat_rat: Careful trial ramp-up limits damage but some unit scrap is expected. Insertion trials proceed without major time impact. Minimizing scrapped units in trials depends on setup finesse. Scrapped high-value sterile units in trials are a direct, notable loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a nonconforming challenge carton enters accepted output during preproduction verification
rows.imp_rat: A nonconforming challenge carton entering accepted output during verification is a preproduction QA concern (low-moderate relevance) with moderate severity for output integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation and reconciliation of challenge cartons is standard but occasionally imperfect. Challenge testing adds a defined preproduction step. Managing challenge cartons requires procedural discipline. A nonconforming challenge entering output risks contamination of accepted lot.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of rejected challenge cartons remaining in the carton path after rejection
rows.imp_rat: Rejected challenge cartons remaining in the path risks contaminating output (moderate severity) but is a rejection-handling concern (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Reject bins and path checks work but retained challenge cartons occasionally remain. Clearing rejected challenges from the path is a quick step. Low skill; verification of empty reject path. Leftover rejects risk re-entering output, a costly error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between the onset of a packaging-integrity deviation and its recognition
rows.imp_rat: Delay recognizing an integrity deviation lets defective units accumulate (moderate severity) but detection timing is somewhat peripheral to core erection (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Detection eventually happens but the onset-to-recognition delay remains substantial. There is often significant lag between deviation onset and recognition given periodic checks. Faster recognition depends on operator vigilance that varies. Delayed recognition increases scrap produced before stopping.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of drift in critical cartoning characteristics from validated limits
rows.imp_rat: Drift in critical characteristics from validated limits affects run quality (moderate relevance) with low-moderate severity since drift is typically detectable and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous drift monitoring is limited, so parameter drift from validated limits recurs. Drift in critical characteristics is often noticed late during runs. Detecting drift depends on technician monitoring and experience. Undetected drift produces scrap before correction.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in incoming packaging-material properties across lots
rows.imp_rat: Incoming material variation across lots affects erection consistency (moderate relevance) but usually yields adjustable variation, not severe harm (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Incoming variation is only partly controlled and frequently causes run-to-run inconsistency. Lot-to-lot variation is assessed at receiving, not continuously. Managing material variation depends on supplier and QC expertise. Variation drives jams, scrap and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in critical cartoning characteristics across equivalent production runs
rows.imp_rat: Run-to-run variation in critical characteristics matters for consistency (moderate relevance) but consequences are usually tunable variation (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Variation across equivalent runs is inconsistently controlled, a recurring reliability gap. Cross-run comparison is typically retrospective, not real-time. Requires analytical skill to compare runs meaningfully. Run-to-run variation causes inconsistent quality and waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that carton labeling is verified against an obsolete or conflicting approved reference
rows.imp_rat: Verifying against an obsolete reference is a documentation-control error, tangential to physical cartoning (low relevance) but with moderate compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Verifying against wrong or obsolete references is a persistent, poorly automated failure mode. Reference validation is a pre-run document check that takes time. Relies on document control to ensure correct reference is used. Obsolete references cause costly escapes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required label attributes omitted from verification
rows.imp_rat: Omitted label-verification attributes weaken checking (low-moderate relevance to erection/insertion) with moderate severity if a bad label escapes.
rows.sat_rat: Verification recipes often omit some attributes, so gaps in coverage recur. Ensuring all label attributes are verified adds configuration time. Requires careful recipe setup to include every attribute in verification. Omitted verification lets defects escape at recall-level cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of sterile-unit movement within cartons during distribution
rows.imp_rat: Unit movement within cartons during distribution can damage barriers (moderate severity) but concerns post-packaging distribution, peripheral to erection/insertion (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Restraint design and testing control movement well but variability still allows some in-transit shifting. Assessing in-carton movement requires distribution or shake testing that takes time. Specifying restraint against movement draws on packaging-dynamics knowledge that is fairly available. Excess movement causes damage and rejects, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying packaging requirements from the wrong market or product variant
rows.imp_rat: Applying wrong-market requirements has serious compliance severity but is an upstream specification error tangential to the machine's physical job (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled requirement libraries help, but wrong-variant application still slips through under complexity. Confirming the correct market/variant requirements adds verification time to each project. Selecting the right market requirements needs regulatory-intelligence expertise that is scarce. Wrong-market requirements are cheap to avoid but hugely costly if they reach production.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between carton artwork and approved product labeling
rows.imp_rat: Artwork-to-approved-labeling discrepancies bear on label correctness (low-moderate relevance to erection/insertion) with moderate severity if wrong labeling ships.
rows.sat_rat: Formal artwork-approval workflows catch most discrepancies, making this comparatively well controlled. Comparing artwork to approved labeling is a defined proofing step but still time-consuming. Artwork reconciliation is a well-established proofreading discipline with supporting roles. Artwork discrepancies caught late scrap printed stock, a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required printed elements failing legibility or machine-readability criteria after cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Printed elements failing legibility/machine-readability after cartoning is moderately severe for downstream scanning but only moderately central to erection/insertion.
rows.sat_rat: Print verification is mature but readability failures after handling still appear at a low rate. Verifying legibility and machine-readability after cartoning requires post-process scanning trials. Setting legibility and code-quality criteria uses established print-quality expertise. Unreadable codes cause line stoppages and rejects, a real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that packaging changes bypass regulatory impact assessment
rows.imp_rat: Packaging changes bypassing regulatory impact assessment is high-severity for compliance but is a change-control process concern peripheral to the machine job (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Change-control gates are established but changes still occasionally bypass regulatory review. Routing changes through regulatory impact assessment adds process time. Judging which changes need regulatory assessment requires experienced change-control staff. Bypassed assessments are cheap to avoid but very costly if they trigger non-compliance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in carton erection and sterile-unit placement across repeated runs under the same defined conditions
rows.imp_rat: Variation in erection and placement across repeated runs is moderately central to consistent output but typically manifests as correctable variation (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern cartoners are fairly repeatable under fixed conditions and statistical control charts capture variation reasonably well. Measuring cross-run variation under fixed conditions adds test time but is manageable. Interpreting placement variation needs metrology skill and process understanding. Repeated runs and measurements cost material and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of nonconforming labeled-unit, carton, label, or insert combinations not rejected during verification testing
rows.imp_rat: Nonconforming combinations not rejected in verification lets bad packs pass (moderate severity) but is a verification-effectiveness concern (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Reject-verification challenge testing is established practice and catches most obvious nonconformances, with residual edge-case gaps. Running deliberate nonconforming challenges adds test time. Constructing valid nonconforming challenge sets requires expertise in likely defect modes. Creating and scrapping nonconforming samples adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reintroduced labeled sterile units retaining invalid prior carton associations
rows.imp_rat: Reintroduced units retaining invalid prior associations is a serialization/traceability defect (low relevance to physical job) with moderate severity for record integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Reintroduction handling is a known weak spot where stale associations frequently persist without robust systemic control. Clearing prior associations on reintroduced units adds handling time during exception recovery. Correctly disassociating and reintroducing units depends on operator adherence to nuanced procedures. Invalid retained associations cause data errors that can quarantine batches, a meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the test-batch setup and the intended production configuration
rows.imp_rat: A mismatch between test-batch setup and production configuration is a changeover verification concern, peripheral to steady-state cartoning (relevance), with moderate consequences of a bad first run (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Test setups often differ subtly from production conditions, so undetected mismatch is a recurring source of later surprises. Matching the test-batch setup to intended production takes effort to replicate materials, speeds, and formats. Recognizing every relevant configuration parameter to match demands experienced process judgement. Using production-representative material for tests adds some consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of final configuration changes absent from the handover baseline
rows.imp_rat: Final configuration changes missing from the handover baseline create documentation gaps rather than immediate operational failure, so relevance to producing cartons is low and severity is limited to later reconciliation and audit effort.
rows.sat_rat: Final changes are among the most likely to escape the baseline, making handover records a chronic weak spot. Capturing last-minute configuration changes into the handover baseline is a step easily lost in the rush to finish. It relies on personnel remembering to log final changes, a discipline-dependent and commonly neglected task. Updating the baseline costs only documentation time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of line stoppages while the operator identifies and responds to out-of-range inputs
rows.imp_rat: Line stoppages while the operator responds to out-of-range inputs cause productivity loss (moderate relevance), but the effect is short-term downtime with low lasting harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems flag out-of-range inputs reliably, yet resolution speed varies widely by operator and situation. When an out-of-range input triggers, the operator must diagnose and respond, and this troubleshooting extends line stoppage. Rapid correct response to an out-of-range alarm depends on operator diagnostic skill. Stoppage time has a throughput cost but little direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeated parameter adjustments caused by overcorrecting the initial deviation
rows.imp_rat: Repeated adjustments from overcorrecting deviations moderately affect setup efficiency (relevance) but mainly cost time and material, a modest consequence (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual open-loop tuning offers no damping, so overcorrection and hunting recur frequently. Overcorrection triggers repeated adjustment cycles that extend the time to stabilize. Dialing in the right correction magnitude without overshooting is a finesse skill that varies greatly between operators. Each extra adjustment cycle wastes more cartons during the hunt.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of tooling shifting unexpectedly while the technician repositions it
rows.imp_rat: Tooling shifting while being repositioned is a handling error during setup, peripheral to production job (relevance), with moderate misalignment consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Clamping and locating features help but tooling can still shift before it is locked, giving inconsistent results. Re-securing shifted tooling adds rework time to setup. Holding tooling steady while repositioning leans on operator dexterity and careful sequencing. Unexpected tooling shift can damage parts and waste setup effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of acceptable cartons rejected during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting acceptable cartons is a yield/waste inefficiency (moderate relevance), but the consequence is modest material loss rather than serious harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Well-tuned inspection systems keep false-reject rates low, but drift and conservative settings still produce avoidable rejects. False rejects don't stop the line but do reduce effective yield over time. Tuning reject thresholds to avoid over-rejection requires setup expertise. Every acceptable carton wrongly rejected is direct scrap and lost product cost.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory, safety, or standards requirements omitted from the pre-acquisition assessment of cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Omitting regulatory/safety requirements from pre-acquisition assessment is an administrative, one-time upstream task (low relevance to running the job); consequences can be compliance gaps but are addressable later (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established pre-acquisition assessment templates cover the common requirements well, but niche or updated standards are sometimes missed. Compiling all applicable regulatory, safety and standards requirements pre-acquisition is a lengthy documentation effort. Knowing the complete applicable requirement set relies on specialized regulatory and safety expertise. Omissions caught after purchase cause requalification and modification costs, though structured assessment limits them.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which each backup differs from the current approved control state
rows.imp_rat: Backups diverging from the current approved control state undermine restore fidelity, but this is a governance/records concern that only manifests during recovery, giving it low relevance to day-to-day cartoning and moderate, containable severity.
rows.sat_rat: Backups are typically taken on irregular schedules, so they routinely lag the current approved control state. Reconciling a backup against the live control state is slow when done manually. Judging whether a backup matches the current approved state requires configuration-management discipline rarely present. Storage of frequent backups is cheap, so cost is not the limiting factor here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the technician calibrates against conflicting or obsolete target tolerances
rows.imp_rat: Calibrating against conflicting or obsolete tolerances is a documentation/procedural error peripheral to core cartoning function (relevance) with moderate downstream miscalibration risk (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled documents and revision management help, but obsolete specs, local copies, and version drift still let technicians occasionally calibrate to wrong targets. Confirming the current tolerance spec adds only brief lookup time. Selecting the correct target tolerance is a document-control matter more than a technical-skill one. Errors here waste rework but the control itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartoning-machine parts are damaged or displaced while trapped prior-run materials are removed
rows.imp_rat: Parts damaged while removing trapped materials is a clearance-handling concern (low-moderate relevance) with modest severity (machine damage, recoverable).
rows.sat_rat: Removal procedures limit damage but forced extraction sometimes displaces parts. Careful material removal takes some time. Removing trapped materials without damage needs skill. Damaged/displaced parts during removal are costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton jams while material flow is established for the next format
rows.imp_rat: Jams while establishing flow for the next format directly impede erection (moderate relevance) but cause recoverable stoppage (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Jam sensors and tuned handling reduce jams but they remain common while establishing flow. Jams during flow establishment directly extend setup time. Clearing and preventing jams needs mechanical skill. Jams scrap materials and cost downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons or sterile units damaged while flow is established for the next format
rows.imp_rat: Cartons/units damaged while establishing flow touches insertion handling (low-moderate relevance) with modest severity (waste plus possible integrity loss).
rows.sat_rat: Careful ramp-up limits damage but early-flow damage is routine. Damage during flow establishment adds recovery time. Avoiding damage needs skilled ramp-up. Damaged cartons and units in flow establishment are scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton or sterile-unit jams during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Jams during the production run directly interrupt erection/insertion (moderate relevance) but consequences are recoverable downtime (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature machines run with acceptable jam rates but jams remain a recurring production reality. Production jams cause stoppages, costing time. Rapid jam recovery depends on operator skill. Each jam scraps material and reduces throughput.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of adjustment cycles required before cartons erect and labeled sterile units insert correctly
rows.imp_rat: Excess adjustment cycles before correct insertion touches the job's setup (moderate relevance) but consequences are lost time only (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Repeatable setpoints reduce cycles but iterative fine-tuning remains the norm. Reducing adjustment cycles is fundamentally about shortening setup duration. Fewer cycles depend on operator skill to get settings right first time. Each extra cycle consumes trial materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of setting drift between setup acceptance and resumed cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Setting drift between acceptance and resumed cartoning is a moderate consistency concern (low-moderate relevance) with modest severity since usually re-verified.
rows.sat_rat: Locked setpoints limit drift but mechanical settling still shifts settings. Detecting drift between acceptance and resumption needs monitoring time. Recognizing setting drift requires experienced observation. Drift causes defects and scrap after resumption.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intermittent rejection failure remains undetected during preproduction verification
rows.imp_rat: Intermittent rejection failure undetected in verification is a QA-reliability gap (low-moderate relevance) with modest severity given later chances to catch.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent reject verification is weak; sporadic failures often evade preproduction checks. Detecting intermittent reject faults may require extended observation time. Spotting intermittent failures demands high diagnostic skill. Undetected reject failures allow costly escapes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required setup checks omitted before carton-format approval
rows.imp_rat: Required setup checks omitted before approval is a setup-completeness gap (low-moderate relevance) with modest severity if defects pass.
rows.sat_rat: Setup checklists are standard and effective, though occasional omissions occur under time pressure. Completing all checks before approval takes defined time. Low skill; depends on checklist discipline. Omitted checks risk expensive escapes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches with configuration records incomplete or inconsistent with packaged output
rows.imp_rat: Config records inconsistent with output is a batch-record integrity defect (low relevance to physical job) with moderate release/compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-record inconsistencies are a common cause of release delays despite review controls. Reconciling batch records with output adds review time at closeout. Consistent records depend on operator documentation discipline. Incomplete records delay release and can scrap batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unit-to-carton traceability records with missing or conflicting information
rows.imp_rat: Missing/conflicting unit-to-carton traceability records is a data-integrity defect (low relevance) with moderate severity for tracing.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability gaps and conflicts remain a recurring documentation problem. Fixing traceability records after the fact takes investigative time. Complete traceability requires disciplined data capture and understanding of the systems. Missing records risk batch rejection, a large cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoning intervals with missing or incomplete quality-state records
rows.imp_rat: Intervals with missing quality-state records is a record-completeness defect (low relevance) with moderate release severity.
rows.sat_rat: MES/electronic records reduce gaps but incomplete interval records still occur. Electronic records capture quality-state data with little added time. Depends on correct system use and operator entry discipline. Missing records cause costly disposition delays.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that short-lived packaging deviations remain hidden in summarized cartoning results
rows.imp_rat: Short-lived deviations hidden in summarized results is a data-resolution gap (low relevance) with moderate severity if a real excursion is masked.
rows.sat_rat: Summarized results routinely hide short-lived deviations, a chronic weakness. Short-lived deviations are typically averaged out of periodic summaries and missed. Detecting transients requires high-resolution monitoring skill rarely present. Hidden transients cause escapes with downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a detected packaging deviation cannot be linked to affected sterile units and the production interval
rows.imp_rat: Inability to link a deviation to affected units is a traceability gap (low relevance to physical cartoning) with moderate severity for recall scoping.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization helps but time-correlated linkage to production intervals is often incomplete. Linking deviations to affected units can require time-consuming record reconstruction. Requires investigator skill to correlate machine and unit data. Poor linkage widens scope of holds and increases cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected units lacking traceability to component lots, machine conditions, and interventions
rows.imp_rat: Units lacking traceability to lots/conditions is a records gap (low relevance to physical job) with moderate severity for investigations/recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Full traceability to component lots and machine conditions is frequently fragmented. Assembling lot/machine/intervention traceability is slow after the fact. Depends on analysts stitching disparate records together. Missing traceability broadens costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of deviation records with missing, inaccurate, or unsynchronized cartoning event data
rows.imp_rat: Deviation records with unsynchronized event data is a data-integrity gap (low relevance) with moderate investigation severity.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation records often have unsynchronized or inaccurate event data, a recurring issue. Synchronizing event data across systems can lag. Requires system integration expertise to keep data aligned. Unsynchronized data prolongs investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of released cartoning batches with incomplete compliance evidence
rows.imp_rat: Batches released with incomplete compliance evidence is a documentation defect (low relevance) with moderate release/audit severity.
rows.sat_rat: Batch record review catches most gaps but incomplete evidence still delays some releases. Compiling compliance evidence for release takes review time. Requires QA reviewer expertise to confirm completeness. Incomplete evidence delays release and adds cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of required packaging checks omitted or completed after affected cartons leave the packaging stage
rows.imp_rat: Required checks omitted or done after cartons leave is a QA-timing gap (low relevance to physical job) with moderate severity if defects escape.
rows.sat_rat: Procedural checks are mostly followed but omissions and late completion recur. Checks completed late after cartons leave the stage defeat their timing purpose. Depends on operator adherence to check schedules. Omitted or late checks widen scope and cost of any escape.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of packaging defect and rejection events omitted or misclassified in quality records
rows.imp_rat: Defect/rejection events misclassified in quality records is a data-integrity defect (low relevance) with moderate severity for quality oversight.
rows.sat_rat: Most events are logged but misclassification and omission persist. Recording defect events is contemporaneous with the run. Correct classification depends on operator/QA judgement. Misclassified events distort trending and cost control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time that cartoning capability deterioration remains undetected after output begins to drift
rows.imp_rat: Undetected capability deterioration after drift begins is moderately relevant to sustained quality with modest severity since drift is usually gradual/recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Drift detection latency is a recurring problem with limited continuous monitoring. Capability deterioration often runs undetected until sampling reveals drift. Recognizing gradual deterioration needs trending expertise. Undetected deterioration produces waste before correction.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartoning capability judgments are based on incomplete or inaccurate inspection data
rows.imp_rat: Capability judgments based on inaccurate inspection data is a data-quality gap (low relevance) with moderate severity if bad output is deemed acceptable.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection data completeness is inconsistent, undermining reliable capability judgments. Basing capability judgments on data assembly can be slow. Sound judgment depends on analyst expertise to spot incomplete inspection data. Poor judgments cause misdirected investment and waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of label verification records that are incomplete, altered, or unattributable
rows.imp_rat: Label-verification records incomplete/unattributable is a data-integrity defect (low relevance) with moderate severity for audit/compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Audit-trailed systems make records largely complete and attributable, with residual legacy gaps. Electronic verification records are captured automatically in near real time. Audit-trail systems reduce reliance on manual attribution. Unattributable records cause costly compliance findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that batch records can support inspector tracing of each packaged sterile unit
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that records support inspector tracing is a traceability-confidence issue (low relevance to physical job) with moderate audit severity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of particulate shedding from carton materials in controlled manufacturing areas
rows.imp_rat: Particulate shedding from carton materials is a contamination concern (moderate severity) but marginal to erection/insertion mechanics (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Qualified low-particulate materials make shedding a fairly well-controlled, predictable property. Particulate testing of carton materials is a standardized but time-bounded assessment. Material selection for low shedding relies on established cleanroom-materials knowledge. Low-shed materials cost modestly more but avoid contamination losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable packaging requirements omitted from compliance review
rows.imp_rat: Applicable requirements omitted from compliance review is a review-completeness gap (low relevance) with moderate compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Review checklists reduce omissions but coverage gaps persist across the many applicable requirements. Comprehensive compliance review across all applicable requirements is a long checklist exercise. Identifying every applicable requirement needs broad regulatory expertise. Omissions are cheap to prevent but expensive when found in audit.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the compliance assessment uses superseded requirements or labeling
rows.imp_rat: Compliance assessment using superseded requirements is a documentation-control error (low relevance to physical job) with moderate compliance severity.
rows.sat_rat: Version control exists but superseded documents still get used when updates lag or aren't propagated. Checking that requirements are current requires querying change systems, adding time. Recognizing superseded requirements demands familiarity with regulatory change history. Using superseded requirements is inexpensive to prevent but costly in rework and compliance risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compliance conclusions lacking traceable supporting evidence
rows.imp_rat: Compliance conclusions lacking traceable evidence is a documentation defect (low relevance) with moderate release/audit severity.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence is often incomplete or informally captured, leaving conclusions poorly supported. Attaching traceable evidence to each conclusion is a documentation burden that extends timelines. Structuring defensible evidence trails needs documentation discipline and QA skill. Weak evidence costs in audit remediation rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of integrity-compromising carton erection or labeled sterile unit insertion failure modes omitted from the assessment
rows.imp_rat: Integrity-compromising failure modes omitted from assessment is a risk-analysis gap (low relevance) with moderate severity if unassessed modes occur.
rows.sat_rat: FMEA practice is standard but always leaves some failure modes unidentified, so omission risk persists. Enumerating integrity-compromising failure modes upfront takes analysis time. Comprehensive FMEA of failure modes demands scarce cross-functional expertise. The analysis is labor cost but avoids far larger downstream losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that planned repetitions fail to expose intermittent cartoning faults and run-to-run variation
rows.imp_rat: Repetitions failing to expose intermittent faults is a verification-design gap (low-moderate relevance) with modest direct severity to production output.
rows.sat_rat: Repetition planning is often based on convention rather than power analysis, so intermittent faults commonly escape exposure. Planning enough repetitions to expose variation extends test duration considerably. Setting the right number and type of repetitions to surface run-to-run variation requires statistical and process expertise. Additional repetitions consume materials and machine hours, driving cost up.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that protocol scenarios fail to expose mismatches among labeled sterile units, cartons, labels, and inserts
rows.imp_rat: Scenarios failing to expose unit/carton/label mismatches is a verification-design gap (low relevance) with moderate severity if mix-ups escape.
rows.sat_rat: Vision and verification systems catch many mismatches, but scenario coverage for rare combinations remains incomplete. Designing scenarios to expose mismatches adds protocol development time. Anticipating mismatch scenarios among units, cartons, labels, and inserts requires experienced foresight. Extra scenario runs consume materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejected labeled sterile units retained in saleable carton aggregation records
rows.imp_rat: Rejected units retained in saleable aggregation records is a records-integrity defect (low relevance) with moderate severity for release accuracy.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregation update on reject works in normal flow but exception paths still leave records inconsistent. Removing rejected units from aggregation records is largely automated but reconciliation adds some time. Ensuring records reflect physical rejects relies on procedure discipline and system correctness. Rejected units left in saleable aggregates create serious compliance cost if shipped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of carton aggregates finalized before associated unit exceptions are resolved
rows.imp_rat: Aggregates finalized before exceptions resolved is a serialization-integrity defect (low relevance to physical job) with moderate severity for record accuracy.
rows.sat_rat: Sequencing controls exist but aggregates are still sometimes finalized before exceptions close. Holding aggregation open until exceptions resolve can slow finalization. Deciding when it is safe to finalize depends on operator understanding of exception status. Premature finalization creates rework of aggregation records, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that throughput figures are compared under non-equivalent carton, product, and sterile fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Comparing throughput under non-equivalent conditions is a benchmarking/assessment error (low relevance to operating the machine) whose consequence is a misleading spec rather than direct harm (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers quote under varied favorable conditions and there is no enforced normalization, so non-equivalent comparisons are a persistent hazard. Insisting on equivalent test conditions for throughput comparison slows the evaluation cadence. Detecting non-equivalent carton, product and sterile conditions behind quoted figures requires deep experience buyers often lack. Apples-to-oranges throughput comparisons lead to costly mis-selection that only surfaces later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that fit is concluded from incomplete or nonrepresentative product-and-carton evidence
rows.imp_rat: Concluding fit from incomplete or nonrepresentative evidence is an evaluation-process ambiguity, peripheral to running the cartoning job (low relevance) with modest, correctable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: There is no strong standard forcing representative evidence, so fit is frequently concluded from limited or idealized samples. Gathering representative product-and-carton evidence takes time that acquisition schedules often compress. Recognizing when evidence is nonrepresentative and insisting on proper trials is a judgement-heavy task requiring experienced evaluators. Concluding fit from thin evidence is cheap up front but expensive when it proves wrong post-purchase.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required carton-formation, filling, and sealing observations absent from the test-batch record
rows.imp_rat: Missing carton-formation, filling, and sealing observations in the test-batch record is a documentation-completeness ambiguity, peripheral to machine operation (low relevance) with correctable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Test-batch records are commonly incomplete because manual capture of formation, filling, and sealing observations is inconsistently enforced. Recording every required observation during test batches is tedious and often shortcut under time pressure. Knowing which observations are required and capturing them consistently depends on disciplined, experienced staff. Documentation labor is the main cost, which is minor but frequently under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of major installed components absent from baseline records
rows.imp_rat: Major installed components absent from baseline records is a primarily administrative/inventory error that does not stop the cartoner from running, so both relevance to the CFJ and severity of consequences are low.
rows.sat_rat: Major components are usually captured in as-built lists, though occasional omissions of substituted or field-added parts persist. Listing major installed components in baseline records is a bounded documentation task at commissioning. Recording major components is straightforward and needs limited specialized skill. The documentation effort is low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required packaging-material lot is unavailable when replenishment is needed
rows.imp_rat: Material lot unavailable at replenishment is a supply-timing delay (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (idle time, recoverable).
rows.sat_rat: Kanban/staging and MRP make required lots usually available, but late deliveries still cause gaps. Unavailable lots cause immediate line starvation and waiting. Availability is a planning function needing little executor skill. Starvation wastes labor and capacity while waiting for material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate at which configured settings move outside specified tolerances during setup trials
rows.imp_rat: Settings moving out of tolerance during setup trials is a setup-stability issue (low-moderate relevance) usually caught in trials (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Setpoint locks reduce drift but mechanical settling causes recurring out-of-tolerance moves. Settings drifting out during trials extend tuning time. Holding settings requires skilled locking and verification. Drift produces scrap during trials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of trial output rejected before material flow stabilizes for the next format
rows.imp_rat: Trial output rejected before flow stabilizes is a setup-waste concern (low-moderate relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Some trial rejection is unavoidable until flow stabilizes. Trial output rejection extends the stabilization period. Faster stabilization depends on operator skill. Trial output scrapped before stability is inherent setup waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of carton blanks scrapped after failing to erect squarely during setup trials
rows.imp_rat: Blanks scrapped for not erecting squarely in setup trials is a setup waste issue (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (material loss).
