rows.sat_rat: Forming tooling gives squareness once set but early trials produce scrap. Squareness trials add iterative time. Achieving square erection is a skilled adjustment. Scrapped non-square blanks are trial material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trial cartons scrapped after being crushed or torn during transfer and closure
rows.imp_rat: Trial cartons crushed/torn during transfer is a setup-waste concern (low-moderate relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Tuned handling reduces damage but trial-phase crushing persists. Transfer/closure trials add setup time. Preventing crush during transfer/closure needs tuning skill. Crushed/torn trial cartons are scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of additional material pieces consumed before unacceptable trial output is recognized
rows.imp_rat: Extra material consumed before recognizing bad trial output is a waste issue (low-moderate relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Inline feedback helps but delayed recognition still consumes extra pieces. Late recognition of bad output wastes trial time. Early recognition of unacceptable output needs skilled judgement. Extra material consumed before recognizing bad output is direct waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of successive adjustments causing previously acceptable setup conditions to become unacceptable
rows.imp_rat: Successive adjustments undoing prior acceptable conditions is a setup-inefficiency issue (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (lost time).
rows.sat_rat: Without systematic setup, adjustments frequently undo prior good conditions. Re-fixing regressed settings lengthens setup considerably. Avoiding interacting-adjustment regressions is highly skill-dependent. Regression wastes trial materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between challenge defects and specified nonconformities for the scheduled carton format
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between challenge defects and specified nonconformities is a verification-design detail (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Defined challenge sets exist but format-specific matching is sometimes imperfect. Matching challenge defects to spec adds preparation time. Selecting representative challenge defects needs knowledge. Wrong challenge samples waste effort and materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that obsolete, incomplete, or conflicting criteria are used to judge setup output
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete criteria to judge setup output is a documentation-control issue (low relevance) with modest severity for the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled acceptance criteria are largely current but conflicting versions occasionally used. Confirming criteria currency is a quick documentation check. Low skill; depends on document control discipline. Bad criteria cause wrong accept/reject decisions and waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning stoppage caused by an unresolved packaging configuration discrepancy
rows.imp_rat: Stoppage from unresolved config discrepancy is a delay issue (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (lost time).
rows.sat_rat: Discrepancy resolution is ad hoc and unpredictable in duration. Configuration-discrepancy stoppages often drag on while the issue is investigated. Resolving discrepancies requires experienced judgement to interpret specs. Stoppage time wastes capacity and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unplanned cartoning stoppages during scheduled campaigns
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned stoppages during campaigns is a delay/availability issue (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (recoverable time).
rows.sat_rat: Stoppages remain a chronic feature of cartoning lines despite preventive practice. Unplanned stoppages during campaigns still consume significant time. Minimizing them relies on maintenance and operator troubleshooting skill. Downtime is direct lost output and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which cartoning throughput falls below plan during active production
rows.imp_rat: Throughput below plan is a productivity concern (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (no harm to units).
rows.sat_rat: Throughput consistency is undermined by frequent minor disruptions. Throughput shortfalls against plan are common and hard to fully prevent. Sustaining plan output depends on operator skill and line balancing. Lost throughput is directly lost capacity value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration by which cartoning changeovers exceed their scheduled windows
rows.imp_rat: Changeovers exceeding scheduled windows is a delay/planning issue (low-moderate relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Window overruns are common and hard to predict. Changeovers routinely overrun their scheduled windows. Staying within windows requires practiced, well-coordinated crews. Overruns eat into productive time and drive overtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons held because physical pack identities conflict with serialization or aggregation records
rows.imp_rat: Cartons held for serialization/aggregation conflicts is a hold/rework administrative issue (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization systems create frequent reconciliation conflicts that are a persistent pain. Resolving serialization/aggregation holds consumes real investigation time. Reconciling physical packs with serialization records needs data-savvy personnel. Holds tie up inventory and labor, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cartoning batches missing their committed completion point
rows.imp_rat: Batches missing committed completion point is a scheduling/reliability concern (low-moderate relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Completion reliability is variable and a routine source of overseer stress. Missing committed completion points is a frequent schedule-adherence failure. Meeting commitments relies on the overseer's planning and expediting skill. Missed completions cascade into downstream cost and overtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned cartoning stoppages caused by machine faults during scheduled production
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned stoppages from machine faults is a reliability/delay concern (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (lost time).
rows.sat_rat: Machine faults are a recurring and imperfectly controlled disruption. Machine-fault downtime duration depends on maintenance response speed. Fast fault recovery requires skilled technicians who may not be immediately available. Fault downtime wastes capacity and incurs repair cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which cartoning throughput falls below the planned operating level during production runs
rows.imp_rat: Throughput below planned level during runs is a productivity concern (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (no unit harm).
rows.sat_rat: OEE tracking exists but sustained rate remains inconsistent. Falling below planned operating level recurs during runs despite monitoring. Maintaining rate needs skilled operation and quick minor-fault recovery. Below-plan operation wastes committed capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to complete packaging-configuration changeovers between scheduled batches
rows.imp_rat: Long changeover time is an efficiency concern (low-moderate relevance) with low severity (no product harm).
rows.sat_rat: Changeover duration varies widely and is a persistent scheduling challenge. Changeover time is a well-known bottleneck that consumes substantial scheduled time. Efficient changeover depends heavily on operator skill and SMED practice. Long changeovers waste capacity and labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the assigned root cause does not explain the observed packaging nonconformity pattern
rows.imp_rat: Assigned root cause not explaining the nonconformity is an investigation-quality issue (low relevance) with modest severity of recurrence risk.
rows.sat_rat: Root-cause accuracy is inconsistent, and mis-assigned causes recur. Correct root cause takes time to converge on. Assigning a correct root cause depends heavily on investigator skill and experience. Wrong root cause leads to recurrence and repeated cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between detection of the packaging nonconformity and initiation of the root-cause investigation
rows.imp_rat: Delay to start root-cause investigation is a response-timeliness issue (low relevance to physical cartoning) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems trigger investigations but initiation latency remains variable. Time from detection to investigation start depends on manual triage and can lag. Prompt initiation relies on QA responsiveness and staffing. Delayed investigation prolongs holds and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of evaluation criteria that cannot be objectively verified under intended fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Criteria that cannot be objectively verified is a protocol-quality issue (low relevance to physical output) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Some criteria remain subjective or unverifiable in situ, weakening consistent verification. Confirming each criterion is objectively verifiable under fill-finish conditions requires additional method development time. Designing verifiable criteria for real conditions needs measurement and process expertise. Unverifiable criteria cause downstream disputes rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of evaluation criteria lacking traceability to approved requirements and risk controls
rows.imp_rat: Evaluation criteria lacking traceability to requirements is an administrative/protocol gap (low relevance) with modest severity for the physical cartoning job.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability is frequently incomplete, leaving criteria unlinked to controls and hard to defend. Building traceability from criteria to approved requirements is a tedious linking task that consumes time. Maintaining traceability requires disciplined documentation skill and tooling familiarity. Poor traceability costs mostly in audit findings and rework rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that manufacturing tolerances are omitted from carton-interface trade-offs
rows.imp_rat: Manufacturing tolerances omitted from carton-interface trade-offs is a design-analysis gap (low relevance) with modest direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: Tolerances are sometimes overlooked in trade-offs, producing interface issues later. Incorporating manufacturing tolerances into interface trade-offs requires added analysis time. Tolerance-stack analysis at the carton-machine interface demands specialized engineering skill. Omitted tolerances cause jams and rework, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of expected production, handling, and environmental conditions omitted from validation
rows.imp_rat: Expected conditions omitted from validation is a coverage gap (low relevance) with modest direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: Validation plans miss some real-world conditions, leaving residual unvalidated scenarios. Enumerating all production, handling, and environmental conditions for validation is time-intensive. Identifying the full condition set requires broad process and distribution expertise. Omitted conditions cause field failures far costlier than fuller validation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of validation samples differing from production packaging configurations
rows.imp_rat: Validation samples differing from production configs is a validation-fidelity gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Protocols require representativeness but sample-to-production differences still slip in. Aligning validation samples to production configuration requires coordination that adds time. Ensuring representativeness needs validation expertise to spot subtle differences. Non-representative samples cause invalid validations and costly repeats.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which qualification conditions differ from expected variation in carton materials, labels, sterile units, and secondary configurations
rows.imp_rat: Qualification conditions differing from expected variation is a qualification-coverage gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification frequently uses a narrow slice of materials, leaving real-world variation under-represented. Covering expected material and configuration variation demands longer, multi-lot campaigns. Anticipating relevant variation in materials and units requires expert judgement about what matters. Sourcing varied lots and configurations for qualification is costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the intended cartoning operating envelope omitted from the validation protocols
rows.imp_rat: Operating envelope omitted from validation protocols is a validation-coverage gap (low relevance) with modest direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: Protocols routinely omit envelope corners, so coverage gaps that hide failures are a persistent, recognized weakness. Broadening protocols to cover the full envelope takes more planning and execution time. Defining the complete operating envelope requires deep process and equipment knowledge that is scarce. Full-envelope coverage multiplies test runs and material consumption.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of carton erection, labeled sterile unit insertion, and secondary-packaging requirements without a corresponding protocol test
rows.imp_rat: Requirements without a corresponding protocol test is a validation-coverage gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices are standard practice and, when maintained, catch most untested requirements, though gaps still slip through. Tracing every requirement to a test takes upfront traceability work that slows protocol development. Building requirement-to-test traceability matrices requires disciplined validation authoring skill. The mapping effort is labor cost but modest relative to test execution.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that interactions among carton formats, labeled sterile units, inserts, line speeds, and start-stop states are absent from protocol scenarios
rows.imp_rat: Interactions absent from protocol scenarios is a validation-design gap (low relevance) with modest direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: Interactions among formats, speeds, and start-stop states are commonly omitted, so combinatorial failure modes escape. Testing interactions among many factors multiplies runs and greatly extends duration. Designing interaction-aware test matrices requires advanced DOE expertise that is rare. Full-factorial interaction testing is expensive in materials and time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between diagnostic test conditions and intended sterile fill-finish production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between diagnostic and production conditions is a test-fidelity gap (low relevance) with modest direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic setups often diverge from real production conditions, so mismatch-driven false results remain common. Recreating production conditions for diagnostics takes setup time and coordination. Matching diagnostic conditions to fill-finish reality needs strong process knowledge. Replicating production conditions consumes sterile units and utilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that tested cartons, labels, inserts, or sterile units differ from the approved production configuration
rows.imp_rat: Tested items differing from approved production config is a validation-fidelity gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and configuration management are mature, so tested-vs-production mismatches are relatively uncommon. Verifying test articles match production config adds a checking step but little delay. Confirming configuration equivalence requires attention to specification detail. Configuration control is administrative cost, not a major driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of defined operating conditions not exercised during verification testing
rows.imp_rat: Operating conditions not exercised in verification is a test-coverage gap (low relevance) with modest direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: Time and cost pressures mean defined conditions are frequently skipped, leaving untested states that can fail in production. Exercising every defined condition lengthens verification testing. Enumerating and prioritizing conditions to test demands experienced protocol authors. Each additional condition tested adds material and run cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of intended carton, labeled sterile unit, insert, and secondary configuration combinations not represented in the evaluation evidence
rows.imp_rat: Config combinations not represented in evidence is a validation-coverage gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence typically covers only a fraction of possible combinations, leaving many configurations unproven. Representing all combinations in evidence lengthens the evaluation campaign. Choosing which combinations adequately represent the space needs statistical sampling skill. Each combination adds material and run cost, so coverage is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable traceability obligations omitted from serialization criteria
rows.imp_rat: Traceability obligations omitted from serialization criteria is a spec-completeness gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Well-known obligations are usually captured, but a share of applicable ones still get omitted. Mapping traceability obligations is a specification activity, not a time constraint. Comprehensive regulatory coverage requires specialist knowledge of varied market obligations. Missed obligations risk non-compliance penalties, a serious potential cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of in-scope secondary packaging configurations omitted from serialization criteria
rows.imp_rat: Configs omitted from serialization criteria is a spec-completeness gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Common configurations are captured reliably, but niche ones are periodically missed from serialization criteria. Enumerating in-scope configurations is upfront scoping work. Ensuring all configurations are covered requires thorough product-portfolio knowledge. An omitted configuration surfaces as costly late respecification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance thresholds for identifier quality and identity-association verification
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous acceptance thresholds for identifier quality is a criteria-definition issue (low relevance) with modest direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: Code-grade standards give partial objectivity, but association-verification acceptance often remains ambiguous. Setting acceptance thresholds is a one-time definition task, not a time bottleneck. Defining objective identifier-quality and association-verification thresholds requires specialist judgement that is inconsistently applied. Ambiguous thresholds cause disputes and rework, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required serialization data exchanges omitted from the cartoning specification
rows.imp_rat: Serialization data exchanges omitted from spec is a specification-completeness gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: EPCIS-style standards help, but specific required exchanges are still sometimes omitted from the cartoning spec. Identifying required data exchanges is done at design time without runtime delay. Knowing every required serialization exchange demands regulatory and integration expertise. Omitted exchanges cause integration failures and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of foreseeable rejection and exception scenarios without defined physical and aggregation dispositions
rows.imp_rat: Rejection/exception scenarios without defined dispositions is a procedure-completeness issue (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: A large fraction of exception scenarios lack pre-defined physical and aggregation dispositions, a well-known chronic gap. Missing dispositions for rejection/exception scenarios cause delays while people decide on the fly. Covering foreseeable scenarios requires broad anticipatory expertise seldom fully applied. Undefined dispositions produce scrap and holds, a recurring avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among quality, traceability, and production requirements governing rejection handling
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among quality/traceability/production requirements is a governance-clarity issue (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts among these requirements are common and rarely resolved by a standard reconciled ruleset. Resolving cross-functional conflicts over rejection handling consumes decision time. Reconciling quality, traceability, and production priorities needs senior cross-functional judgement. Unresolved conflicts cause inconsistent handling and waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required packaging levels omitted from the specified aggregation hierarchy
rows.imp_rat: Packaging levels omitted from aggregation hierarchy is a serialization-spec gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Level completeness is fairly well handled by templates, with omissions mainly on unusual hierarchies. Checking that all packaging levels are present is a quick specification review. Standard hierarchy templates reduce the skill needed to include all levels. An omitted level forces respecification, a contained occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting unit-per-carton values across packaging records used for configuration recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting unit-per-carton values across records is a data-consistency issue (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Master-data discipline is imperfect, so conflicting unit-per-carton values across records recur. Reconciling conflicting unit-per-carton values across records takes investigative time. Detecting and resolving record conflicts relies on careful cross-checking discipline. Acting on a wrong value causes scrap and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of partial-carton and removed-unit states lacking defined parent-child handling
rows.imp_rat: Partial-carton/removed-unit states without defined handling is an edge-case procedure gap (low relevance) with modest severity.
rows.sat_rat: Partial-carton and removed-unit parent-child handling is a chronic under-specified area with frequent inconsistent outcomes. Partial-carton and removed-unit handling, when undefined, forces slow ad hoc decisions during running. These edge states demand experienced judgement precisely because rules are commonly absent. Undefined handling causes scrap and quarantine of otherwise good material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified parent-child relationships lacking an objective verification condition
rows.imp_rat: Parent-child relationships lacking verification conditions is a serialization-spec gap (low relevance) with modest direct severity.
rows.sat_rat: Many specified relationships still lack objective verification conditions, a recurring specification gap. Adding objective verification conditions is a specification effort that does not affect runtime. Formulating testable verification conditions for each relationship demands rigorous specification skill often lacking. Unverifiable relationships lead to disputes and revalidation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that machine evaluations overlook available floor space, utility, and line-interface constraints
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking floor space and utility constraints during evaluation is a selection-phase error not central to running the cartoning job (low relevance); severity is moderate since it forces costly reselection, but ranking holds it below 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Established practice includes floor-plan and utility reviews, but ad hoc execution means constraints are still occasionally missed. Site surveys and constraint checks take time but are a routine part of the evaluation schedule. Layout and utility interface assessment relies on plant engineers whose availability and attention vary, leaving some constraints unexamined. Overlooked constraints translate into costly late modifications, though the check itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile fill-finish compatibility requirements omitted from machine evaluations
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile fill-finish compatibility requirements from machine evaluations is relevant to selection but peripheral to the cartoning machine's core running job; when it occurs, consequences are contained to evaluation rework. Capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile-specific checklists help, but some compatibility requirements are still omitted from machine evaluations. Ensuring every sterile fill-finish compatibility requirement is in the evaluation adds time to protocol design. Identifying the full sterile compatibility requirement set requires specialized aseptic and cleanability knowledge. Omitted requirements surface as costly rework in a sterile environment where remediation is expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of quoted line items based on incorrect sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.imp_rat: Quoting line items based on incorrect sterile fill-finish requirements is a pre-purchase procurement error peripheral to the machine's cartoning job (low relevance); its consequences are contained to rework of a quote rather than production harm, but capped at 2.5 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement miscommunication between buyer and supplier is common, so quoted line items frequently reflect the wrong sterile basis until caught in review. Correcting quotes built on wrong sterile requirements forces re-quotation cycles that lengthen acquisition. Specifying sterile fill-finish requirements correctly upfront depends on scarce specialist judgement that is often not fully transmitted to suppliers. Line items priced against incorrect requirements produce misleading budgets and later change-order costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate configurations documented without critical sterile fill-finish suitability information
rows.imp_rat: Candidate configurations lacking sterile fill-finish suitability info is a documentation completeness error, relevant to niche evaluations but low-severity and recoverable. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor documentation is often written for general packaging and omits critical sterile-suitability parameters, so this gap recurs consistently across candidates. Extracting sterile fill-finish suitability details from generic vendor datasheets requires repeated back-and-forth that delays candidate shortlisting. Recognizing which sterile-specific attributes are missing needs deep aseptic-process knowledge that general buyers seldom possess. Missing suitability information carries little upfront cost but drives expensive rework if a wrong machine is pursued.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incompatibilities between the candidate machinery and site space, utilities, and adjacent packaging stages
rows.imp_rat: Incompatibilities between candidate machinery and site space/utilities are a pre-install fit error, tangential to the core cartoning function (low relevance); severity moderate due to installation rework, but held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility checks are standard yet frequently incomplete for line-interface details, so incompatibilities still slip through with some regularity. Verifying compatibility with site space, utilities, and adjacent stages adds evaluation steps but is normally scheduled in. Detecting subtle incompatibilities with adjacent packaging stages requires integration know-how that is unevenly available. Incompatibilities found late trigger expensive retrofits, so the modest checking cost is well justified.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of drilling dust and debris released beyond the controlled work area
rows.imp_rat: Drilling dust escaping the work area is an installation-phase contamination concern that is peripheral to routine cartoning operation; severity moderate but occurrence is one-off. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Dust escape beyond the work area is a common, hard-to-contain problem where standard shrouding only partly succeeds. Erecting dust containment and cleaning after drilling extends the setup timeline noticeably. Effective containment relies on installers' diligence rather than deep expertise, so competent crews can manage it. Vacuum drill shrouds and barrier materials add modest but real consumable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of installation debris introduced into the sterile fill-finish environment while setting up the cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Installation debris in a sterile fill-finish environment risks contamination (relevance to product safety), but this occurs only at setup and the ranking caps severity below higher-priority items.
rows.sat_rat: Despite protocols, contamination excursions from installation debris remain a recurring risk that is hard to fully eliminate in practice. Cleanroom-compatible installation staging and cleaning add significant time to setup in fill-finish environments. Keeping installation debris out of a sterile zone demands trained aseptic-conscious technicians who are scarce and expensive. Temporary containment, gowning, and post-install cleaning consume real material and labor resources.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning-line unavailability caused by calibration after installation, format changeover, or scheduled maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Line unavailability from post-install or changeover calibration is a downtime error somewhat peripheral to the cartoning job (low relevance); severity moderate due to lost availability, but ranking caps it at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration outcomes are fairly consistent, but the duration remains stubbornly long and variable across format changes. Calibration downtime after installation or changeover is a well-known chronic drain on line availability with only partial mitigation. Fast calibration still leans heavily on experienced technicians whose availability constrains turnaround. Lost production hours during calibration downtime carry substantial opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required preventive-maintenance tasks omitted from the initial schedule
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required PM tasks from the initial schedule is a planning defect peripheral to immediate cartoning function (low relevance) that could contribute to later failures (moderate severity), but the ranking places it below the batch above.
rows.sat_rat: OEM manuals and templates guide PM schedules, but tasks are still routinely omitted from initial setups. Building the initial PM schedule is time-bounded but rushed schedules omit tasks. Complete PM schedules depend on maintenance planners' knowledge of OEM recommendations. Omitted PM tasks lead to failures whose remediation cost far exceeds the setup effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a maintenance due point is based on the wrong commissioning date or usage trigger
rows.imp_rat: Basing a maintenance-due point on the wrong commissioning date or usage trigger is a scheduling-data error that only indirectly affects cartoning output (low relevance); a mis-timed task can lead to premature or late service (moderate severity), still constrained under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS setup often carries over incorrect commissioning dates or triggers, making this a recurring reliability gap. Setting maintenance triggers is quick once the correct date or usage basis is known. Selecting the right trigger requires understanding of the asset's commissioning and usage that planners sometimes lack. Wrong triggers cause either premature or missed maintenance with associated cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of carton or insert stock being depleted before the scheduled run is complete
rows.imp_rat: Running out of carton or insert stock before run completion stops output (moderate relevance), but it is a plannable supply issue with low intrinsic consequence severity within this ranked batch.
rows.sat_rat: Low-level magazine sensors and reload alerts are mature and widely deployed, yet timing gaps and human response lags still cause occasional mid-run stock-outs. Low-level sensors and lookahead prompts give operators time to reload before depletion, though busy lines still hit surprise run-outs. Anticipating stock depletion depends on operator vigilance and material-planning experience that varies widely between shifts. Unplanned run-outs cause line stoppages and scrap, but the sensing hardware to avoid them is cheap and standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prior-SKU cartons or inserts left in the machine after changeover
rows.imp_rat: Prior-SKU cartons or inserts left after changeover risks mispackaging, yet detection and clearing are routine and consequences limited; relevance moderate, severity low. Bounded by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Documented line-clearance checks are the norm, yet reliance on manual inspection means stray blanks and inserts occasionally remain. Manually clearing prior-SKU cartons and inserts adds changeover time, especially in complex magazines and tracks. Knowing every hiding spot for stray cartons demands machine familiarity that not all operators possess. Leftover prior-SKU material causes mixed product and recalls, but the clearing itself consumes little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine unavailability caused by format-changeover complications
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from changeover complications reduces availability (moderate relevance), yet it is a planned-activity delay with recoverable impact (low severity), held low by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Tool-less change parts and recipe recall help newer machines, but changeover complications are still common and inconsistently resolved. Format changeovers remain a major source of downtime, with complications routinely extending planned changeover windows. Clean changeovers depend on skilled setters, and tribal knowledge gaps make complications frequent on less-experienced shifts. Extended changeover downtime is costly in lost throughput and sometimes scrapped setup cartons.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons damaged or rejected at equipment transfers because of mismatched timing
rows.imp_rat: Cartons damaged at transfers due to timing mismatch cause direct product loss (relevance to the packing job), and rejects carry cost, but the ranking caps consequences so severity stays modest.
rows.sat_rat: Servo-coordinated transfers greatly reduce timing-driven damage, but mismatches during speed transitions still cause some rejects. Transfer jams from timing mismatch cause short stoppages when they occur. Setting proper transfer timing at commissioning takes engineering skill more than operator skill. Cartons damaged at transfers are direct scrap, a recurring small loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machine desynchronization after connected equipment changes pace
rows.imp_rat: Desynchronization duration after speed changes impacts line flow (moderate relevance), but it is transient and recoverable (low severity), keeping importance under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Modern line control with electronic camming resynchronizes reliably, though legacy or poorly integrated lines still desync noticeably. When connected equipment changes pace, resynchronization is usually fast on integrated lines but can lag on loosely coupled ones. Line integration reduces the need for manual speed adjustment by operators. Brief desync causes minor throughput loss and occasional scrap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected cartoning pace conflicts with upstream or downstream capacity
rows.imp_rat: A cartoning pace conflicting with line capacity affects throughput coordination (moderate relevance), yet the mismatch is usually correctable with limited harm (low severity), and the ranking holds it low.
rows.sat_rat: Line-balancing and PLC coordination handle steady-state well, but pace conflicts still arise during changes or product changeovers. Mismatched pace shows up as brief starving or backups rather than a hard stop. Setting a compatible line speed requires understanding upstream/downstream capacity. Pace conflicts mainly cost throughput efficiency rather than material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of captured production data being lost before production reporting
rows.imp_rat: Losing captured production data before reporting harms record accuracy (moderate severity) but does not stop cartoning; the ceiling keeps proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: PLC historians and MES buffering make data loss uncommon, though gaps occur with network or storage failures on less-integrated lines. Automated data capture and logging happen continuously without adding operator time. Data retention requires little operator skill once the collection system is configured. Lost data rarely wastes material, though it can force costly re-runs of reporting effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning machine fails to reach the ready state during scheduled startup
rows.imp_rat: Failure to reach ready state at startup delays production (moderate relevance to uptime), but startups are retried and the consequence is delay rather than harm (low severity) under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Modern PLC startup interlocks and self-checks make successful startup common, but sensor faults and setup errors still cause intermittent no-ready conditions. Startup sequences with guided checks reduce time to ready, though failed startups can add significant diagnostic delay. Reaching ready state often requires an experienced operator or technician to interpret why the machine will not sequence up. Failed startups waste labor and delay the schedule but rarely consume material directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of materials from the previous run remaining in the cartoning area at startup
rows.imp_rat: Leftover materials from the prior run at startup is a real contamination concern but here it is a lower-ordered residual-check error with recoverable consequences. Kept within the 2.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance procedures are standard in regulated packaging, but they are manual and human oversight still lets residual material slip through. Clearance checks add time at startup but are usually brief when a checklist is followed. Detecting leftover material relies on operator diligence and a trained eye rather than automated verification. Missed leftover materials can cause mispacks or contamination that waste product and require rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of jog movement being blocked by an unmet machine-state prerequisite during troubleshooting or setup
rows.imp_rat: Jog movement blocked by an unmet state prerequisite during troubleshooting/setup is a peripheral usability friction (low relevance to core cartoning throughput), and its consequences are minor delays easily worked around (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: HMIs increasingly display the unmet condition, making the blockage more consistently understandable than in the past. A blocked jog during troubleshooting wastes time until the prerequisite is identified. Knowing which machine-state condition blocks the jog requires familiarity with the control logic, a real skill gap for less-experienced staff. The delay costs labor time but little material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of packaging debris or leaked product remaining in the machine after shutdown
rows.imp_rat: Packaging debris or leaked product after shutdown is a cleanliness concern that is routine to address with limited consequence; relevance moderate, severity low. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning SOPs exist but are manually executed with no automated verification, so completeness varies run to run. Post-shutdown cleaning is time-consuming and often compresses under schedule pressure, leaving debris behind. Thorough cleaning of debris and leaked product depends on operator conscientiousness and knowing all accumulation points. Residual debris and leaked product can foul the next run and force rework, wasting material and cleaning supplies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a rejected carton becomes mixed with acceptable output after removal
rows.imp_rat: Mixing a rejected carton back into acceptable output undermines the quality-assurance role of cartoning (relevance modest), and consequences can reach the customer, but the ranking caps this below higher batch items so severity stays low.
rows.sat_rat: Locked reject bins and diverter confirmation are mature practices that make reject commingling uncommon. Segregating rejects is a quick automated diversion needing little added time. Once reject bins/conveyors are set, minimal operator skill is needed to keep separation intact. A commingled reject reaching customers risks costly recalls, but incidents are rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time to identify a critical machine alarm among simultaneous notifications
rows.imp_rat: Time to identify a critical alarm among many is an alerting-latency error not central to cartoning production itself (low relevance); severity moderate because a missed critical alarm can escalate, but held at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm prioritization and shelving practices exist but poorly configured alarm systems still bury critical notifications among nuisance alarms. Modern HMIs prioritize and time-stamp alarms so operators can find the critical one faster than legacy stacks, though alarm floods still slow identification. Interpreting which simultaneous alarm is the true root cause still requires trained judgement rather than being obvious to any operator. Delayed identification of a critical alarm prolongs stoppage cost, only partly contained by current alarm management.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a scheduled maintenance window conflicting with sterile fill-finish production commitments
rows.imp_rat: A maintenance window conflicting with fill-finish commitments is a scheduling coordination error, not central to the machine's cartoning function (low relevance); severity is moderate since it can disrupt a production commitment, but capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Established shutdown-scheduling practice and production coordination meetings reduce most conflicts, but unplanned faults and shifting batch priorities still create recurring clashes. Maintenance windows are scheduled around production calendars via CMMS and shutdown planning, which usually finds a slot but not always without contention. Coordinating maintenance with sterile fill-finish commitments demands experienced planners who understand both production and validation constraints. Scheduling conflicts that force overtime or lost sterile batches are costly, and current planning only partially mitigates them.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required personnel, replacement parts, or machine access are unavailable during a scheduled maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Missing personnel, parts, or access during a scheduled window delays maintenance but consequences are schedule slippage rather than catastrophe; relevance and severity both low-moderate. Held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS scheduling coordinates resources reasonably well, but conflicting production demands still cause window shortfalls. Missing people or access wastes the booked maintenance window and pushes work later. Coordinating skilled staff, parts, and access needs planning competence that varies by site. A wasted window means paid labor and lost production, a notable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical spare part is unavailable when maintenance is required
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable critical spare during maintenance extends downtime but is a supply-logistics error with moderate, recoverable impact. Capped below prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Min/max spare policies exist but under-stocking of low-frequency critical parts remains a frequent recovery blocker. A missing critical spare stalls the whole repair while it is sourced, sometimes for days. Deciding which parts are truly critical requires reliability-engineering judgement many sites lack. Holding critical spares ties up capital, so stocking is often trimmed too far.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of adjusted settings shifting when drive assemblies are secured or returned to service
rows.imp_rat: Setting shifts when drives are secured cause minor quality drift that is re-adjustable; relevance to core running is modest and severity low. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Torque procedures and lock features reduce shift, but returning assemblies to service still occasionally moves adjusted settings. Re-checking settings after securing drives adds minor time to the task. Preventing setting shift on tightening relies on technique like counter-holding that experienced fitters apply. Shifted settings cause scrap and re-adjustment, a small recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of packaging components or batch materials remaining in the cartoning machine after line clearance
rows.imp_rat: Leftover packaging components or batch materials after line clearance is a real cross-contamination and mislabeling risk (severity moderate), and clean changeover is fairly central to cartoning; but the ranking caps this batch, so relevance and severity are held low.
rows.sat_rat: Formal line-clearance procedures with sign-off are highly mature in GMP environments and reliably catch residual materials. Line-clearance checks are a quick, routine step before machine removal in pharma packaging. Line clearance is a well-trained standard operator task requiring modest judgement. Leftover packaging components pose product mix-up and audit risk more than direct cost.

rows.source_job: Erase proprietary data from machine controller
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that proprietary recipes, credentials, or operational data remain recoverable after erasure
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable proprietary recipes or credentials after erasure is a data-security risk (moderate severity) but peripheral to the physical cartoning job; ceiling caps both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard, verifiable sanitization practice for legacy PLCs/HMIs, so recoverable data frequently remains after disposal. Thorough data sanitization of controllers is often skipped or rushed because it competes with removal schedules. Securely erasing proprietary recipes and credentials from industrial controllers requires OT-security skills rarely present on disposal crews. Failing to erase risks IP loss and breach liability, but the erasure effort itself is cheap.

rows.source_job: Drain residual fluids from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of lubricant, hydraulic oil, and adhesive residue remaining inside the cartoning machine after drainage
rows.imp_rat: Residual lubricant or adhesive after drainage is a maintenance-cleanliness concern moderately tied to product integrity, but the consequence is typically contained (low severity) within this ranked batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard drain-down procedures remove most fluid, but trapped adhesive and dead-legs routinely leave residue that is hard to fully eliminate. Draining lubricants, hydraulic oil and adhesive takes established but non-trivial time, especially for cured adhesive lines. Thorough flushing and clearing of residual adhesive requires technicians familiar with the machine's fluid paths. Residual fluids left inside become disposal liabilities and can contaminate scrap value, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a mismatch between candidate machinery electrical demand and available site utility capacity
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking an electrical-demand vs site-capacity mismatch is a procurement/utility planning error peripheral to cartoning operation (low relevance); severity moderate since it delays commissioning, but constrained by the ranking ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Utility demand data is usually documented and checkable, making mismatches comparatively rare relative to other gaps. Comparing machine electrical demand against site capacity is a quick check when nameplate data is provided. Electrical load assessment is a standard facilities-engineering competency generally on hand. An overlooked utility shortfall forces costly infrastructure upgrades, so the cheap verification has high leverage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering a footprint or service-clearance collision after candidate machinery selection
rows.imp_rat: Discovering a footprint or service-clearance collision after selection is a late-stage siting error with limited bearing on the cartoning job itself (low relevance); severity is moderate given rework cost, capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Where dimensioned drawings and layouts are used the risk is well controlled, though service-clearance surprises still occur when data is incomplete. Clearance and footprint verification is a defined pre-selection task that fits within normal timelines. CAD layout overlays and clearance checks are routine engineering skills widely available in acquisition teams. A late footprint collision is costly to remedy, making the modest upfront layout effort clearly worthwhile.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to detect a mismatch between candidate machinery throughput and existing packaging line product flow
rows.imp_rat: Failing to detect a throughput mismatch versus line flow affects planning relevance but its consequences surface later as tunable bottlenecks rather than catastrophic failure. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Line-balancing reviews catch many mismatches, but interactions with upstream/downstream flow are imperfectly modeled and sometimes missed. Detecting throughput-to-line-flow mismatch requires line-balancing analysis that extends evaluation time. Spotting a flow mismatch against existing line rates requires experienced line-balancing engineers. An undetected mismatch creates bottlenecks that waste capacity and money after installation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required carton, leaflet, product, and case configurations absent from the specified operating envelope
rows.imp_rat: Missing carton/leaflet/product/case configurations from the operating envelope can cause specification gaps, but this is a scoping error whose severity is moderate and recoverable. Ranking below prior batch caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration matrices in the URS usually capture most cases, yet occasional formats are left out of the specified envelope. Cataloguing all carton, leaflet, product and case configurations for the operating envelope is a detailed, time-consuming task. Defining a complete operating envelope needs packaging engineers who know the full configuration range. A configuration absent from the envelope means later change parts or re-spec costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of essential changeover, cleaning, inspection, maintenance, ergonomics, and safe-access conditions omitted from the machinery specification
rows.imp_rat: Omitting changeover/cleaning/access conditions from the spec is a documentation-completeness error, relevant to procurement but low in immediate severity. Bounded by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: These operability requirements are frequently under-specified because operations and maintenance voices are added late, so gaps recur. Specifying changeover, cleaning, inspection, maintenance and access conditions thoroughly adds significant spec-writing time. Anticipating all operational and ergonomic access needs requires experienced operations and maintenance input often underrepresented in buying teams. Omitted access or cleaning provisions cause costly retrofits and lost uptime later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of treating invalid or out-of-scope supplier certifications as qualifying for the proposed cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Treating invalid supplier certifications as qualifying is a verification error affecting selection quality, but downstream checks limit severity. Bounded by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier-qualification reviews usually validate certificates, but out-of-scope or expired certifications occasionally pass unchecked. Verifying certificate scope and validity is a modest-time step in supplier qualification. Judging whether a certification is in-scope and valid for the proposed machinery requires trained QA reviewers. Accepting an invalid certification is low-cost until it triggers costly compliance failure later.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning machinery assessment relies on superseded regulations or standards
rows.imp_rat: Relying on superseded standards in the assessment risks compliance gaps but is caught in review with recoverable consequences; relevance moderate, severity low. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory affairs functions track standard revisions reasonably well, so use of superseded standards is uncommon but not impossible. Checking current regulation and standard versions is a quick step within a documented review. Knowing which regulations or standards have been superseded requires ongoing regulatory-intelligence expertise. Relying on superseded standards can force costly rework, but this is relatively infrequent.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of candidate cartoning machinery configurations lacking documentation needed to verify compliance
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance-verification documentation on candidate configurations is a due-diligence gap, relevant to selection but low in immediate severity. Held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers routinely quote configurations with documentation gaps that only surface during later verification, so buyers frequently discover missing compliance evidence too late to correct cheaply. Assembling and cross-checking compliance documentation from multiple suppliers during acquisition is slow and stretches evaluation timelines considerably. Judging whether a configuration's documentation is complete enough to verify GMP compliance demands scarce regulatory and validation expertise that few procurement teams carry in-house. Chasing missing documentation adds administrative cost but rarely dominates the acquisition budget.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking concealed wear, damage, or incomplete refurbishment when assessing machinery condition
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking concealed wear or incomplete refurbishment risks later failures, a genuine reliability concern, but the ceiling forces low scores here.
rows.sat_rat: Concealed defects are inherently hard to surface and inspections are inconsistent, so this remains a chronic, poorly served risk. Thorough condition inspection of used or refurbished machinery is time-consuming and often compressed under deal pressure. Detecting concealed wear or incomplete refurbishment demands rare hands-on mechanical inspection expertise that most buyers lack. Undetected condition problems lead to major post-purchase repair and downtime costs.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which total acquisition cost is underestimated after including transport, installation, integration, qualification, parts, and downtime
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating total acquisition cost is a budgeting error with financial but not functional job consequences; relevance to running the machine is low. Held within cap.
rows.sat_rat: TCO practice exists but estimates are consistently optimistic, so cost surprises during the project are common. Building a full total-cost-of-ownership estimate takes time that acquisition schedules rarely allow fully. Estimating transport, install, integration, qualification, parts and downtime demands broad cost-engineering experience. Total acquisition cost is routinely underestimated because hidden qualification and downtime costs are hard to quantify and often excluded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of glue, tape, adhesive, or carton blanks being depleted before replenishment during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Depletion of glue or blanks mid-run halts cartoning (moderate relevance), yet replenishment is a manageable interruption with limited lasting harm (low severity) under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Low-level warnings and glue-level monitors are widely deployed and make surprise depletion fairly uncommon. Level sensors give advance warning so refills can be scheduled without unplanned delay. Monitoring consumable levels requires only basic attention with modern low-level alerts. Running out mid-run risks scrap and rework, but proactive refill mostly avoids that spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine stoppage caused by replenishing consumables during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Stoppage to replenish consumables reduces uptime (moderate relevance to the job), but such stops are routine and short (low severity), and this batch ranking holds it low.
rows.sat_rat: Many modern cartoners offer splice tables or low-level refill, but plenty still require a stop, so results vary by machine generation. Replenishing glue, tape or blanks often forces a brief stop unless the machine supports on-the-fly refill. Loading consumables is routine and needs little skill. The stoppage costs throughput but the consumables themselves are budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of carton blanks damaged or misoriented during replenishment
rows.imp_rat: Blanks damaged or misoriented during replenishment cause minor scrap and feed issues (moderate relevance), but the loss is small and correctable (low severity) under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Keyed magazines and orientation guides help, but manual replenishment remains error-prone and outcomes vary by operator. Careful manual loading into magazines slows replenishment, and a jam from a misfed blank costs additional stoppage time. Correct orientation and gentle handling of blanks depends heavily on operator technique, and untrained staff routinely nick or misload stock. Damaged or misoriented blanks become scrap and can trigger jams that waste further material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required lubrication points left untreated during maintenance
rows.imp_rat: Untreated lubrication points during maintenance can eventually cause wear, a moderate reliability concern, but the batch ranking forces low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Documented lube schedules and checklists make most points get treated, but manual rounds still miss awkward or undocumented points occasionally. Lubrication rounds are scheduled tasks that take predictable time when a checklist is followed. Following a lube-point checklist is a low-skill task, though knowing all hidden points requires familiarity with the machine. A missed lubrication point risks component wear costs, but consumable cost itself is minor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine maintenance history is unavailable during a compliance review or troubleshooting activity
rows.imp_rat: Missing maintenance history during a compliance review is largely administrative, so its centrality to actually cartoning product is low (relevance); consequences can touch compliance standing but are typically recoverable (moderate severity), and the ranking holds it below 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: GMP-driven CMMS and controlled logbooks make history reliably available in regulated cartoning environments, though paper-heavy sites still risk misfiling. Retrieving maintenance history during a review is fast when a CMMS or validated logbook is in place. Pulling history records is a low-skill lookup task in most systems. Unavailable history during audit risks findings, but the record systems themselves are already budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machinery unavailability caused by pneumatic servicing
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from pneumatic servicing reduces availability somewhat, a moderate relevance to throughput, but the batch ranking forces low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard pneumatic maintenance is well understood, so servicing rarely causes unexpected extended unavailability. Pneumatic servicing is generally quick with modular fittings, so unavailability from it is short and predictable. Replacing pneumatic components is a routine skill most maintenance techs hold. Downtime for pneumatic service has moderate cost, largely contained by planned scheduling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required control-data elements omitted from each backup
rows.imp_rat: Omitting control-data elements from backups risks recovery gaps, but rarely halts cartoning production; relevance and severity are minor and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Automated backup routines capture most data, but incomplete backup scoping and untested restores still leave gaps discovered only during recovery. Backups run quickly once configured, so time is rarely the limiting factor for capturing control data. Ensuring every required control-data element is included in a backup requires knowing the full configuration scope, a specialist concern. A backup missing critical elements only costs when recovery is needed, so day-to-day cost impact is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intermittent stoppage does not recur while the cartoning machinery is being observed
rows.imp_rat: The error of an intermittent stoppage not recurring during observation is a diagnostic annoyance, only peripherally central to the cartoning job (low relevance); when it occurs it delays troubleshooting but causes no irreversible harm (modest severity), and the batch ceiling caps it regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent faults notoriously fail to reproduce under observation, and current logging rarely captures enough transient detail to confirm root cause. Waiting for an intermittent stoppage to recur while observing can waste large, unpredictable amounts of technician time. Catching an intermittent fault demands an expert's patient observation and interpretation, a scarce skill. Observation time and repeated interventions for elusive faults are costly with no guaranteed result.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning line idling while the fault source is diagnosed
rows.imp_rat: Line idling during fault diagnosis is a downtime-duration error somewhat tangential to the core packing outcome (low relevance); severity moderate given lost throughput, but the ranking limits it to 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Onboard diagnostics and alarm logs help but rarely pinpoint root cause reliably, leaving diagnosis inconsistent between events and technicians. Diagnosing the fault source on an idled cartoner still relies heavily on technician trial-and-error, so idle time varies widely and is often long. Fast diagnosis depends on scarce senior technicians who know the machine's failure patterns, a heavy skill dependency today. Every minute of line idling burns production capacity, and slow diagnosis directly inflates downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of machine surface, conveyor, and product contact zone area with residue remaining after cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residue remaining after cleaning has contamination and quality implications (moderate severity) and cleaning is relevant to changeover, but the ceiling holds it to low scores.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning SOPs and visual/swab checks catch most residue, yet manual cleaning still leaves inconsistent residue in hard-to-reach zones. Cleaning contact zones follows defined SOPs with allotted time, though thorough residue removal can extend the window. Effective cleaning follows a procedure but consistent residue-free results depend on operator diligence. Cleaning consumes labor and cleaning agents but is a routine, budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine components damaged during daily cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Components damaged during daily cleaning cause maintenance cost and minor downtime, a low-severity operational error peripheral to core output. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning SOPs and approved tools reduce damage, but manual daily cleaning still causes occasional harm. Careful cleaning to avoid damage adds modest time to daily work. Avoiding component damage during cleaning depends on operator care and product knowledge. Damaged components during cleaning generate recurring replacement cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator injury while cleaning machine surfaces and conveyors
rows.imp_rat: Operator injury while cleaning carries genuine severity yet is ancillary to the core packaging function and ranks below the prior batch. Held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO, guarding, and cleaning SOPs are mature and widely enforced, making injuries during cleaning uncommon. Lockout and safe-cleaning steps add minor time to the daily routine. Injury avoidance depends on established safety training rather than specialist skill. Injuries incur significant cost but mature safety practice keeps them rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator contact with hot adhesive during daily cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Operator contact with hot adhesive during cleaning is a safety concern with real severity, but it is peripheral to the cartoning job itself and lower-ordered here. Capped at 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Standard lockout, cool-down steps, gloves, and guarding make hot-adhesive contact uncommon in disciplined shops. Waiting for adhesive to cool before cleaning adds a small delay to the routine. Avoiding hot-glue contact relies on trained handling and PPE habits that are well established. Burn incidents carry injury and downtime cost but are relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of adhesive deposits remaining on nozzles and applicator heads after daily cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Adhesive deposits left on nozzles after cleaning can cause carton-sealing defects (moderate severity) and sealing is core, yet the batch ranking caps both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual daily cleaning removes most deposits, but hardened adhesive in nozzles recurs and is inconsistently cleared between technicians. Daily adhesive-head cleaning is a short scheduled task within the shift routine. Cleaning nozzles and applicator heads properly requires knowing how to avoid damaging fine components, a moderate skill need. Residual adhesive causes clogging and defect costs, partly offset by preventive cleaning consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatibility among the installed machine controller, PLC, and HMI versions
rows.imp_rat: Controller/PLC/HMI version incompatibility is a configuration error tangential to the moment-to-moment cartoning job (low relevance); severity moderate since it can halt the machine, but held at 2.5 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: OEM compatibility matrices and change-control practices reduce mismatches, but ad hoc field updates still introduce incompatible version combinations. Verifying controller, PLC, and HMI version compatibility takes time during upgrades but is a bounded, planned activity. Confirming version compatibility requires controls-engineering expertise and OEM documentation not held by every site. Incompatibility that halts the line or forces rollback is expensive, and current version tracking only partly prevents it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machinery unavailability beyond the approved maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Cartoning unavailability beyond the approved maintenance window affects throughput and is somewhat relevant to keeping the line running (low-moderate relevance) with real production-loss consequences (moderate severity), yet it must stay at or below the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Even with planned windows, discovered defects and parts delays routinely cause unpredictable overruns. Overruns beyond the approved maintenance window are common when unexpected findings extend the job. Staying within the window depends on skilled techs estimating and executing accurately, which varies. Window overruns push into production time, incurring lost-output cost that current planning only partly controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that condition indicators misrepresent the machine's actual condition
rows.imp_rat: Misleading condition indicators can hide machine problems, moderately relevant to reliable operation, but the direct consequence is delayed maintenance rather than immediate failure, so severity is low within this capped batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor drift, poor calibration, and uncalibrated condition flags mean indicators fairly often misrepresent actual machine state. Chasing a misleading indicator wastes diagnostic time before the real fault is found. Distinguishing a false indicator from a true condition demands experienced troubleshooting judgement. Unnecessary part swaps prompted by bad indicators add avoidable material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine stoppage caused by carton jams during closing
rows.imp_rat: Stoppage from carton jams during closing directly disrupts the job, but ranking caps it below batch 2; consequences are recoverable downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Jam sensors and accessible closing sections limit stoppage duration, though jams recur. Carton jams during closing stop the machine, the downtime felt at once. Clearing closing jams is routine but faster with experience. Jams scrap the jammed cartons plus lost run time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cartoning interruptions caused by accumulated rejects
rows.imp_rat: Interruptions from accumulated rejects disrupt flow, relevant to continuity but ranking-capped; consequences are downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Reject bins with full sensors reduce accumulation stops, but they still occur when unattended. Reject accumulation causes interruptions that stop flow. Managing reject buildup needs attentiveness rather than skill. Interruptions from rejects waste run time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton-feed jams during erection
rows.imp_rat: Carton-feed jams during erection disrupt the core erection job, but the ranking caps this batch; consequences are minor stops.
rows.sat_rat: Vacuum feeders are mature but carton-quality variation still causes recurring feed jams. Feed jams cause frequent short stoppages that eat run time. Reducing feed jams depends on correct magazine and sucker setup by techs. Jams waste blanks and downtime, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of multiple carton blanks feeding into erection together
rows.imp_rat: Multiple blanks feeding together disrupts erection and risks defects, relevant but ranking-capped; consequences are rejects/stops.
rows.sat_rat: Double-blank detection is mature and fairly reliable, catching most multiple feeds. Double-blank feeds cause stops that cost time. Preventing double feeds depends on correct feeder setup by techs. Double feeds waste blanks and cause jams.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption caused by unit insertion jams
rows.imp_rat: Interruption from unit-insertion jams directly affects the insertion job, yet ranking-capped; consequences are downtime not catastrophe.
rows.sat_rat: Sensors detect insertion jams but clearing them without damage remains inconsistent. Insertion jams halt production and take variable time to clear. Clearing insertion jams while protecting units requires careful technique. Insertion jams scrap units and cartons and lose run time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of carton-to-unit misalignment at insertion
rows.imp_rat: Carton-to-unit misalignment at insertion touches the core insertion job, yet ranking-capped; consequences are rejects/rework.
rows.sat_rat: Servo timing and guides largely align insertion, but drift still produces recurring misalignment. Misalignment at insertion triggers stops that cost time. Alignment depends on precise setup of guides and timing by skilled staff. Misalignment damages units and cartons, a material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning interruption caused by unavailable sterile units
rows.imp_rat: Interruption from unavailable sterile units stalls insertion, relevant but ranking-capped; consequences are downtime not job failure.
rows.sat_rat: Buffer accumulation smooths upstream supply but sterile-unit gaps still stop the machine. Sterile-unit unavailability halts cartoning, felt immediately as waiting. Upstream supply coordination needs planning more than executor skill. Starvation idles the line, a labor and capacity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning interruption caused by unavailable cartons
rows.imp_rat: Interruption from unavailable cartons stalls the erection job, relevant to continuity but capped; consequences are downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Magazine capacity and staging usually prevent carton starvation, with occasional gaps. Carton-unavailability interruptions are directly felt as waiting time. Preventing carton starvation is a replenishment task needing little skill. Idle time wastes labor while cartons are fetched.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of minor stops disrupting carton flow
rows.imp_rat: Minor stops disrupting carton flow reduce throughput, relevant to continuity but capped; consequences are cumulative delay.
rows.sat_rat: Minor stops remain an endemic, poorly-solved feature of high-speed cartoning despite monitoring. Frequent minor stops are a chronic drain on effective run time. Reducing minor stops requires experienced fine-tuning of the machine. Minor stops erode throughput and labor efficiency.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement when applying cartoning acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement applying acceptance criteria is a judgment-consistency issue; moderate relevance, bounded consequences, ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Written criteria and samples help but subjective judgement still yields disagreement. Disagreements over acceptance criteria stall decisions and add time. Consistent application of acceptance criteria depends heavily on trained judgement that varies. Inconsistent decisions cause over- or under-rejection, wasting material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between the final acceptable carton of one run and approval of the next run
rows.imp_rat: Duration between last good carton and next-run approval is a changeover-timing issue relevant to throughput but capped; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Structured startup approval steps make the gap predictable, though it remains a routine delay. The changeover-to-approval gap is pure downtime the operator waits through. Faster approval needs experienced setup and QA coordination. Idle line time during approval is a labor and capacity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of collisions between sterile units during accumulation or insertion
rows.imp_rat: Collisions between sterile units during accumulation/insertion touch the insertion job and integrity, but ranking caps this batch; consequences bounded.
rows.sat_rat: Guides and controlled accumulation reduce collisions but speed variation still causes them. Unit collisions cause jams and stops that cost time. Preventing collisions depends on tuned accumulation and timing setup. Collisions damage units, a recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of packaging-material requirements not covered by allocated supply
rows.imp_rat: Requirements not covered by allocated supply risk starvation, relevant to continuity but ranking-capped; consequences are stoppage delay.
rows.sat_rat: Material planning systems usually cover requirements but shortfalls still occur, making coverage moderately reliable. Coverage shortfalls surface as delays only when supply runs out. Allocation is a planning task requiring little executor skill. Uncovered requirements mean short supply and wasted partial runs, a direct cost concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine starvation caused by late packaging-material replenishment
rows.imp_rat: Starvation from late replenishment stalls the job, relevant to continuity but ranking-capped; consequences are downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Low-level alarms and staging make timely replenishment fairly reliable, with occasional late refills. Late replenishment starvation is directly a waiting-time loss. Timely replenishment depends on operator attentiveness to levels. Starvation idles the machine, wasting labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of machine stoppages caused by incorrectly loaded replenishment materials
rows.imp_rat: Stoppages from incorrectly loaded replenishment materials disrupt runs, relevant but ranking-capped; consequences are downtime/rework.
rows.sat_rat: Loading guides and poka-yoke reduce mis-load stoppages, though operator loading errors persist. Incorrectly loaded materials stop the machine, costing time. Correct loading requires operator attention and correct technique. Mis-loads cause jams and wasted material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning downtime while locating and correcting the interrupting condition
rows.imp_rat: Downtime locating/correcting the interrupting condition affects continuity, relevant but ranking-capped; consequences are delay.
rows.sat_rat: Fault indicators help but many conditions still take variable time to find, keeping downtime inconsistent. Downtime locating and correcting the interrupting condition is the direct pain and often prolonged. Fast fault location depends heavily on operator troubleshooting experience. Extended downtime wastes labor and capacity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a misfeed, jam, or reject is attributed to the wrong initiating cause
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing misfeed/jam initiating cause risks recurring stoppages but is a diagnostic error; ranking caps it below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Misattribution of jam/reject causes is a common, persistent failure. Wrong-cause attribution prolongs troubleshooting. Correct cause attribution relies heavily on scarce experienced operators. Chasing wrong causes wastes materials and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of diagnostic adjustments that create additional misfeeds, jams, or rejects
rows.imp_rat: Diagnostic adjustments creating new jams/rejects directly disrupt runs, but as a recovery-side error the ranking caps it; consequences are added downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Trial-and-error diagnosis frequently introduces additional misfeeds. Diagnostic adjustments that create new faults lengthen recovery. Avoiding harmful diagnostic changes requires expert judgement. New induced faults waste materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of conforming cartons diverted as rejects during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Conforming cartons diverted as rejects is a yield-loss waste issue (low relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Reject thresholds cause a persistent baseline of good-carton rejection. Diverting conforming cartons costs little time. Minimizing over-rejection needs tuning skill. Good cartons diverted as rejects are direct yield loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeat cartoning-machine stoppages after restart with the initiating condition unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Repeat stoppages after restart with cause unresolved harm continuity, relevant but capped below batch 2; consequences are recurring downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved initiating conditions commonly cause repeat stoppages. Repeat stoppages after restart consume significant time. Preventing repeats depends on resolving root cause skillfully. Repeated stoppages waste throughput and materials.
