rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the restart hold while affected sterile units await disposition
rows.imp_rat: Restart-hold duration awaiting sterile-unit disposition affects timing; relevant but capped, consequences are delay and held stock.
rows.sat_rat: Disposition holds are managed but often prolonged by approval delays. The restart hold duration is the very quantity to be reduced. Speeding disposition depends on decision-maker availability more than operator skill. Extended holds tie up product and capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of conforming challenge cartons incorrectly rejected during preproduction verification
rows.imp_rat: Conforming challenge cartons incorrectly rejected in verification is a false-reject/waste issue (low relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Reject systems favor over-rejection, so false rejects of conforming challenges recur. False rejects during verification cause minimal time loss. Reducing false rejects needs skilled reject tuning. Incorrectly rejected conforming cartons are wasted good product.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which verification output fails to represent carton behavior under sustained production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Verification not representing sustained production behavior undermines confidence; moderate relevance, bounded consequences, ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Short verification often fails to represent sustained production behavior. Representative sustained-condition verification takes longer runs. Designing representative verification needs expertise. Non-representative verification risks costly production surprises.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that prior-run labels, cartons, leaflets, or sterile units remain before the next format starts
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that prior-run items remain before next format is a line-clearance/mix-up concern, relevant but ranking-capped; bounded consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty created by conflicting indications about whether the cartoning machine is clear
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting indications on machine-clear status can cause mix-up risk, relevant but ranking-capped; consequences bounded to re-clearance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about releasing full production when early carton results conflict
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about full-production release when early results conflict is a decision-risk issue; moderate relevance, capped consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required cartoning positions left unfilled for scheduled packaging runs
rows.imp_rat: Unfilled cartoning positions for scheduled runs affect ability to operate, relevant but ranking-capped; consequences are missed runs.
rows.sat_rat: Staffing gaps recur, especially across shifts and absences. Unfilled positions delay run starts until coverage is arranged. Filling positions with qualified staff depends on the qualified-labor pool being available. Unfilled positions can idle the line, wasting capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning delay caused by approved packaging components being unavailable when required
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable approved components stall cartoning, relevant to throughput but not job capability; consequences are delay, capped here.
rows.sat_rat: Just-in-time component supply frequently fails to align with need. Delays from components unavailable when required consume scheduled time. Largely a supply/planning function outside operator control. Delays waste line capacity and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the cartoning line waits for released sterile units from fill-finish
rows.imp_rat: Line waiting for released units from fill-finish is an upstream-dependency delay (low relevance to the machine's own job) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Upstream release timing is outside the line's control and often unreliable. Waiting for released sterile units from fill-finish is a frequent idle-time source. Little operator skill affects upstream release timing. Idle line waiting wastes labor and capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the scheduled cartoning campaign waits for required line clearance
rows.imp_rat: Campaign waiting for line clearance is a scheduling-delay issue relevant to throughput but ranking-capped; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Clearance timing is often unpredictable and depends on QA availability. Waiting for line clearance is a recurring campaign-start delay. Clearance execution follows procedure needing moderate skill. Waiting idles the line and staff.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning line idleness caused by unavailable sterile units or packaging materials
rows.imp_rat: Line idleness from unavailable components is a supply-timing waste issue (low relevance to the machine's own job) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Material availability gaps recur despite planning efforts. Idleness from unavailable units or materials is a recurring lost-time issue. Depends on material planning rather than line-operator skill. Idle capacity is wasted committed cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delayed cartoning starts caused by unresolved clearance, documentation, or approval requirements
rows.imp_rat: Delayed starts from unresolved clearance/approval affect timing, relevant but capped; consequences are schedule delay not job failure.
rows.sat_rat: Clearance and documentation readiness is frequently a start-up bottleneck. Delayed starts from unresolved clearance/approval requirements consume time. Depends on QA coordination more than line skill. Delayed starts waste scheduled capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of minor stops that interrupt active cartoning runs
rows.imp_rat: Minor stops interrupting active runs reduce throughput, relevant to job continuity but ranking-capped; consequences are cumulative delay.
rows.sat_rat: Minor stops are an endemic, poorly-eliminated feature of cartoning. Frequent minor stops accumulate into significant lost run time. Reducing minor stops needs skilled operators and good machine tuning. Cumulative minor stops erode capacity value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about the cartoning disruption cause before recovery is authorized
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about disruption cause before authorizing recovery affects safe restart; moderate relevance, bounded consequences, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Cause ambiguity is common and slows confident recovery. Diagnosing disruption cause before authorizing recovery takes time. Reducing uncertainty requires experienced diagnostic judgement. Uncertainty prolongs downtime and its cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the cartoning line remains stopped because conforming packaging components are unavailable
rows.imp_rat: Line stopped for unavailable conforming components is an availability delay (low relevance) with low severity (recoverable time).
rows.sat_rat: Component conformance gaps cause unpredictable stoppages. Stoppages for unavailable conforming components add unplanned wait time. Resolution depends on procurement and QC rather than line skill. Component-related idle time wastes capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recovery attempts that fail to restore stable cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Recovery attempts failing to restore stable cartoning prolong disruption; relevant but ranking-capped, consequences bounded to added downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery success is inconsistent, especially on complex faults. Failed recovery attempts extend downtime repeatedly. Stable recovery depends on troubleshooting expertise that varies by shift. Repeated failed recoveries waste labor and output.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same cartoning disruption recurring after production resumes
rows.imp_rat: Same disruption recurring after resumption harms continuity, relevant to the job but capped below batch 2; consequences are repeat downtime.
rows.sat_rat: Recurrence of the same fault is common because fixes are often temporary. Recurring disruptions repeatedly consume time until root cause is fixed. Preventing recurrence needs skilled root-cause analysis often lacking in the moment. Repeated disruptions multiply lost-output cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of scheduled packaging output lost while the disruption is resolved
rows.imp_rat: Scheduled output lost during disruption resolution is a throughput-loss consequence, relevant but capped; bounded financial impact.
rows.sat_rat: The amount lost varies with each event's severity and is hard to bound. Output lost during disruption resolution scales with resolution time. Limiting loss depends on quick skilled response. Lost scheduled output is a direct, largely unrecovered cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in expected carton erection, labeling, insertion, and integrity states
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in expected erection/labeling/insertion states affects verification clarity; moderate relevance, bounded consequences, capped here.
rows.sat_rat: Specifications reduce ambiguity but expected erection/labeling/integrity states still leave interpretive gaps. Clarifying expected states requires upfront specification time. Reducing ambiguity depends on clear specs authored by skilled staff. Ambiguous states cause disputes and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons outside quality targets after startup or changeover
rows.imp_rat: Cartons outside quality targets after changeover touches output quality but is ranking-capped; consequences are startup scrap.
rows.sat_rat: Startup and changeover reliably generate some out-of-target cartons before stabilization. Startup/changeover produces off-target cartons until the line stabilizes, costing time. Reducing startup rejects depends on technician setup skill. Startup scrap is a routine, accepted material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of recurrence of the same cartoning deviations across production runs
rows.imp_rat: Recurring same deviations across runs signal unresolved issues, relevant but ranking-capped; consequences are repeated rework not catastrophe.
rows.sat_rat: The same cartoning deviations recur across runs, indicating weak recurrence control. Recurrence prevention depends on slow CAPA cycles. Preventing recurrence depends on effective root-cause and CAPA expertise. Recurring deviations repeat scrap and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among approved cartoning specifications at production start
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting approved specs at start can misdirect setup, relevant to job correctness but ranking-capped; consequences are startup rework.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-start reviews catch most conflicts but some approved-spec conflicts reach the floor. Resolving spec conflicts at start delays production start. Requires QA/regulatory expertise to reconcile approved specs. Unresolved spec conflicts cause rework and costly rejects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of carton protective-strength degradation under expected environmental exposure
rows.imp_rat: Carton protective-strength degradation touches package integrity but is downstream/environmental; consequences bounded and ranking-capped here.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental testing makes degradation fairly predictable and manageable. Testing strength degradation under environmental exposure requires conditioning trials taking time. Predicting environmental strength loss uses established packaging-materials science. Degradation-driven failures cause losses; over-spec wastes material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in candidate scores caused by inconsistent interpretation of packaging criteria
rows.imp_rat: Score variation from inconsistent criteria interpretation is an evaluation-consistency issue (low relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Without strong calibration, candidate scores vary by interpreter, making results poorly reproducible. Reconciling divergent interpretations among evaluators consumes review cycles. Consistent interpretation depends on evaluator experience and calibration, which is uneven and hard to standardize. Inconsistent scoring wastes rework effort rather than direct material.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of lot-to-lot variation in carton crease behavior during erection and closure
rows.imp_rat: Lot-to-lot crease variation influences erection/closure reliability but sits below batch 2; consequences are increased minor stops rather than job failure.
rows.sat_rat: Material specs limit but don't eliminate lot-to-lot crease variation affecting erection consistency. Characterizing lot-to-lot crease behavior requires ongoing material testing time. Interpreting crease variation needs converting and materials expertise. Crease variation causes erection jams and scrap, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the carton configuration conflicts with cartoning-machine handling limits
rows.imp_rat: Carton-configuration/handling-limit conflicts relate to erection feasibility, but the ranking caps this below batch 2; consequences are contained setup rework rather than job failure.
rows.sat_rat: Design-for-machinability reviews catch many conflicts but some surface only at line trials. Checking carton design against machine handling limits requires machine trials that take time. Knowing machine handling limits needs equipment-specific expertise not always at hand during design. Conflicts cause downtime and scrap, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in packaging results across equivalent validation trials
rows.imp_rat: Variation across equivalent validation trials is a validation-repeatability concern (low relevance) with low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Some run-to-run variation persists despite controlled protocols, limiting reproducibility. Running repeated equivalent trials to gauge variation extends validation timelines. Interpreting trial-to-trial variation needs statistical competency. Excessive variation forces repeat trials, wasting materials and effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that package-integrity damage caused by cartoning emerges only after the assessment window
rows.imp_rat: Package-integrity damage emerging after the assessment window touches integrity but is a latent-detection concern; ranking-capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standard assessments rarely include aging/stability of cartoning-induced damage, so late-emerging failures are a real gap. Latent damage emerging after the assessment window is a delayed-detection problem. Predicting latent, time-delayed damage requires stability and materials expertise. Post-window failures cause expensive field consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty in attributing product-integrity compromise to carton erection or labeled sterile unit insertion
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty attributing integrity compromise to erection/insertion affects investigation quality, not the live insertion job; consequences are diagnostic ambiguity rather than immediate harm.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple concurrent handling steps make clean attribution genuinely difficult and uncertain. Root-cause attribution of integrity compromise is time-consuming investigation work. Attributing compromise to a specific cartoning step requires deep diagnostic expertise. Investigations consume labor and sample units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria lacking objective acceptance thresholds for carton erection, labeled sterile unit insertion, and package integrity
rows.imp_rat: Criteria lacking objective thresholds weaken acceptance decisions on erection/insertion/integrity but are capped here; consequences are subjective judgments.
rows.sat_rat: Many criteria still rely on subjective pass/fail language, leaving objective thresholds missing. Defining objective thresholds adds protocol development time. Setting defensible objective thresholds requires strong technical and statistical judgement. Threshold definition is mostly labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that qualification evidence no longer applies after changes to cartons, labels, sterile units, inserts, or secondary configurations
rows.imp_rat: Qualification evidence lapsing after changes affects requalification validity; moderate relevance, bounded consequences, ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Mature change-control practice generally flags when evidence lapses, though scope calls sometimes err. Re-validating after changes takes time but change-control triggers it predictably. Assessing whether evidence still applies needs experienced change-impact judgement. Re-qualification after changes is costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for carton erection, labeled sterile unit insertion, closure integrity, identification accuracy, and rejection behavior
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous acceptance criteria for erection/insertion/closure touch job definition but sit below batch 2; consequences are inconsistent disposition.
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous acceptance criteria remain common and directly cause inconsistent judgements. Clarifying ambiguous acceptance criteria upfront is a one-time protocol effort. Writing unambiguous, measurable criteria requires experienced validation authors. Criteria definition is low material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons that fail to erect to defined geometry during verification testing
rows.imp_rat: Cartons failing to erect to geometry in testing is core to the erection job, yet ranking-capped here; consequences bounded to test rejects.
rows.sat_rat: Carton erection to defined geometry is a well-solved core function with reliable, measurable outcomes. Checking carton erection geometry is a fast, routine inspection. Erection geometry inspection needs modest skill with gauges or vision. Inspection cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of labeled sterile units outside defined insertion-position criteria during verification testing
rows.imp_rat: Labeled units outside insertion-position criteria during testing directly reflects the insertion job, but as a verification-stage measure the ranking caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Insertion position is directly measurable and well-controlled on modern cartoners, so out-of-criteria units are relatively uncommon. Measuring insertion position across runs adds inspection time. Position measurement needs metrology setup and some skill. Inspection consumes units and time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in applying acceptance criteria to carton erection and labeled sterile unit insertion findings
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent application of acceptance criteria is a judgment-consistency issue; moderate relevance, bounded consequences, ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Different evaluators apply criteria differently, producing inconsistent findings. Inconsistent application forces re-reviews that add time. Consistency depends heavily on evaluator training and shared interpretation, which varies. Rework from inconsistency costs labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of labeled sterile-unit integrity being compromised during format setup or restart
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-unit integrity loss during setup/restart touches the core insertion job, but the ranking caps it here; consequences are moderated scrap or hold rather than uncontrolled release.
rows.sat_rat: Format setup and restart remain integrity-risk windows with inconsistent outcomes across operators. Protecting integrity during setup/restart adds careful, time-consuming handling. Avoiding integrity compromise at these moments leans heavily on operator care. Compromised units at setup are scrapped, a recurring material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile packaging production interruption caused by each format transition
rows.imp_rat: Format-transition interruption duration affects sterile-packaging throughput; relevant but capped, consequences are schedule loss.
rows.sat_rat: Changeover procedures exist but transition duration remains long and variable. Format transitions still cause substantial sterile-production interruption despite changeover practices. Efficient changeover depends on experienced operators, so duration varies with skill. Lost sterile production time during transitions is a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conforming labeled sterile units incorrectly classified as rejected
rows.imp_rat: Conforming units misclassified as rejects is a yield-loss/waste issue (low relevance) with low severity (recoverable material loss).
rows.sat_rat: Detection reliability is decent, but conservative thresholds routinely misclassify conforming units as rejects. False rejects are detected only through yield review, adding no immediate time but delaying recovery of good units. Tuning reject thresholds to avoid over-rejection needs experienced calibration. Good units scrapped as rejects are a direct, recurring material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical running, adjustment, and troubleshooting actions operators perform incorrectly during competency assessment
rows.imp_rat: Operators performing critical actions incorrectly during a competency assessment is a training-evaluation defect, not live production, so its relevance to the running job is indirect (low relevance) and consequences are contained to the assessment (low severity). Capped at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Operators still perform critical adjustment and troubleshooting actions incorrectly during assessment at meaningful rates. Competency assessments take dedicated time on and off the line. This need is fundamentally about building operator skill, and current on-the-job training only partially guarantees correct critical actions. Assessment and retraining consume trainer and operator hours.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled hands-on cartoning-machine training displaced by production demands
rows.imp_rat: Hands-on training displaced by production demands is a scheduling/support error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance), reducing operator competence over time (low-moderate severity), held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Because production consistently wins over training slots, this displacement is a persistent, poorly mitigated problem. Scheduled hands-on training is chronically displaced by production demands, directly cutting training duration. When training time is squeezed, operators never build the intended competency, so the skill deficit persists. Deferred training trades short-term production capture for later error and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts in instructions for running, adjusting, and troubleshooting the applicable cartoning-machine configuration
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts in running/adjusting/troubleshooting instructions is a documentation error peripheral to the cartoning function (low relevance) with mild, resolvable consequences (low severity), under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation for specific machine configurations is frequently inconsistent, and reconciling it is a recurring, poorly solved pain. Reconciling conflicting instructions consumes troubleshooting time when operators must decide which document to trust. Detecting and resolving instruction conflicts requires experienced judgement that not all operators have. Conflicting documentation causes rework and misadjustment with modest resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of priority cartoning-machine fault scenarios omitted from hands-on troubleshooting practice
rows.imp_rat: Omitting priority fault scenarios from troubleshooting practice weakens operator readiness, a training-support error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with moderate downstream downtime impact but capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Priority fault scenarios are frequently omitted from hands-on practice, leaving operators unprepared for real faults. Setting up hands-on fault scenarios takes scarce line time to arrange. Practicing priority fault scenarios directly builds troubleshooting skill, but comprehensive scenario coverage is rarely achieved. Simulating faults may require sacrificing product or downtime, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical machine settings accessible to personnel without authorization
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized access to critical settings is a security/governance concern that is peripheral to the mechanical cartoning job (low relevance) with contained consequences here (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access levels are a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably keeps critical parameters behind authorization, though misconfiguration still occasionally exposes settings. Setting up password-protected access levels adds modest configuration time during preparation but is a routine part of commissioning. Modern PLC/HMI platforms ship with built-in multi-tier access control, so little specialized skill is needed to lock critical settings behind authorization. Access-control features are bundled into standard control packages at no meaningful added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning a user to a role that conflicts with the user's approved responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Assigning a user to a conflicting role is an access-governance issue with compliance consequences but is peripheral to the physical cartoning job; both proxies stay low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Access-management workflows and approval gates reduce mismatched role assignments, though manual assignment still lets occasional conflicts through. Role assignment through user-management systems is quick once configured. Assigning correct roles requires an administrator who understands both responsibilities and system permissions. Role management is a low-cost administrative task within existing systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in role permissions across the combination packer, case packers, and wrap-arounds
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent role permissions across packing equipment is a governance detail with limited bearing on producing cartons; relevance and severity both minor.
rows.sat_rat: Different machines and vendors use different permission models, so inconsistency across the line is a common, weakly addressed issue. Harmonizing permissions across multiple machine types adds configuration time during preparation. Aligning role permissions across combination packers, case packers, and wrap-arounds requires cross-platform expertise that is scarce. Reconciling permission schemes is mostly labor with little material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine control accounts assigned to multiple personnel
rows.imp_rat: Shared machine control accounts weaken traceability and access control, an administrative/security matter peripheral to the cartoning job; low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Shared machine-control accounts remain common on the plant floor despite policy, so this need is only partly served in practice. Provisioning individual accounts takes slightly longer than sharing one during setup. Creating individual accounts needs only basic administrative skill. Individual named accounts add negligible cost.

rows.source_job: Calibrate sensors, registration, and closing mechanisms
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved standards or representative format materials are unavailable when calibration begins
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable standards or format materials at calibration start is a preparatory logistics gap, peripheral to running the cartoner (low relevance) with easily-absorbed delay consequences (low severity); it sits firmly under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-staging checklists help but availability gaps for approved standards still occur with some regularity. Chasing missing standards or format materials at calibration start can stall the process. Ensuring materials are staged is a planning task requiring coordination rather than technical skill. Missing reference standards force idle time and expedited procurement costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for accepted commissioning documentation needed to establish baseline records
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for accepted commissioning documentation to establish baselines is a one-time onboarding delay that does not affect ongoing cartoning (low relevance) and is recoverable (low severity). Held within 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Document approval workflows are consistent but slow, so the waiting duration remains a chronic issue. Waiting for accepted commissioning documentation is a well-known bottleneck that delays baseline record creation. Approval bottlenecks stem from process, not from any skill shortage in the preparers. Delayed baselines can hold up qualification and production, carrying schedule cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of installed components lacking identification or revision details
rows.imp_rat: Missing component identification or revision details hampers traceability but the machine can still run (low relevance), and the impact is a slower troubleshoot rather than harm (low severity). Bounded to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Asset tagging practices are established, yet components lacking full identification or revision detail still slip through commonly. Recording component IDs and revisions during install adds incremental time. Capturing identification details needs diligence more than expertise. Missing IDs cause costly delays later when sourcing spares, but capture itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the recorded component locations and interconnections
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in recorded component locations and interconnections slows service documentation but is peripheral to producing cartons (low relevance) with limited fallout (low severity). Bounded to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: As-built documentation is frequently ambiguous or outdated, leaving interconnection records a persistent weak spot. Documenting locations and interconnections clearly takes deliberate effort during handover. Producing unambiguous location records requires experienced documenters familiar with the layout. Ambiguous records cost troubleshooting labor later rather than material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting configuration entries across the commissioning report and asset register
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting entries across commissioning report and asset register are documentation inconsistencies with little bearing on cartoning output; low proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Manual double-entry between commissioning docs and asset registers frequently produces conflicting entries that go undetected. Cross-checking commissioning reports against the asset register is a slow manual reconciliation. Spotting configuration discrepancies demands attentive, knowledgeable reviewers. Manual reconciliation costs labor and downstream rework when errors propagate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of packaged product accumulated between the cartoning machine and downstream equipment
rows.imp_rat: Product accumulation between machines is a buffering/flow concern that is peripheral to the core cartoning function (low relevance) with easily absorbed consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Accumulation tables and buffer conveyors are standard and reliably absorb short mismatches on most lines. Accumulation is a buffering mechanism, not a delay, so it rarely costs time directly. Managing accumulation buffers needs modest operator attention. Excess accumulated product ties up work-in-process and risks handling damage, a modest cost concern.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of downtime events omitted from the production record during a cartoning run
rows.imp_rat: Downtime events omitted from the production record are a reporting-completeness issue with limited operational consequence; both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Where logging is manual, micro-stops and short stops are systematically missed, and automated capture is not yet universal. Manual downtime logging competes with running the line, so short events go unrecorded under time pressure. Capturing every event depends on operator diligence rather than automation, making omissions routine. Omitted downtime events distort OEE and hide costly losses but spend nothing directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a machine-state change and its entry in the production record
rows.imp_rat: Latency between a machine-state change and its record entry affects data timeliness only, a minor administrative concern; low relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Directly interfaced machines timestamp changes reliably, but many lines still rely on delayed manual entry, so latency varies widely. Automated state logging is near-instant, but manual entry practices introduce lag between the event and its record. Prompt recording depends on operator habit rather than skill where entry is manual. Recording latency itself wastes no material, only degrading the timeliness of reporting.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of interruption events assigned an unclear or incorrect cause category
rows.imp_rat: Miscategorizing interruption causes degrades analytics quality only; it is peripheral to running the machine and low-severity, so both proxies are small.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, dropdown-based cause coding is the norm and produces widely unreliable, vague, or wrong categorizations. Assigning causes is often deferred and done after the fact, adding little run-time delay but slowing analysis. Correct cause categorization depends heavily on operator experience and honesty, making it inconsistent and error-prone. Miscategorized downtime causes misdirected improvement spend but consumes little in the moment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which cartons selected for periodic inspection differ from discharged output across the production run
rows.imp_rat: Variability between inspected samples and discharged output weakens sampling validity, but it is a peripheral statistical concern to the core packing job (low relevance) with limited direct consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sampling plans exist but manual periodic pulls often fail to represent the full run's variation, leaving a real gap. Pulling representative periodic samples takes little time but manual selection can be inconsistent. Choosing a truly representative sample across the run depends on inspector discipline and understanding of sampling. Non-representative sampling risks quality escapes rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that cartoning-machine operating-status indications are unavailable or stale during an active production run
rows.imp_rat: Stale or unavailable status indications is a monitoring-freshness error tangential to physically forming cartons (low relevance) with modest, recoverable consequences (low severity), under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Modern HMIs and PLCs deliver reliable status most of the time, though comms hiccups and slow polling occasionally leave indications stale. HMI status refreshes are generally near-real-time, but network or sensor lags cause brief stale-indication windows. Interpreting whether an indication is stale takes some operator awareness but little specialized skill. Stale status rarely wastes material directly, its cost is mainly indirect through delayed reactions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among alarm states, output-quality observations, and throughput indications during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among alarm, quality, and throughput indications is a data-consistency error marginal to actual carton production (low relevance) with low, diagnosable consequences (low severity), within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Data from separate subsystems is poorly integrated on most machines, so conflicting indications are common and largely unmanaged. Reconciling disagreeing alarm, quality, and throughput signals costs operators time during troubleshooting. Resolving contradictory indications demands significant diagnostic expertise that is scarce on the floor. Acting on inconsistent data can cause bad-batch decisions that waste product, but the disagreement itself spends little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of false or nuisance alerts obscuring actionable cartoning-machine conditions during a production run
rows.imp_rat: False or nuisance alerts obscuring real conditions is an operator-attention error peripheral to the cartoning job (low relevance) with low, non-catastrophic consequences (low severity), well within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm flooding and poorly tuned thresholds are a chronic, largely unaddressed problem that obscures real conditions. Chasing nuisance alerts wastes operator time and can slow genuine fault response amid the noise. Distinguishing false alarms from actionable ones requires experienced judgement that varies by operator. Nuisance alerts drive unnecessary stops and lost throughput, though they rarely consume material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of purchase order fields inconsistent with the approved machinery configuration and commercial terms
rows.imp_rat: Purchase-order fields inconsistent with approved configuration is an administrative procurement error, peripheral to running cartons, with correctable consequences. Capped by ranking position.
rows.sat_rat: ERP templates and approval workflows catch many mismatches, yet configuration-to-PO discrepancies still slip through. PO creation is quick, but verifying each field against the approved configuration adds review cycles. Cross-checking commercial and technical PO fields needs someone fluent in both, which is a recurring staffing gap. Field errors cause change orders and rework whose administrative cost is real but bounded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved scope and acceptance-term discrepancies across the purchase documents
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved scope and acceptance-term discrepancies in purchase documents are contractual matters peripheral to the machine's function (low relevance), risking disputes but not operational harm (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Purchase document sets are commonly assembled from mismatched templates, so scope and acceptance discrepancies persist unless a rigorous review catches them. Reconciling scope and acceptance-term discrepancies across purchase documents requires multiple review rounds that extend closing. Spotting cross-document scope conflicts demands careful contract-review skill that is unevenly applied. Unresolved scope discrepancies become disputed change orders that raise final cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of submitted cartoning machinery specifications that conflict with the authorized configuration
rows.imp_rat: Submitted specifications conflicting with authorized configuration is a documentation-consistency error, only indirectly affecting cartoning output, and typically caught in review. Held at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Formal spec-review gates help, but a real percentage of submitted specs still conflict with the authorized configuration. Specification review against the authorized configuration is a defined step but often compressed near award. Spotting spec conflicts requires technical reviewers who understand the authorized cartoner configuration in detail. Undetected spec conflicts drive costly late redesigns and rejected equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of committed cartoning machinery spending above the authorized value
rows.imp_rat: Committed spending above authorized value is an administrative budgeting error with limited bearing on the machine's core cartoning function and modest financial consequences, held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: ERP-enforced spend limits and approval hierarchies are widely deployed and reliably block committing above authorized amounts. Purchase-order authorization limits are enforced quickly through standard sign-off systems. Enforcing spend against an authorized value is a routine controls task requiring little specialist skill. Authorization ceilings and PO controls directly prevent spending above the sanctioned value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree the demonstrated machinery setup differs from the intended packaging formats and production conditions
rows.imp_rat: A demonstration setup diverging from intended formats/conditions is an acceptance-testing fidelity error, moderately relevant to validating the CFJ but ranked below batch 3, so both proxies held at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance demonstrations are common practice but often run under idealized rather than production-representative conditions, so gaps between demo and reality persist. Arranging FAT-style demonstrations under representative formats and conditions takes scheduling effort and time. Designing a demonstration that truly mirrors intended production conditions requires seasoned process and packaging judgement. A demo that misses real conditions leads to costly post-installation surprises.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among diagnostic indicators used to assess the machine's condition
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting diagnostic indicators complicate troubleshooting but are a secondary diagnostic-support concern (low relevance) with recoverable impact (low severity), ranked below higher batches.
rows.sat_rat: Independent, uncorrelated diagnostic sources commonly disagree, leaving diagnosis dependent on interpretation. Resolving conflicting diagnostics extends the time to reach a confident diagnosis. Reconciling contradictory indicators is precisely where scarce expert judgement is relied upon today. Conflicting signals lead to trial-and-error part replacement that wastes money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of preventive maintenance tasks scheduled after the applicable service threshold is reached
rows.imp_rat: Scheduling PM tasks after the service threshold is a maintenance-timing administrative error only loosely tied to producing cartons (low relevance); its consequences are usually absorbable slippage (low severity), well under 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS due-date alerts help keep most PMs on time, but production pressure still pushes some past threshold. The core issue is PM tasks slipping past their due threshold, i.e., being done late. Scheduling adherence depends on planner discipline more than technical skill. Overdue PMs raise breakdown risk and its associated costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of preventive maintenance due dates calculated from inaccurate operating-hour, cycle-count, or component-condition data
rows.imp_rat: PM due dates from inaccurate operating-hour data is a maintenance-calculation error marginal to the cartoning job (low relevance) with mild, recoverable consequences (low severity), consistent with the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many PMs still run on calendar estimates rather than validated operating-hour or condition data, so due dates are often miscalculated. Bad interval data doesn't slow a task directly but drives mistimed scheduling. Setting accurate hour/cycle-based intervals requires engineering judgement often absent. Inaccurate triggers cause either premature part replacement or late-failure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine unavailability caused by poorly sequenced preventive maintenance tasks
rows.imp_rat: Poorly sequenced preventive maintenance causing downtime is a peripheral scheduling issue for cartoning; its consequences are modest lost uptime, capped low by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS job plans and standard PM procedures give reasonable sequencing for routine work, but complex overhauls still suffer from ad-hoc ordering. Poor task sequencing inflates downtime through idle waiting and rework of already-completed steps. Good sequencing depends on an experienced planner or lead who understands task dependencies. Wasted labour hours and extended line stoppage add cost when sequencing is poor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of stocked spare parts incompatible with the intended cartoning machine
rows.imp_rat: Stocking spare parts incompatible with the machine is an inventory-quality defect that can delay a repair but does not directly halt normal cartoning (low relevance) and is recoverable (low severity). Held within the 2.0 limit.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS BOMs and OEM part catalogs help, but machine revisions and undocumented modifications still let incompatible spares slip into stores. Discovering an incompatible part only at install adds a fresh procurement delay to the outage. Correct cross-referencing of part numbers to specific machine builds demands experienced storekeepers or engineers who are not always available. Wrongly stocked parts tie up capital and often become obsolete write-offs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a critical spare part remains below its required stock level after withdrawal
rows.imp_rat: Stockout duration for a critical spare after withdrawal is a supply-chain replenishment error peripheral to cartoning operation (low relevance); it may delay a repair (moderate severity) but is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated reorder triggers in modern CMMS reduce the risk, but manual withdrawals and lagging paperwork still create silent shortfalls. After a withdrawal, replenishment lead times mean critical parts can sit below reorder level for days or weeks. Setting appropriate reorder points requires analytical judgement that many maintenance stores lack. Faster replenishment or higher safety stock raises carrying and expediting costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of corrosion on critical machine surfaces during extended shutdown
rows.imp_rat: Corrosion during extended shutdown is a storage-preservation defect peripheral to active cartoning; consequences are repairable degradation, capped by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Rust preventives and covers are standard, but during extended shutdowns humidity control and coverage lapses still let corrosion appear. Corrosion prevention is applied before shutdown, so it adds little to active downtime. Effective preservation of critical surfaces requires knowledge of correct inhibitors and coverage that is not universally held. Preservation materials are cheap relative to the cost of a corroded machine after restart.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical machine surfaces or mechanisms omitted from preservation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting machine surfaces from preservation matters mainly at end-of-life storage, not core cartoning throughput; consequences are contained and recoverable. Held at or below 2.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without enforced preservation checklists, some surfaces or internal mechanisms are routinely missed, making omissions a recurring problem. Preservation work is done ahead of storage and does not extend operational downtime. Ensuring every critical surface and mechanism is covered depends on a thorough checklist and diligent execution that varies by person. Omitting preservation saves negligible short-term cost but risks expensive restoration later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of machine exposure to damaging storage conditions
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to damaging storage conditions is a preservation concern peripheral to active cartoning; consequences are recoverable degradation, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Best-practice guidance exists, but machines are frequently left in whatever space is available, so damaging exposure remains common. Moving or protecting a machine from bad storage conditions is a one-time effort that does not affect ongoing downtime. Choosing suitable storage environments requires some engineering judgement but is not highly specialized. Climate-controlled or protected storage carries facility cost that is often minimized, exposing machines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production interruption during drive-component adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Sterile fill-finish interruption during drive-component adjustment is tangential to the cartoning job itself and yields recoverable production loss, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented setup procedures exist, but achieving repeatable adjustment without breaching the sterile envelope remains difficult and variable. Drive-component adjustment on sterile fill-finish lines interrupts production and, given aseptic requirements, restart validation extends the outage considerably. Adjusting drives while protecting sterility demands highly trained technicians, who are scarce. Sterile-line downtime and any batch impact are extremely costly per hour.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required ownership, maintenance, and validation records missing at handover
rows.imp_rat: Missing ownership/maintenance/validation records at handover is a documentation gap peripheral to cartoning throughput, generally correctable. Held at or below 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Handover packages routinely omit some maintenance or validation records due to fragmented storage. Gathering ownership, maintenance, and validation records at handover can slow the transfer. Knowing the full required record set for a regulated cartoner takes documentation expertise. Missing validation records reduce resale value and may force revalidation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the intended recipient cannot accept or operate the cartoning machine because of space, utility, or capability constraints
rows.imp_rat: Recipient inability to accept the machine due to space/utility constraints is a transfer-logistics issue, not central to the cartoning job itself; effects are limited and detectable. Held under 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-transfer site checks are inconsistent, so capability mismatches are discovered too late fairly often. Verifying recipient capability upfront avoids late relocation delays. Assessing space, utility, and capability fit requires engineering evaluation the recipient may not perform. A machine that can't be accepted incurs return shipping and storage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required ownership, decontamination, or transfer records missing at handoff
rows.imp_rat: Missing ownership/decontamination/transfer records at handoff is a records-completeness issue at transfer, peripheral to producing cartons, with recoverable consequences. Ceiling enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Document packages at handoff are frequently incomplete because no single owner controls the set. Assembling ownership, decontamination, and transfer records at handoff adds delay when files are scattered. Coordinating cross-functional records demands administrative diligence often under-resourced at disposal. Missing records delay transfer and may require re-doing work, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage sustained by the cartoning machine during removal or transport to the recipient
rows.imp_rat: Damage during removal or transport is a decommissioning/logistics error peripheral to the cartoning CFJ (low relevance); it can harm asset value (moderate severity) but is ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Established rigging and packaging practice keeps damage uncommon, though incidents still occur with awkward or delicate assemblies. Careful rigging and crating for transport adds time but is standard for machinery moves. Protecting the machine during removal relies on competent riggers, a widely available skill. Transport damage erodes resale or transfer value, a meaningful cost incentive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of machine damage during disconnection, loading, or return transit
rows.imp_rat: Machine damage during disconnection/loading/return transit is an end-of-life logistics error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance); it can degrade the asset (moderate severity) but ranks below batch 3.
rows.sat_rat: Mature handling and securement practice makes serious transit damage relatively rare but not eliminated. Protective disconnection, loading and securing procedures add manageable time. Standard riggers and drivers cover this with common competencies. Damage in transit reduces recovered value and may incur claims, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish packaging disruption while the machine is removed for return
rows.imp_rat: Fill-finish disruption during machine return is a removal-logistics delay, peripheral to the cartoning job and typically planned/absorbable. Held under 2.0 per batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: With no ready backup, disruption to sterile packaging during machine removal is prolonged and hard to bound. Sterile fill-finish lines cannot easily absorb the downtime when a cartoner is removed for return, and interim capacity is scarce. Managing removal without breaching sterile-area constraints needs specialized aseptic and validation expertise. Sterile-line downtime is extremely costly per hour.

rows.source_job: Isolate energy sources on the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine energy feeds left connected because their isolation points were not identified
rows.imp_rat: Leaving energy feeds connected due to unidentified isolation points is a safety concern, but within the cartoning CFJ it is a peripheral shutdown/service error (low relevance) and though consequences could be serious, ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO energy-isolation procedures are mature, but incomplete drawings and hidden feeds still leave the occasional connection unidentified. Locating every energy isolation point can be slow when documentation is outdated. Identifying all feeds relies on skilled techs interpreting drawings and the physical machine, a real dependency. A missed live feed risks safety incidents and rework, with limited direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required controller access is unavailable before decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable controller access before decommissioning (relevance ~2) is a narrow procedural prerequisite in the transition phase, not the operating job; severity (~2) capped, as delays are recoverable rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Access is usually available but lost passwords or expired vendor support still leave controllers inaccessible at decommissioning. Missing controller access blocks decommissioning until credentials or vendor support are obtained, adding delay. Regaining controller access needs specific PLC/HMI expertise and vendor relationships. Locked-out controllers may require paid vendor intervention.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of production or decommissioning disruption caused by data extraction
rows.imp_rat: Minimizing data-extraction disruption during decommissioning is peripheral to the core job of cartoning production; severity is low as such delays are typically absorbable. Ranking caps this below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Planned extraction windows generally succeed but occasional access issues extend disruption. Data extraction can be scheduled during planned downtime to minimize disruption. Extracting controller and historian data needs a technician familiar with the specific systems. Extended extraction ties up lines and staff, adding modest cost.

rows.source_job: Drain residual fluids from the machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of lubricants, hydraulic oil, and adhesive residue being combined into an incompatible waste stream
rows.imp_rat: Combining lubricants, hydraulic oil, and adhesive into an incompatible waste stream is a waste-handling error not part of the cartoning function (very low relevance); severity moderate for reaction/compliance risk, but limited to 2.5.
rows.sat_rat: Established container-and-label practice usually keeps streams distinct, though shared drain pans and haste occasionally cause commingling. Keeping oil, hydraulic fluid and adhesive in separate containers costs little extra time under normal drainage. Knowing which of these fluids are chemically incompatible needs basic hazmat training that most disposal crews possess. Combined incompatible fluids often become higher-cost regulated waste, so separation saves money.

rows.source_job: Recover reusable components from the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of recovered components contaminated by product, cleaning-agent, or lubricant residues
rows.imp_rat: Residue contamination of recovered components is a decommissioning-hygiene concern marginal to core cartoning throughput; consequences are containable, capped low per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Validated cleaning protocols exist in regulated plants but residual contamination in hard-to-reach areas still occurs. Achieving residue-free recovery adds cleaning cycles that extend the decommissioning timeline. Effective residue removal from complex cartoner geometries requires trained cleaning technicians. Contaminated components lose salvage value and may require disposal as waste.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production interruption between retirement of the former cartoning machine and stable output from the replacement line
rows.imp_rat: Production interruption during changeover (relevance ~2 under the ceiling) touches output continuity but is a one-time transition event, not the ongoing cartoning job; severity (~2) is capped though real downtime costs exist, ranked below prior batch items.
rows.sat_rat: Even with planning, replacement lines rarely reach stable output immediately, so interruption persists. The gap between retiring the old line and stable replacement output is a well-known but hard-to-shrink downtime. Ramping a replacement to stable output needs experienced commissioning engineers. Extended interruption means lost production revenue.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartons produced during output validation fail approved packaging specifications
rows.imp_rat: Cartons failing spec during output validation is nearer the core job but bounded to the validation stage; caught before production, so consequences are limited. Capped by ranking at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validation protocols catch failures, but initial-run spec misses during commissioning are still common. Failed validation cartons force reruns that extend the qualification window. Achieving spec-compliant output requires skilled setup and adjustment technicians. Failed cartons waste materials and consume rework time.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned stoppages caused by mismatches between the replacement cartoning line and adjacent production stages
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned stoppages from line-mismatch (relevance ~2 under ceiling) affects throughput but is an integration issue rather than the intrinsic cartoning function; severity (~2) capped despite genuine downtime impact, as ranking places it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Integration testing helps but adjacent-stage mismatches still trigger recurring unplanned stoppages after changeover. Interface mismatches cause repeated stoppages that extend the ramp period. Diagnosing and resolving line-integration mismatches requires cross-stage integration expertise. Unplanned stoppages waste throughput and incur troubleshooting cost.

rows.source_job: Transition production to the replacement machine
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved packaging materials are unavailable when output validation begins
rows.imp_rat: Approved packaging materials unavailable at validation start is a scheduling error affecting a validation step, not the ongoing cartoning function, and is recoverable. Held at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Material staging practices are established, though occasional supply gaps still stall validation. Approved packaging materials can usually be staged in advance of output validation. Procuring correct approved materials requires materials-management coordination that is routine. Unavailable materials idle validation crews, wasting labor.

rows.source_job: Disconnect the machine from facility utilities
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, water, or data connections left attached after disconnection work
rows.imp_rat: Connections left attached after disconnection work is a decommissioning safety error peripheral to the cartoning job (low relevance); could cause equipment damage or hazard (moderate severity) but capped by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and walkdowns catch most connections, but a stray line is still occasionally missed before the machine is moved. Disconnecting all utility and data connections is routine work that adds predictable time. Standard techs handle multi-utility disconnection, though data lines occasionally need specialist attention. A leftover attached connection can cause damage on lift, a moderate cost risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of condition-critical maintenance and decommissioning records that are missing or incomplete
rows.imp_rat: Missing condition-critical maintenance/decommissioning records affects verification, not live cartoning output; consequences are contained and correctable. Ceiling enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Condition-critical decommissioning records are often missing or incomplete because capture wasn't enforced during service life. Filling gaps in condition-critical records at disposal takes time that is rarely budgeted. Judging which condition records are critical requires maintenance-engineering knowledge. Missing condition data undercuts salvage decisions and warranty/liability positions.

rows.stk: Environmental Health and Safety Specialist / Maintenance Technician
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among regulatory, safety, and contractual cleanliness criteria before decontamination begins
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting cleanliness criteria before decontamination is a documentation-alignment concern peripheral to core cartoning throughput; consequences are procedural delays, capped low per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard framework reconciling regulatory, safety, and contractual cleanliness demands, so conflicts routinely surface late. Reconciling conflicting cleanliness criteria stalls the start of decontamination while parties negotiate. It takes regulatory and contractual expertise to spot and harmonize divergent criteria, a scarce combined skill set. Unresolved conflicts force rework or over-cleaning, wasting agents and labor.

rows.stk: Environmental Health and Safety Specialist / Maintenance Technician
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required decontamination records that are incomplete at transfer approval
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete decontamination records at transfer approval is a records-completeness defect peripheral to cartoning operation, with recoverable compliance consequences, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists exist but gaps at transfer approval remain common because record capture is manual and inconsistent. Chasing down missing decontamination records at transfer approval delays handoff. Compiling complete records needs disciplined documentation practice that decommissioning crews often lack. Incomplete records can force re-decontamination or hold shipments, adding cost.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulated waste removed from the cartoning machine is routed to an unauthorized disposal or treatment channel
rows.imp_rat: Routing regulated waste to an unauthorized channel is a compliance/disposal error peripheral to producing cartons (very low relevance); severity moderate given regulatory exposure, but held under the 2.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manifested hazardous-waste tracking and licensed haulers are a mature system that reliably prevents misrouting in well-run plants. Routing regulated waste through licensed channels follows a set manifest process that does not materially slow disposal. Selecting authorized treatment channels leans on EHS/waste-broker expertise that regulated facilities routinely retain. Proper channeling avoids fines and cleanup liability far exceeding the modest handling cost.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible waste streams from the cartoning machine being mixed during segregation or storage
rows.imp_rat: Mixing incompatible waste streams is a downstream disposal error largely disconnected from the cartoning job (very low relevance); severity moderate for safety/compliance risk, but capped at 2.5 by the batch order.
rows.sat_rat: Labeled segregated containers and hazardous-waste protocols are mature and generally prevent mixing, with occasional lapses under time pressure. Segregating incompatible waste streams adds sorting time that is well absorbed into normal decommissioning workflow. Correct segregation relies on trained EHS staff reading SDS and compatibility charts, a common competency in regulated plants. Mixing incompatibles can trigger costly reclassification of the whole container, an incentive that keeps practice disciplined.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of materials removed from the cartoning machine that are assigned an incorrect waste classification
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect waste classification of removed materials is a disposal-administration error disconnected from the cartoning job (very low relevance); severity moderate for regulatory consequences, but capped at 2.5 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Classification guidance exists but ambiguous or mixed materials from a machine still produce a real rate of misclassification. Classifying each removed material takes review time but is embedded in the waste-manifest workflow. Accurate waste classification demands specialist knowledge of codes and thresholds, a genuine skill bottleneck. Misclassification either overspends on disposal or risks fines, creating cost exposure both ways.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier-selected references omit unfavorable experiences with machinery reliability, delivery, or after-sales support
rows.imp_rat: Omission of unfavorable references (relevance ~2) is an administrative due-diligence gap far from the machine's core cartoning function; severity (~2) is low because it only mildly biases purchase decisions and is correctable through independent verification.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers self-select favorable references, so negative reliability, delivery, or support experiences are routinely hidden. Uncovering the reliability or support problems suppliers omit takes lengthy independent digging. It requires savvy, well-networked buyers to surface unfavorable experiences suppliers withhold. Acting on curated references risks a costly wrong purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning machinery is damaged during freight, unloading, or rigging
rows.imp_rat: Damage during freight or rigging is a logistics error marginal to the operating job, with consequences that are repairable or covered, held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established freight and rigging practices make serious damage uncommon, though mishandling incidents still occur. Coordinated rigging schedules exist but rushed unloading windows still raise handling risk. Specialized riggers and packaging engineers substantially reduce damage when properly engaged, but their availability varies. Proper crating, air-ride freight, and professional rigging cost more but are commonly specified for high-value cartoners.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of underestimation in installation, commissioning, and validation costs for each candidate machine
rows.imp_rat: Underestimating installation/commissioning/validation costs is a procurement-budgeting error remote from cartoning operation (low relevance) with financial overrun impact (low-moderate severity), held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Estimates rely on optimistic vendor inputs and thin historical data, so underestimation recurs predictably. Building realistic install, commissioning, and validation cost estimates requires data-gathering that stretches the evaluation. Accurate estimation of commissioning and validation effort demands experienced project engineers who are frequently unavailable during acquisition. These downstream costs are chronically underestimated, causing budget overruns after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of unplanned machinery downtime cost omitted from each lifecycle cost estimate
rows.imp_rat: Downtime cost omitted from lifecycle estimates is an estimation error affecting financial planning, not core cartoning function; consequences are financial and limited. Capped below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Because downtime cost is inconsistently included, lifecycle estimates give an unreliable basis for machine selection. Downtime cost modeling is rarely done up front and is skipped when acquisition timelines are tight. Quantifying unplanned downtime cost demands reliability-engineering judgement seldom applied at acquisition. Lifecycle estimates routinely omit downtime cost, badly understating true cost of ownership for cartoners in sterile lines.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of acceptance, performance, or delivery provisions open to conflicting interpretation
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous acceptance/performance provisions can create contract disputes, but relative to running the cartoning job this is a peripheral contractual error (low relevance) with disputes usually resolvable (moderate-low severity), capped low by batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Provisions are frequently left open to interpretation, and ambiguity commonly survives into signed contracts. Tightening acceptance, performance, and delivery provisions requires negotiation cycles that add time. Drafting unambiguous provisions takes experienced contracts and validation expertise that is scarce. Conflicting interpretations later drive dispute and rework costs.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the compliance assessment is completed after a purchase commitment for cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Late compliance assessment relative to purchase is an administrative/procurement timing error, largely peripheral to running cartoning (low relevance) with limited operational consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature capital-approval workflows in regulated firms reliably place compliance review before commitment, so late assessment is fairly rare. Stage-gate procurement processes normally schedule compliance assessment before purchase commitment, so timing is usually right. Sequencing the assessment ahead of commitment is a process-discipline matter more than an expertise gap. Assessing after commitment risks locked-in noncompliance costs, but gated processes largely prevent this.

rows.source_job: Assess regulatory and safety compliance requirements
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among engineering, compliance, and suppliers about which requirements apply to the cartoning machinery
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among engineering, compliance, and suppliers about applicable requirements is an administrative alignment issue peripheral to the machine actually forming and sealing cartons (low relevance), and while it can cause rework its consequences are recoverable (moderate-low severity). Capped at 2.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Without a single authoritative requirements baseline, disagreement over applicable requirements recurs frequently and is only partly mitigated by review meetings. Reconciling differing requirement interpretations across engineering, compliance, and suppliers drags out the specification phase. Aligning cross-functional views requires senior facilitation and regulatory judgement that is in short supply. Requirement disputes cause rework and scope changes that inflate acquisition cost.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transit damage remaining undetected during the delivery inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage at inspection is a delivery-QA defect peripheral to running operation, with consequences that surface later but are recoverable, capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Shock indicators and visual checks catch obvious damage but internal or latent damage frequently escapes the delivery inspection. Delivery inspection windows are short, pressuring inspectors to sign off before hidden damage is found. Detecting concealed transit damage on precision cartoners requires experienced eyes that are not always present at receiving. Undetected damage becomes a costly dispute later when the claim window has closed.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of missing or incorrect machine components left unidentified after comparison with the accepted order
rows.imp_rat: Missing or incorrect components left unidentified after order comparison (relevance ~2) is a receiving-inspection error peripheral to running production; severity (~2) since it is caught and remedied via reconciliation rather than causing irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Packing-list checks catch most discrepancies, though some missing or wrong components still go unnoticed at receipt. Component reconciliation against the order at delivery is a defined receiving step done reasonably promptly. Matching delivered components to the order needs a knowledgeable receiver but is largely checklist-driven. Unidentified missing components cause commissioning delay costs if caught late.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of deviation from the specified lubricant quantity for each moving component
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from specified lubricant quantity affects component longevity somewhat, but consequences are gradual and non-catastrophic; both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual grease-gun application means quantity deviation is common and inconsistent, with automated lubrication systems still uncommon on cartoners. Applying the correct lubricant quantity adds little time when done by a diligent technician. Judging the specified quantity per component relies on technician feel and experience rather than precise metering. Over- or under-lubrication wastes lubricant or causes wear, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of periodic inspections postponed because the cartoning machinery remains in production
rows.imp_rat: Inspections postponed because the machine stays in production is a maintenance-scheduling delay tangential to the cartoning function (low relevance) that risks latent faults (moderate severity), capped at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Production-priority conflicts routinely override PM schedules, and while planned shutdown windows exist, they are often too short so postponement remains common. Inspections deferred because the line is running accumulate and compress into risky catch-up windows. Deciding what can safely wait requires planner judgement about condition and risk. Postponement avoids short-term production loss but risks costlier unplanned failures later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspection points assessed without current wear limits or baseline condition records
rows.imp_rat: Inspection points assessed without current wear limits is a maintenance-documentation gap tangential to the core function (low relevance) that can let degradation go unnoticed (moderate severity), capped by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: OEM manuals provide some limits, but real-world baseline and wear-limit records are frequently incomplete or not maintained per machine. Missing wear limits force inspectors to improvise, slowing the inspection and any resulting decision. Without documented baselines, inspection quality relies heavily on the individual technician's memory and judgement. Absent limits lead to either premature replacement or run-to-failure, both wasteful.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed service, repair, and inspection events missing from the machine maintenance history
rows.imp_rat: Completed events missing from the maintenance history is a record-keeping defect, only peripherally tied to the cartoning job (low relevance) with mostly administrative, recoverable consequences (low severity); it is held under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: GMP work-order discipline captures most events, but rushed or paper-based recording still lets some events go unlogged. Logging each service event takes minor time and is usually done at task close-out. Recording completed events is a low-skill clerical task once procedures require it. Missing history entries cost mainly during audits, not day-to-day operation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of maintenance entries containing inaccurate dates, work performed, parts replaced, or inspection results
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate maintenance-record entries are a documentation-quality defect that does not directly stop carton production (low relevance) and their consequences accumulate slowly rather than causing acute failure (low severity). Within the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured CMMS fields and review steps improve accuracy, yet manual transcription of dates, parts, and results still introduces recurring errors. Entering accurate details takes marginally longer than sloppy entry, a minor time factor. Accurate recording of parts and results needs the doer's attention and knowledge but not scarce expertise. Inaccurate entries cost through rework and audit findings rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between the machine maintenance history and supporting service, repair, or inspection records
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between maintenance history and supporting records are a records-reconciliation defect that does not impede active cartoning (low relevance) and produce recoverable audit friction (low severity). Kept within the 2.0 limit.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic reconciliation and change control reduce mismatches, but parallel paper and electronic records still diverge frequently. Reconciling history against supporting records is a time-bounded but occasionally lengthy cross-check. Spotting discrepancies between systems requires quality-review familiarity rather than deep expertise. Discrepancies mainly cost investigation labor and audit exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of compressed air lost through pneumatic leaks remaining after service
rows.imp_rat: Residual pneumatic leaks after service waste compressed air and energy, a modest efficiency loss rather than a job-blocker; proxies held low.
rows.sat_rat: Leaks rarely stop production so they persist, and current practice seldom verifies leak-free state after servicing. Finding and sealing pneumatic leaks during service is time-consuming and often deprioritized when the line still runs. Locating small leaks requires deliberate testing technique that many technicians skip without ultrasonic tools. Compressed air is expensive to generate and leaks left after service waste energy continuously, a chronic overspend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that fault history and machine-state evidence are missing when diagnosis begins
rows.imp_rat: Missing fault history at diagnosis slows repair but does not directly affect the running cartoning function (low relevance), and its consequence is delay rather than damage (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern PLC/HMI fault logging is widespread but limited buffer depth and unconfigured logging still leave evidence gaps. Missing fault history forces re-creation of the failure before diagnosis can proceed, extending downtime. Reconstructing lost machine-state evidence requires experienced inference rather than reading a record. Logging capacity is inexpensive, so gaps stem from configuration not cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of nozzles or applicator components damaged during daily cleaning
