rows.imp_rat: Nozzle or applicator damage during cleaning is a narrow, easily-remedied maintenance error with low relevance to the overall job and low severity. Bounded by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Even with soft tools and SOPs, fragile nozzles are frequently nicked or clogged during daily manual cleaning. Gentle handling of delicate nozzles slows the cleaning routine somewhat. Delicate applicator nozzles are easily damaged without careful, skilled handling. Nozzles and applicator heads are costly, so damage during cleaning is a real expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applicator components being misaligned after daily cleaning and reassembly
rows.imp_rat: Applicator misalignment after cleaning is a rework/setup concern that is peripheral to core packing (low relevance) and normally caught quickly (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Keyed fittings and documented reassembly help, but daily manual teardown still yields intermittent misalignment. Realigning applicator components after cleaning is a repeatable daily task that fits the shift routine. Correct reassembly depends on operator care and familiarity, so alignment quality varies by person. Misalignment causes glue defects and rework, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machinery unavailability caused by the required replacement component being unavailable when maintenance begins
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from an unavailable replacement part touches uptime but is framed here as a spares-logistics delay rather than the core forming function (low relevance) and is a recoverable production loss (low severity). Held to the 2.0 ceiling per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Stockout events remain unpredictable because failure timing is hard to forecast, so the shortfall recurs despite min-max policies. When the needed part is not on hand, expedited freight and emergency ordering still leave the machine down for hours to days, and this is a common cause of extended downtime. Planners can usually identify the right part quickly, so the delay is procurement-driven rather than a skill gap. Emergency stocking and rush shipping inflate costs, and holding safety stock for every possible failure is prohibitively expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of packaging line interruption during calibration and verification
rows.imp_rat: Line interruption during calibration and verification is a routine downtime error with limited centrality and recoverable impact, held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled calibration programs with defined intervals and traceable standards are mature practice, giving fairly consistent, planned interruptions. Calibration and verification require the line to stop, and the duration is largely fixed by the procedure and instruments. Calibration needs qualified metrology or technician skills that not every shift has on hand. The lost production time during calibration is a recurring but predictable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning-line unavailability during device checks and adjustments
rows.imp_rat: Line unavailability during device checks is routine adjustment downtime, peripheral to the core job and with recoverable impact, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized check routines and adjustment procedures make these interruptions largely repeatable and well contained. Device checks and adjustments stop the cartoning line, though routine checks are usually short and predictable. Most device checks follow standard procedures that trained operators can perform without specialists. Brief planned stoppages carry modest, foreseeable production cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent resolving discrepancies in the approved software and firmware versions
rows.imp_rat: Time spent resolving software/firmware version discrepancies is largely administrative overhead with minor direct impact on cartoning output; low relevance and low severity.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration management and validated-state records help, but incomplete change logs frequently leave version status ambiguous. Resolving discrepancies between approved and installed software/firmware versions is often a slow manual reconciliation against documentation. It takes controls specialists to interpret version records and determine the approved baseline, a skill bottleneck. Time spent chasing version discrepancies diverts skilled labor but is not itself a large material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable critical diagnostic data during machine operation
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable diagnostic data during operation hampers troubleshooting, a moderate reliability concern, yet the ceiling holds relevance and severity low.
rows.sat_rat: Onboard diagnostics are generally present, but data dropouts, unlogged parameters, and limited sensor coverage leave intermittent blind spots. Diagnostic data is usually available in real time on the HMI, minimizing wait during operation. Interpreting diagnostic data streams still requires trained personnel to be useful. Gaps in diagnostic data raise the cost of blind troubleshooting but the data infrastructure itself is a sunk investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between reject identification and physical segregation
rows.imp_rat: Delay between reject identification and segregation risks mix-up but is a handling-timing concern; low relevance to the job, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Automatic reject chutes segregate quickly, but manual reject handling still delays physical separation. Delay between reject identification and segregation is the crux and often lags in manual handling. Prompt segregation requires operator attentiveness rather than skill. Delayed segregation risks reject escape, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of discrepancy between recorded material counts and reconciliation totals
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded and reconciled counts is a material-accounting error, distant from erecting/inserting; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Counters and reconciliation procedures reduce discrepancies but manual counts still create mismatches. Reconciling count discrepancies consumes review time at batch close. Accurate reconciliation depends on careful counting discipline. Discrepancies trigger investigations, a documentation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a cartoning activity and its packaging-record entry
rows.imp_rat: Lag between activity and record entry is a documentation-timeliness issue, distant from erecting/inserting; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: EBR prompts encourage contemporaneous entry, though paper systems still create lags. Lag between activity and record entry is the core issue, reduced by contemporaneous logging. Timely entry requires documentation discipline more than expertise. Delayed entries cause rework but little material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of omitted conformance observations during production
rows.imp_rat: Omitted conformance observations during production is a monitoring-completeness gap, peripheral to the physical job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists prompt observations but reliance on manual attention makes omissions recurrent. Omitted observations aren't a delay but a gap discovered later. Consistently making required conformance observations depends on sustained operator vigilance that is scarce. Missed observations risk defects escaping, an indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cartons and sterile units discarded during setup verification
rows.imp_rat: Cartons/units discarded during setup verification is minor waste tied to the job; low severity, ranking-capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Setup scrap is predictable and partly controlled, but no practice eliminates verification waste. Setup verification scrap is generated quickly during startup. Skilled setup reduces the number of verification rejects. Startup cartons and units discarded during verification are a recurring known cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cartons and sterile units discarded during correction and restart
rows.imp_rat: Cartons/units discarded during correction/restart is job-adjacent waste but low severity; consequences are scrap cost, ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: No standard practice reliably salvages product straddling a stoppage, so scrap is routine. Discarding during correction/restart is quick but the loss recurs each event. Reducing restart scrap depends on skilled restart judgement. Cartons and sterile units scrapped at every correction are a chronic material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of removed prior-run material items that cannot be reconciled after line clearance
rows.imp_rat: Unreconciled removed prior-run items is a line-clearance accounting gap, peripheral to the physical job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation procedures work well but discrepancies still occur occasionally. Reconciling removed items takes counting time. Low skill; accurate counting and logging. Unreconciled items trigger investigations, costing time and money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that removed printed prior-run materials leave controlled custody before documented disposition
rows.imp_rat: Printed prior-run materials leaving custody is a control/mix-up risk, but low relevance to the current insertion job; ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation and custody logs are standard and largely reliable for printed materials. Documented custody adds procedural time. Low skill; procedural custody control. Lost custody of printed materials risks compliance cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury while removing trapped prior-run materials from the cartoning machine
rows.imp_rat: Injury removing prior-run materials is a housekeeping-safety concern with low relevance to erecting/inserting; consequences constrained by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established safety procedures make injury during removal rare. Safe prior-run material removal adds time. Requires safe-handling awareness. Injury cost dominates; low material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of abnormal noises, vibrations, leaks, or motions left unresolved before production release
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved noises/vibrations before release are a diligence gap, peripheral to erecting/inserting; consequences are latent risk, capped here.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-release checks catch obvious symptoms but subtle ones are often left unresolved. Resolving abnormal signs before release takes time. Interpreting noises/vibrations/leaks needs experienced senses. Unresolved issues risk later costly failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of required safety-verification evidence missing or unclear at production release
rows.imp_rat: Missing safety-verification evidence at release is a compliance-diligence gap, peripheral to the physical erecting/inserting job.
rows.sat_rat: Safety-verification documentation is standard but occasionally incomplete at release. Assembling safety evidence at release adds time. Low skill; completeness checking. Missing evidence delays release, costing capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to reproduce a sporadic cartoning fault for diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Time to reproduce a sporadic fault is a diagnostic-efficiency issue, peripheral to the job; consequences are investigation delay.
rows.sat_rat: Sporadic faults are hard to reproduce, so diagnosis drags unpredictably. Reproducing sporadic faults is inherently time-consuming. Reproduction demands strong diagnostic skill. Extended reproduction consumes materials and downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in linking observed cartoning symptoms to their initiating cause
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity linking symptoms to initiating cause is a diagnostic issue, peripheral to executing the job; consequences are slower resolution.
rows.sat_rat: Limited diagnostic instrumentation leaves symptom-cause links ambiguous. Ambiguous symptom-cause links slow diagnosis. Resolving ambiguity depends on deep machine knowledge. Ambiguity drives trial-and-error material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that diagnostic evidence is lost while a carton or sterile-unit jam is cleared
rows.imp_rat: Evidence lost while clearing a jam affects diagnosis, not job execution; consequences are weaker root-cause findings.
rows.sat_rat: Jam clearing routinely destroys diagnostic evidence, hampering root-cause work. Preserving evidence during clearing may slow clearing. Knowing what evidence to preserve requires diagnostic discipline. Lost evidence prolongs future waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a secondary cartoning fault masks the initiating cause
rows.imp_rat: A secondary fault masking the initiating cause affects diagnosis depth, not job execution; consequences are misdirected fixes.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostics often stop at the visible secondary fault, leaving root causes recurring. Untangling masked faults extends diagnosis time. Separating a secondary fault from the root cause demands high diagnostic expertise. Misdiagnosis wastes parts and repeated trials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required setup verification or production release after adjustment completion
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for verification/release after adjustment is a process-latency issue, peripheral to the job; consequences are delay, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Release timing depends on QA availability, causing inconsistent, often long waits. Waiting for verification/release after adjustment is a direct delay to reduce. Little operator skill affects this queue-driven wait. Idle waiting wastes line capacity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury while clearing trapped cartons or sterile units from the cartoning machine
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury clearing jams is a safety consequence but peripheral to the packaging job itself; severity capped by ranking below batch 2 items.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout and safe-access practices make injury during clearing uncommon. Safe clearing procedures add handling time. Safe manual clearing needs training and care. Injury cost is severe; material cost minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of verification results not traceable to the approved setup, carton format, and batch
rows.imp_rat: Verification results not traceable to setup/format/batch is a documentation-traceability gap, peripheral to job execution.
rows.sat_rat: Batch records and electronic logs make traceability generally good, with occasional gaps. Linking results to setup and batch adds documentation time. Low skill; depends on record discipline. Untraceable results cause costly re-verification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting assurance that sterile units were not contaminated during adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting sterile-contamination assurance is a psychological/confidence concern; low relevance to erecting/inserting, capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that an adjustment damaged the cartoning machine
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that an adjustment damaged the machine is a confidence/uncertainty concern, peripheral to the physical job; bounded consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of assigned personnel lacking the qualifications required for their cartoning tasks
rows.imp_rat: Unqualified personnel on cartoning tasks is a staffing-competency gap; relevant to execution quality but capped, bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Qualification matrices help but gaps in qualified coverage persist. Qualification checks are done during scheduling without much time cost. The core issue is availability of adequately qualified personnel for the tasks. Unqualified assignment risks errors and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a scheduled cartoning run waits for assigned personnel to become available
rows.imp_rat: Run waiting for assigned personnel is a staffing-timing delay, peripheral to the machine's job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Personnel availability timing is inconsistent, especially at shift edges. Waiting for assigned personnel to become available delays run start. Reflects scarcity of qualified staff more than scheduling technique. Waiting idles the line and equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of required cartoning positions remaining uncovered during shift transitions
rows.imp_rat: Uncovered positions during shift transitions is a staffing-coverage gap, distant from erecting/inserting; ranking-capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Shift-transition coverage gaps recur and are hard to eliminate. Uncovered positions during shift transitions cause coverage-gap time. Depends on cross-trained staff being available at handover. Coverage gaps idle the line briefly, wasting capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of schedule delay caused by pending quality review or batch-release decisions
rows.imp_rat: Schedule delay from pending quality/release decisions is a governance-latency issue, peripheral to the physical job; consequences capped.
rows.sat_rat: Review/release timing is variable and often a bottleneck. Schedule delays from pending quality review are a frequent time sink. Outside line-operator skill; depends on QA throughput. Delayed release ties up inventory and capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of conforming secondary packages classified as deviating
rows.imp_rat: Conforming packages wrongly rejected is a false-reject waste; relevant to yield but low severity and ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Over-sensitive inspection routinely classifies conforming packages as defective, a persistent false-reject problem. False rejects waste line time re-inspecting good cartons. Tuning inspection to avoid over-rejection requires vision expertise. Conforming packages wrongly rejected are pure scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that affected cartons, labels, sterile units, or machine-state evidence are unavailable or altered before examination
rows.imp_rat: Loss/alteration of evidence before examination affects forensic quality, peripheral to producing packages; consequences are weaker investigations.
rows.sat_rat: Physical and machine-state evidence is often altered or unavailable before examination. Evidence must be secured quickly or it degrades over time. Preserving machine-state evidence requires disciplined investigation practice. Lost evidence prolongs costly investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of examined cartons and sterile units unrepresentative of the affected production conditions
rows.imp_rat: Unrepresentative examined samples weaken conclusions, only indirectly relevant to running the job; consequences are misleading analysis.
rows.sat_rat: Examined units are often not representative of the affected conditions, weakening conclusions. Retrieving representative samples after the fact takes time. Selecting representative units requires sampling expertise. Unrepresentative samples mislead investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contributors involving carton erection, labeling, insertion, or closure are excluded from the investigation
rows.imp_rat: Excluding erection/labeling/insertion contributors from an investigation is an analytic-completeness issue, only indirectly central to running the job; consequences are limited to a weaker root-cause report.
rows.sat_rat: Investigations often narrow prematurely, leaving relevant contributors unexamined. Comprehensive investigation of all contributors extends timelines. Ensuring erection/labeling/insertion contributors are all considered depends on investigator thoroughness. Excluded contributors cause recurrence and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intermittent cartoning condition cannot be reproduced during the investigation
rows.imp_rat: Non-reproducible intermittent conditions hamper diagnosis, not the running job; consequences are prolonged investigation rather than direct package failure.
rows.sat_rat: Intermittent cartoning conditions are notoriously hard to reproduce, a chronic pain. Reproducing intermittent conditions consumes substantial investigation time. Reproducing intermittent faults demands rare diagnostic expertise. Unreproducible faults leave problems unresolved at ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unresolved discrepancies among issued, used, rejected, and returned cartoning components
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved component-count discrepancies are reconciliation admin, peripheral to the physical job; consequences are documentation delay.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation processes exist but discrepancies often remain open longer than desired. Resolving component discrepancies can drag out reconciliation duration. Reconciliation relies on material-accountability expertise. Prolonged discrepancies tie up inventory and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved discrepancies among issued, consumed, rejected, and returned components at batch disposition
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved discrepancies at batch disposition is a reconciliation/documentation error, peripheral to erection/insertion; consequences are release delay.
rows.sat_rat: Component reconciliation at disposition resolves most cases but residual unresolved discrepancies persist. Disposition-stage reconciliation adds review time. Depends on reconciler diligence and system support. Unresolved discrepancies delay release and cost money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether the cartoning batch remains deviation-free while others await direction
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty whether the batch stays deviation-free while awaiting direction is a disposition-timing concern, distant from erecting/inserting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty that cartoning line-clearance controls will withstand inspector scrutiny
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty that line-clearance controls survive inspector scrutiny is a compliance-confidence concern, peripheral to erecting/inserting; capped here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed without defensible evidence for an inspector's packaging-integrity question
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed without defensible evidence for an inspector is a confidence/compliance concern, peripheral to the physical job.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether every carton's status is accounted for during cartoning
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about every carton's status accounting is a traceability concern, peripheral to erecting/inserting; consequences are reconciliation gaps.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a patient-reaching labeling, serialization, or configuration escape is attributed to the overseer's governance
rows.imp_rat: A patient-reaching escape attributed to the overseer is a governance-blame concern; the physical job relevance is low and ranking caps it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which conflicts between packaging-control claims and records erode the overseer's credibility with inspectors
rows.imp_rat: Claims-vs-records conflicts eroding overseer credibility is a reputational/compliance issue, distant from erecting/inserting cartons.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cartoning-batch deviations are attributed to the overseer's governance during leadership reviews
rows.imp_rat: Batch deviations attributed to overseer governance is a blame/reputational concern, distant from erecting/inserting cartons.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton blanks scrapped during erection, insertion, or closure
rows.imp_rat: Blanks scrapped during erection/insertion/closure is waste tied to the job but low-consequence; trivial financial loss per unit.
rows.sat_rat: Scrap rates are tracked and improved but variability keeps some baseline loss. Measuring and reducing blank scrap requires line data collection over time. Reducing scrap uses standard process-optimization competency. Scrapped blanks are a direct, measurable material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packaging decisions reopened after downstream constraints emerge
rows.imp_rat: Packaging decisions reopened after downstream constraints is a planning-rework issue, distant from the physical job; capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Stage-gate reviews reduce reopenings but late-emerging constraints still force rework fairly often. Reopened packaging decisions cause major rework loops and schedule slippage, the primary loss felt. Anticipating downstream constraints upfront requires broad cross-functional expertise that is often missing early. Reopened decisions waste prior engineering effort and sometimes materials.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that released-packaging deviations are publicly attributed to the specifier's recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Released deviations publicly attributed to the specifier is a reputational-blame concern, peripheral to the physical job; ranking-capped.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with quality and regulatory leaders after a recommendation conflicts with approved controls
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with regulators after a conflicting recommendation is a reputational concern, distant from erecting/inserting; capped consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when endorsed configurations cause recurring carton or label rejects
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when endorsed configs cause rejects is a reputational concern, peripheral to erecting/inserting; bounded consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification observations with missing, incorrect, or untraceable cartoning failure classifications
rows.imp_rat: Missing/untraceable failure classifications is a data-quality gap in observations; low relevance to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or incorrect failure classifications are common where taxonomies are weak. Classifying failures correctly at capture adds minor time. Accurate failure classification depends on trained observers with a clear taxonomy. Classification is low direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cartoning failure-risk assessment remains unresolved when the purchase recommendation is due
rows.imp_rat: An unresolved failure-risk assessment at purchase time is a procurement-timing issue, distant from the cartoning job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved risk at decision time undermines confidence in the recommendation, a recurring problem. Risk assessments frequently remain open when the recommendation deadline arrives, forcing rushed or caveated decisions. Closing risk assessments on schedule requires experienced risk leads to drive resolution. Delays and rework carry schedule cost more than direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of evaluator discretion in protocol setup, intervention handling, reset conditions, and observation recording
rows.imp_rat: Evaluator discretion in protocol handling is a methodology-consistency issue, peripheral to the physical job; consequences are variable results.
rows.sat_rat: Loosely controlled setup and observation discretion introduces variability into results. Discretion in setup and recording rarely adds time but affects quality. High evaluator discretion means outcomes hinge on individual expertise and judgement. Discretion has little direct cost impact.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of protocol results lacking traceability to product format, operating condition, intervention, and acceptance criterion
rows.imp_rat: Protocol results lacking traceability to format/condition is a documentation-quality issue, peripheral to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Protocol results often lack complete linkage to condition, intervention, and criterion, leaving traceability gaps. Ensuring full traceability of results adds recording overhead. Structured traceable recording requires validation discipline. Traceability tooling is modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that disruptions in labeled-unit delivery or packed-carton transfer are absent from protocol scenarios
rows.imp_rat: Delivery/transfer disruptions absent from protocol scenarios is a test-coverage gap, peripheral to job execution; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Upstream/downstream disruption scenarios are frequently omitted from protocols, leaving those failure paths unproven. Adding delivery/transfer disruption scenarios extends protocol runtime. Designing realistic disruption scenarios requires operational insight. Extra scenario runs add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of attributing a cartoning qualification deviation to the wrong causal factor
rows.imp_rat: Wrong causal attribution in qualification is a diagnostic-accuracy issue; low relevance to the running job, moderate but capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Confounded factors make causal attribution unreliable, so wrong-cause conclusions occur regularly. Misattribution triggers repeated investigation cycles that waste time. Correct causal attribution is highly skill-dependent and error-prone. Wrong-cause chases consume investigation resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification deviations with incomplete evidence of machine state, carton condition, labeled sterile unit condition, and test conditions
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete deviation evidence weakens investigations, peripheral to job execution; consequences are inconclusive records, capped here.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation records commonly lack full machine/material/condition context, weakening investigations. Capturing complete state evidence for each deviation adds documentation time. Knowing what evidence to capture requires investigative expertise. Documentation effort is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of deviation records that cannot be linked to the affected carton, labeled sterile unit, material lot, and test condition
rows.imp_rat: Deviation records unlinkable to carton/unit/lot is a traceability gap, peripheral to erecting/inserting; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Lot and unit traceability is fairly mature in GMP settings, though condition linkage still breaks sometimes. Linking records to affected units and lots requires traceability effort. Maintaining linkage requires disciplined data practices. Traceability systems are a modest infrastructure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of observed cartoning qualification deviations that cannot be reproduced under recorded test conditions
rows.imp_rat: Non-reproducible qualification deviations are a test-methodology issue; peripheral to production and low-consequence beyond rework.
rows.sat_rat: Non-reproducible deviations are a chronic pain because recorded conditions rarely capture everything relevant. Reproducing deviations under recorded conditions can require repeated lengthy attempts. Reproducing elusive deviations demands high diagnostic skill. Repeated reproduction attempts waste materials and machine time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of diagnostic trials in which multiple suspected cartoning causes are changed simultaneously
rows.imp_rat: Changing multiple suspected causes at once is a diagnostic-discipline issue; low relevance to running the job, bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Simultaneous multi-cause changes are frequent under time pressure, confounding diagnosis. One-factor-at-a-time diagnostics are slower than changing many things at once. Disciplined single-variable diagnostics require methodological rigor many teams lack under pressure. More controlled trials cost more runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of unaccounted variation in carton and labeled sterile unit attributes across qualification lots
rows.imp_rat: Unaccounted qualification-lot attribute variation is a characterization gap, low central to daily insertion; consequences limited to qualification uncertainty.
rows.sat_rat: Lot variation is often under-characterized, so unexplained variation regularly appears. Accounting for lot-to-lot attribute variation requires multi-lot testing over time. Characterizing unaccounted variation needs statistical and materials expertise. Multiple qualification lots are costly to source and run.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected cartons or labeled sterile units whose diagnostic evidence is altered during stoppage recovery or handling
rows.imp_rat: Diagnostic evidence altered during recovery affects investigation fidelity, peripheral to running the job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence is routinely altered or lost during stoppage recovery, degrading diagnosis. Preserving diagnostic evidence during recovery slows stoppage handling. Preserving evidence during jam recovery requires trained discipline under pressure. Lost units and preserved samples add cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of verification results with missing, incorrect, or untraceable evidence
rows.imp_rat: Verification results with missing/untraceable evidence is a data-integrity gap; low relevance to job execution, bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Verification results with missing or untraceable evidence still occur regularly despite GDP expectations. Chasing missing or untraceable evidence delays synthesis. Complete, traceable evidence capture depends on trained documentation practice. Rework to recover evidence costs labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unaccounted changes to machine settings or test inputs between repeated verification runs
rows.imp_rat: Unaccounted setting/input changes between verification runs harm test validity; low relevance to production, bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Unaccounted setting changes between runs are a common confounder that undermines comparability. Tracking every setting change between runs adds documentation time. Disciplined change logging requires trained, attentive operators. Documentation overhead is minor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required verification runs not completed
rows.imp_rat: Required verification runs not completed is a qualification-completeness gap, peripheral to the running job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Skipped runs are usually tracked, but schedule pressure still leaves some required runs incomplete. Incomplete verification runs directly extend or reopen the qualification schedule. Completing all planned runs is more about scheduling discipline than expertise. Uncompleted then re-run testing wastes resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consequential cartoning evaluation findings omitted from the recommendation rationale
rows.imp_rat: Findings omitted from recommendation rationale is a decision-completeness gap, distant from job execution; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Consequential findings sometimes get dropped from recommendations, especially under deadline pressure. Ensuring all consequential findings reach the rationale adds review time. Judging which findings are consequential requires senior evaluator expertise. Review effort is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions among findings from different runs, conditions, or cartoning configurations
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved contradictions among run findings is an analysis-coherence issue, peripheral to job execution; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved contradictions across runs are common and often carried into conclusions. Resolving contradictory findings requires additional analysis cycles. Reconciling conflicting run results demands strong analytical judgement. Reconciliation is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recommendation conclusions lacking traceability to acceptance criteria and underlying evaluation observations
rows.imp_rat: Recommendation conclusions lacking traceability is an analysis-rigor gap, distant from erecting/inserting; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Recommendation conclusions are often incompletely traceable to underlying evidence, a recurring audit finding. Tracing conclusions back to observations adds documentation time. Building traceable conclusion rationale requires structured authoring skill. Documentation is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material deviations or anomalous cartoning findings given unsupported dispositions in the recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported dispositions in the recommendation is a decision-quality gap, peripheral to the physical job; ranking-capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Unsupported dispositions still appear, but review gates catch many, keeping this moderate. Properly dispositioning anomalies takes deliberation time. Sound disposition of anomalous findings requires expert justification. Deliberation is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation records associated with the wrong run, condition, or cartoning configuration during synthesis
rows.imp_rat: Records tied to wrong run/config during synthesis is a mislabeling error in analysis, peripheral to erecting/inserting.
rows.sat_rat: Records occasionally get tied to the wrong run or config, but structured systems keep this fairly contained. Reconciling mis-associated records during synthesis wastes time. Correct association requires careful attention during data synthesis. Correction effort is labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which predicted changeover effort differs from effort under representative sterile fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Predicted-vs-actual changeover-effort gap is a planning-accuracy concern, peripheral to erecting/inserting; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Predictions rarely match representative sterile fill-finish conditions, a persistently unreliable area. Predicted-versus-actual changeover effort gaps only surface during real runs, delaying accurate planning. Estimating effort under representative sterile conditions requires experienced judgement that is often off. Misprediction leads to under-resourced changeovers and cost overruns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required adjustments, clearance checks, and traceability verifications omitted from each configuration assessment
rows.imp_rat: Omitted adjustments/checks in each configuration assessment weaken completeness; peripheral to the physical job, ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists help but omissions of required adjustments and verifications still recur per assessment. Completing all adjustments and verifications per assessment adds structured time. Remembering every required clearance check and verification depends on assessor thoroughness rather than a foolproof aid. Omitted checks cause downstream failures and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in changeover effort across operators, shifts, and format transitions
rows.imp_rat: Changeover-effort variation across operators/shifts is a process-consistency issue, peripheral to whether cartons erect; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standardization is incomplete, leaving significant operator- and shift-dependent variation. Variation in changeover effort translates into unpredictable transition times. Changeover effort still depends heavily on individual operator skill, so variation across operators and shifts is high. Inconsistent effort causes unpredictable labor and material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of labeled sterile units and carton material discarded during format setup and restart
rows.imp_rat: Units/material discarded during setup/restart is job-adjacent waste but low severity; consequences are limited scrap cost, ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Waste levels vary and are only partly controlled by current setup practice. Setup/restart scrap generation is quick but the wasted material recurs each transition. Minimizing discarded units and carton material depends on operator skill during setup. Discarded sterile units and carton material at each setup are a recurring, directly measurable expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to obtain an authorized disposition for an exception not covered by agreed rules
rows.imp_rat: Time to authorize an uncovered-exception disposition is a governance-latency issue, peripheral to the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Ad hoc escalation is unpredictable in duration, making this one of the worst-served needs. Obtaining an authorized disposition for an uncovered exception is chronically slow, stalling the line while approvals are chased. These decisions require senior authority whose availability is limited, offering no quick self-service path. Prolonged holds tie up product and capacity at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the aggregation specification remains outdated after an approved unit or carton change
rows.imp_rat: Outdated aggregation spec after a change is a documentation-latency issue, distant from the physical job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Change control eventually updates specs but the lag is a recurring, poorly controlled gap. The aggregation specification often stays outdated for a noticeable duration after an approved change, delaying compliant production. Propagating changes correctly requires change-control diligence that varies in speed. Running against a stale spec causes rework and waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of risk-adjusted financial exposure from sterility, quality, and compliance failures omitted from the justification
rows.imp_rat: Omitted risk-adjusted financial exposure is a justification-completeness gap, distant from the physical job; ranking-capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Because the exposure is hard to quantify, justifications inconsistently capture it, leaving the outcome unreliable. Quantifying risk-adjusted exposure is analytically slow and often deferred in the justification. Modeling sterility and compliance failure exposure requires specialized risk-quantification skill rarely resident in procurement. Financial cases routinely omit risk-adjusted exposure from sterility and quality failures, understating true cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory pharmaceutical compliance and validation requirements unaddressed by each supplier offer
rows.imp_rat: Compliance/validation requirements unaddressed in supplier offers is a sourcing-completeness gap, distant from job execution; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: URS and quality checklists catch many gaps, yet suppliers still submit offers with unaddressed mandatory requirements that surface during qualification. Compliance checklists take time to assemble but are a known step in pharma procurement workflows. Identifying every mandatory validation and compliance requirement demands scarce quality/regulatory expertise that purchasing must borrow. Missed compliance requirements are expensive to remediate later but the checklist approach adds modest upfront cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical capability claims unsupported for required sterile-unit and carton configurations
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported capability claims for configurations is a sourcing-verification gap, peripheral to the running job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers make broad capability claims that are frequently unverified for the actual required configurations until testing. Verifying capability claims for specific sterile-unit and carton formats extends the evaluation timeline considerably. Confirming that vendor claims hold for the exact configurations requires specialized packaging-engineering judgement. Unsupported claims lead to expensive post-award redesign, though FAT/SAT catches some before final payment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier delivery and qualification commitments fail to support the required production date
rows.imp_rat: Supplier commitments failing to support the production date is a procurement-timing risk, distant from the physical job; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Delivery and qualification commitments frequently fail to hold under real conditions, and status-quo contracts give weak assurance that they will support the date. Suppliers routinely quote lead times but late slippage against the required production date is common and surfaces too late for the buyer to react. Judging whether a supplier's delivery and qualification commitment is credible requires scarce cross-functional experience that most purchase executors lean on engineering for. Milestone payments and penalty clauses partially offset late delivery but rarely recover the true cost of a missed production date.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical capability, format-handling, and acceptance requirements omitted from the cartoning-machine contract
rows.imp_rat: Critical capabilities omitted from the machine contract is a procurement-spec gap, upstream of the job; ranking-capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Even mature URS templates leave critical requirements out of the contract, producing recurring disputes at acceptance. Drafting a complete capability and acceptance spec is time-consuming and often compressed under schedule pressure. Capturing all critical format-handling and acceptance requirements needs deep engineering and quality input that purchasing cannot supply alone. Omitted requirements trigger costly change orders and rework not budgeted in the contract.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict between supplier obligations and sterile fill-finish quality, documentation, and data-integrity requirements
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between supplier obligations and quality/data-integrity requirements is a contractual-alignment gap, distant from the physical job.
rows.sat_rat: Quality-agreement templates help but conflicts between supplier terms and sterile fill-finish requirements routinely slip through. Reconciling supplier obligations against fill-finish quality and data-integrity requirements adds review cycles. Detecting conflicts with data-integrity and documentation requirements demands scarce quality-systems expertise. Unresolved conflicts create costly deviations and audit findings later in the lifecycle.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of installation, integration, qualification, and documentation responsibilities unassigned between buyer and supplier
rows.imp_rat: Unassigned installation/qualification responsibilities is a project-governance gap, peripheral to erecting/inserting; bounded consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Responsibility matrices exist in mature contracts but gaps in assignment still frequently emerge during installation and qualification. Assigning installation, qualification and documentation responsibilities takes negotiation time that is often rushed. A clear RACI across buyer and supplier requires experienced project-scoping judgement not always present in purchasing. Unassigned responsibilities cause scope-gap charges and duplicated effort that inflate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contractual delivery occurs after the required production-readiness date
rows.imp_rat: Contractual delivery after readiness date is a procurement-schedule issue, peripheral to erecting/inserting; ranking-capped consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PO terms provide some enforcement but delivery reliability against a hard readiness date remains inconsistent. Contractual delivery dates are set but slippage past production-readiness is a recurring, poorly-controlled event. Setting realistic dates demands experienced buyers who understand qualification lead times, expertise that is unevenly available. Liquidated-damages clauses exist but seldom cover downstream production losses from late readiness.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between completing a maintenance event and recording it in the machine history
rows.imp_rat: Delay between completing maintenance and recording it in history is a bookkeeping timeliness issue peripheral to the core job (low relevance) with trivial, easily absorbed consequences (very low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Immediate electronic logging keeps most entries timely, but deferred end-of-shift recording still introduces memory-based inaccuracies. With mobile CMMS entry the lag between finishing work and recording it is short, though paper backlogs still delay some sites. Prompt recording is a discipline matter, not a skill barrier. Delayed recording carries little direct cost beyond occasional data-integrity questions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning machine unavailability caused by daily cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from daily cleaning is expected routine unavailability, only loosely central and with trivial per-instance consequences, held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established cleaning SOPs and tool-less quick-clean designs make daily cleaning a consistent, predictable activity. Daily cleaning stoppages are routine and their duration is well understood, though changeover and cleaning still consume real uptime. Cleaning is largely operator-level work with clear SOPs, requiring little specialist skill. The recurring daily uptime loss is a known, budgeted cost of operation.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among the purchase order, packing list, and acceptance criteria at delivery
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts among purchase order, packing list, and acceptance criteria (relevance ~1) are documentation-reconciliation errors quite distant from cartoning performance; severity (~2) is low since discrepancies are resolvable clerically at delivery.
rows.sat_rat: These documents are prepared by different parties and routinely conflict in detail at delivery. Cross-checking PO, packing list, and acceptance criteria at delivery adds receiving effort but is scheduled. Reconciling three documents demands attentive staff familiar with all of them. Document conflicts cause disputes and delayed payments with moderate cost impact.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, maintenance engineer, procurement manager
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the machinery and accompanying components inaccessible for physical examination at delivery
rows.imp_rat: Components inaccessible for examination at delivery is a receiving-inspection limitation, marginal to operation with minor consequences absorbed at intake.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful fraction of components stays inside sealed crates or factory-installed guards, so some remain unexamined at delivery. Scheduled delivery inspections give reasonable time to walk the equipment, though packed sub-assemblies slow full access. Trained receiving inspectors and vendor reps can access most components, but tightly crated internals need supplier assistance. Uncrating and re-crating for full inspection adds handling cost that is often skipped to save money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of anchor holes drilled outside the required mounting locations
rows.imp_rat: Anchor holes drilled outside required mounting locations is an installation rework error, secondary to the machine's cartoning function (very low relevance) with correctable but costly rework (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Drilling templates and marked layouts make correct hole placement fairly consistent, with occasional misplacements. Using templates or drill jigs adds minor setup time but speeds accurate hole placement. Accurate anchor drilling relies on installer care and basic layout skill that competent crews possess. Misdrilled holes waste effort and require patching, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of initial meter readings recorded incorrectly
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly recorded initial meter readings is a data-entry defect at setup with little bearing on cartoning operation (very low relevance) and minor downstream tracking impact (low severity). Within 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual meter recording is error-prone, though the low complexity keeps most readings acceptably accurate. Recording initial meter readings is a quick task at setup. Reading and logging meters needs minimal skill, so errors are transcription slips. Incorrect baseline readings are cheap to make but costly to trace later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material acquisition, installation, validation, and ownership cost elements omitted from machine comparisons
rows.imp_rat: Omitting cost elements from machine comparisons is a procurement-analysis defect, peripheral to the running job with only trivial financial-decision consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Machine comparisons routinely omit cost elements inconsistently across bidders, so the comparison itself is not apples-to-apples. Assembling every installation, validation, and ownership cost element for each machine takes weeks and is often truncated under deadline pressure. Knowing which lifecycle cost elements to include requires cross-functional expertise that is rarely fully assembled for a comparison. Omitted validation, tooling, and ownership costs mean the cheapest-looking machine is frequently not the cheapest to own.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in quotation exclusions and pricing terms
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in quotation exclusions and pricing terms is a procurement/commercial matter with negligible bearing on the cartoning function (very low relevance), risking only cost disputes (low severity). Held below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Quotation terms are habitually vague and non-standard, so ambiguity in exclusions and pricing persists across most quotes. Clarifying ambiguous exclusions and pricing terms requires repeated supplier queries that slow quote comparison. Interpreting quotation terms takes procurement and contracts expertise that many technical evaluators lack. Ambiguous exclusions hide costs that later emerge as change orders, undermining budget accuracy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of quoted commercial terms that conflict with purchaser approval requirements
rows.imp_rat: Quoted commercial terms conflicting with approval requirements is a purely commercial-approval error with little bearing on the cartoning function and mild, correctable consequences. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard approval matrices catch most conflicting commercial terms, though edge-case clauses occasionally pass unflagged. Quote-to-approval reviews are routine and reasonably timely within procurement cycles. Procurement staff are trained to screen terms against approval policy, reducing dependence on rare expertise. Non-conforming terms mainly cost negotiation time rather than direct money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which the total acquisition cost exceeds the approved project budget
rows.imp_rat: Acquisition cost exceeding approved budget is a purely administrative procurement error, largely irrelevant to the machine functioning and with trivial operational consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Total-cost tracking is fragmented across procurement, engineering, and finance, so the final number frequently surprises the project. Budget-vs-actual reconciliation happens late in the acquisition timeline, so overruns surface only after commitments are locked. Reliable cost forecasting for complex cartoning lines demands experienced estimators who are scarce and inconsistently involved. Acquisition costs routinely creep past approved budgets because scope changes, currency, and integration extras are chronically underestimated with only spreadsheet controls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among stakeholder requirements for the cartoning machinery purchase
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent stakeholder requirements is a requirements-gathering ambiguity peripheral to producing cartons, with low, correctable consequences. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Stakeholder requirements are commonly inconsistent and captured informally, so conflicts persist into procurement. Aligning stakeholder requirements is an iterative process that extends the front end of acquisition. Facilitating consensus across production, quality, engineering, and finance demands strong requirements-management skill. Unreconciled requirements cause expensive late changes and scope disputes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which the purchase commitment exceeds the approved capital budget
rows.imp_rat: Purchase commitment exceeding the capital budget is a financial-governance error unrelated to physically running cartons (very low relevance) with budget-overrun consequences (low-moderate severity), held low.
rows.sat_rat: Budget gating and purchase approval workflows are mature and routinely catch overcommitment, making breaches relatively uncommon. Capital approval and budget-check gates are established steps that fit within the acquisition timeline. Comparing commitment against an approved budget is straightforward finance work well within team capability. Formal capital-authorization controls directly guard against committing beyond the approved budget.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved warranty start-date conflicts across purchase, acceptance, commissioning, and registration records
rows.imp_rat: Warranty start-date conflicts across records is a purely administrative reconciliation error irrelevant to machine function, with minor contractual consequences, capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Warranty start dates are captured in disconnected systems, so conflicting dates across purchase, acceptance, and registration are common and unresolved. Reconciling warranty start dates across records is a manual afterthought that lags commissioning by weeks or months. It takes contract-savvy staff to spot conflicting warranty triggers across purchase and acceptance documents. Ambiguous start dates can silently shorten coverage, exposing the buyer to avoidable repair costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency among supplier definitions for cartoning machinery types and configurations
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent supplier definitions for machine types is a terminology-alignment nuisance during selection, peripheral to the CFJ with trivial consequences. Held under 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is no universal taxonomy for cartoner types and configurations, so supplier definitions routinely conflict. Reconciling divergent supplier definitions of machine types adds back-and-forth clarification time. Normalizing inconsistent vendor terminology requires experienced engineers who know the equipment class. Definitional mismatches cause mis-scoped buys whose correction costs money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between the purchaser's requested demonstration scope and the supplier's understood scope
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between requested and understood demonstration scope is a pre-purchase communication error, peripheral to the core job with mild consequences. Capped below prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Written demo protocols reduce mismatch, yet requested versus understood scope still diverges on details. Demonstration scope alignment happens in pre-award meetings but is often finalized late. Clearly framing FAT/demo scope needs a knowledgeable buyer to prevent misunderstanding. Scope mismatches trigger repeat demonstrations or travel whose cost is moderate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of stocked spare parts rendered unusable by storage damage or contamination
rows.imp_rat: Spare parts ruined by storage damage is a maintenance-logistics error only tangential to the machine's core cartoning function (very low relevance); consequences are absorbable replacement costs (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Controlled stores with shelving, climate control and FIFO rotation are common practice, though contamination and shelf-life lapses still occur in less disciplined stores. Discovering a damaged spare at point of use forces a scramble that extends downtime. Proper storage of seals, elastomers and electronics needs some know-how but is largely procedural. Damaged or expired parts are direct scrap losses plus the cost of re-procurement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the transferred machine title remains subject to an undisclosed lien or security interest
rows.imp_rat: An undisclosed lien on transferred title is a legal-ownership defect remote from cartoning operation; while it can complicate ownership, it is peripheral to the CFJ. Capped by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: UCC/lien search practice is mature, so undisclosed encumbrances rarely survive a proper transfer. Lien searches add days but are routine within asset-transfer workflows. Clearing title requires legal/finance expertise that established transactions engage. Lien searches and releases carry modest transactional cost.

rows.source_job: Isolate energy sources on the machine
rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve inaccessible or defective isolation points before removal
rows.imp_rat: Time to resolve defective isolation points before removal is a decommissioning-logistics delay barely related to the cartoning CFJ (very low relevance) with modest schedule cost (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Ad hoc repair approaches vary widely, so the time to resolve is inconsistent and hard to predict. Resolving seized or defective isolation points is a common, unpredictable delay with few fast remedies. Freeing or bypassing a defective isolation point safely needs experienced maintenance judgement. The labor and hold time to fix bad isolation points add cost but are usually contained.

rows.source_job: Erase proprietary data from machine controller
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that records subject to retention obligations are erased before preservation
rows.imp_rat: Erasing records subject to retention before preservation is a records-governance error essentially unrelated to the cartoning function, though it carries some compliance risk, still capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Retention holds and archiving policies are established, but ad hoc deletion during equipment retirement still happens. Preserving retention-obligated records is quick when procedures are followed but flagging can lag decommissioning. Knowing which records carry retention obligations requires records-management judgement not always present on the floor. Premature erasure risks regulatory penalties that dwarf storage costs.

rows.source_job: Erase proprietary data from machine controller
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the machine is transferred or scrapped before controller data erasure is completed
rows.imp_rat: Failure to erase controller data before transfer or scrap is an end-of-life data-security concern unrelated to the cartoning job itself (very low relevance), though a data leak could carry some consequence (low severity). Held at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Few facilities have a gating control tying disposal release to completed erasure, so the sequencing failure is common. Erasure is often the last thing considered and machines are frequently moved out before it happens. Coordinating erasure before transfer needs someone owning OT-security accountability, a role often unassigned at disposal. Premature transfer with intact data creates outsized IP and compliance cost against negligible avoidance cost.

rows.source_job: Erase proprietary data from machine controller
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that completion of controller data erasure cannot be demonstrated to security, quality, and regulatory approvers
rows.imp_rat: Inability to demonstrate controller data erasure to approvers is a decommissioning compliance-evidence gap disconnected from the operating carton job (very low relevance), with modest audit consequences (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized, demonstrable erasure certificates for OT devices barely exist today, so proving completion to approvers is chronically unreliable. Producing erasure evidence is an afterthought that delays sign-off when demanded at all. Generating audit-grade proof of controller erasure requires specialized tooling and expertise that is largely absent. Inability to demonstrate erasure risks failed audits and rework, though evidence capture is inexpensive if planned.

rows.stk: Sustainability Officer / Environmental Health and Safety Specialist
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoning machine components assigned to an incompatible recovery stream
rows.imp_rat: Assigning components to an incompatible recovery stream is an end-of-life recycling defect unrelated to producing cartons (very low relevance), carrying modest environmental/compliance impact (low severity). Kept at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: General recycling practice exists, but complex composite machine components are frequently misassigned to incompatible streams. Sorting components into recovery streams adds handling time that scrap processors partly absorb. Assigning mixed-material machine parts to the right recovery stream needs materials knowledge often lacking at disposal. Wrong-stream assignment loses recovery value and can incur reprocessing charges.

rows.stk: Sustainability Officer / Environmental Health and Safety Specialist
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing recyclable cartoning machine components to facilities not authorized to accept them
rows.imp_rat: Routing recyclables to unauthorized facilities is an end-of-life disposal defect disconnected from cartoning (very low relevance) with limited compliance exposure (low severity). Bounded to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vetted recycler networks and downstream audits reduce misrouting, though verification gaps persist for lower-value scrap. Verifying a recycler's authorization adds procurement time that is usually manageable. Confirming facility authorization requires waste-management diligence that established brokers handle. Routing to unauthorized facilities risks liability far above the vetting cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required maintenance, calibration, compliance, and operational records missing from the retired cartoning machine archive
rows.imp_rat: Missing records from a retired-machine archive is an end-of-life documentation error with minimal impact on active cartoning and contained consequences. Capped by batch position.
rows.sat_rat: Full maintenance/calibration/compliance archives are commonly incomplete for machines nearing end of life. Reconstructing a complete retired-machine archive is slow when records were never centralized. Completing the archive needs staff who understand which records the machine's lifecycle generated. Gaps force costly reconstruction or write-offs of the asset's documented value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of archived machine records becoming unreadable or corrupted before the retention obligation ends
rows.imp_rat: Archived records becoming unreadable is a data-preservation reliability issue unrelated to the cartoning function, with limited compliance consequence, capped low per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Media degradation and obsolete formats still corrupt archives despite backup practices. Format migration and integrity checks take time but the loss manifests years later. Long-term digital preservation requires archival IT skills seldom dedicated to retired machines. Migrating and maintaining readable archives adds ongoing storage and IT expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of archived machine records assigned an incorrect retention requirement
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect retention requirement on archived records is an administrative filing defect essentially irrelevant to cartoning function, with modest compliance consequence, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Retention taxonomies exist but manual assignment produces a meaningful error rate. Assigning correct retention classes is not time-critical but errors surface much later. Correct classification demands specialized retention-schedule knowledge that is unevenly applied. Misclassification causes either costly over-retention or risky early disposal.

rows.source_job: Remove machine anchors and foundation fixtures
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging concealed floor utilities beneath the cartoning-machine foundation during anchor extraction
rows.imp_rat: Damaging concealed floor utilities during anchor extraction is a decommissioning hazard peripheral to the cartoning CFJ (very low relevance); though facility damage could be costly (moderate severity), ranking caps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: With incomplete facility records the strike risk during blind anchor removal remains high and poorly mitigated today. Locating concealed utilities before anchor extraction adds delay, but the real failure is a sudden strike rather than a schedule slip. Reliable avoidance depends on scarce experienced riggers and accurate as-built drawings that are frequently missing or outdated. A struck conduit or drain line triggers expensive facility repairs and downtime far exceeding the extraction cost.

rows.stk: EHS coordinator, maintenance technician, facilities manager
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of waste containers or shipment records missing required identification for materials removed from the cartoning machine
rows.imp_rat: Missing identification on waste containers or shipment records is a waste-disposal documentation defect unrelated to carton forming (very low relevance), risking minor regulatory tracking gaps (low severity). Within the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manifest and labeling systems are mature, but incomplete or mismatched records still occur under manual data entry. Labeling containers and completing shipment records is a routine clerical step in the manifest process. Applying required identification follows checklists that need only modest training. Missing IDs cause rejected shipments and rework, a moderate cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that available references do not represent comparable sterile fill-finish installations
rows.imp_rat: References not representing comparable installations is an evaluation-input error, remote from the cartoning function itself, with modest consequences. Ceiling enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Comparable sterile fill-finish references are scarce, so available references often do not represent the intended application. Finding truly comparable sterile fill-finish references takes extended vetting time. Judging whether a reference installation is genuinely comparable requires niche sterile-processing expertise. Site visits to verify comparability add travel and staff cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that site-route constraints block movement of the machinery to its intended position
rows.imp_rat: Site-route constraints blocking machinery movement (relevance ~1) is a logistics/installation planning error remote from the cartoning function itself; severity (~2) since it can be pre-checked and mitigated, causing at most reschedule delay.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-move route surveys make blockages uncommon, though unanticipated site constraints still occasionally arise. Route surveys are typically done ahead of delivery, giving time to plan movement. Rigging planners and site engineers reliably assess access routes when engaged. A blocked route forces costly rework or partial disassembly, but planning usually prevents it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in cost inclusions and exclusions across candidate quotations
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in cost inclusions/exclusions across quotations is a purely commercial comparability issue with no impact on the cartoning function (very low relevance) and only budget-estimation consequences (low severity). Held at or below 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Because vendors structure quotes differently, inconsistency in cost inclusions is the norm and only partially corrected by manual leveling. Normalizing inclusions and exclusions across quotes into a comparable basis is tedious manual work. Building an apples-to-apples cost comparison needs experienced estimators who can spot hidden scope differences. Inconsistent inclusions distort total-cost comparisons and lead to selecting on misleading price signals.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of recurring-cost assumptions unsupported by sterile fill-finish operating evidence
rows.imp_rat: Recurring-cost assumptions unsupported by fill-finish operating evidence is a financial-modeling ambiguity remote from the carton job (very low relevance) with only budgeting-accuracy consequences (low severity). Bounded to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recurring-cost figures are typically assumption-based with little sterile-specific operating evidence, so this need is poorly served. Gathering sterile fill-finish operating evidence to support recurring-cost assumptions is slow and often skipped under deadline. Validating operating-cost assumptions against real aseptic-process evidence requires specialist operational data few buyers can access. Unsupported recurring-cost assumptions produce total-cost-of-ownership errors that surface only during operation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material warranty, liability, or delivery provisions on which buyer and supplier positions remain irreconcilable
rows.imp_rat: Irreconcilable warranty/liability provisions is a contractual negotiation error remote from operating the machine (very low relevance) with potential financial exposure (low-moderate severity), capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard contract negotiation resolves most such provisions, though a residual set of positions can remain deadlocked. Negotiating warranty, liability, and delivery provisions to closure can prolong the contracting phase. Resolving irreconcilable commercial positions needs skilled legal and procurement negotiators. Unresolved liability and warranty terms expose the buyer to later financial risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required compliance, validation, or documentation deliverables omitted from the contract
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance/validation deliverables from the contract is an administrative documentation gap (very low relevance to running cartons) though it could cause compliance exposure (low-moderate severity), held at 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deliverable lists are often incomplete and omissions recur, making this a consistently under-served risk in contracts. Compiling the full list of required compliance and validation deliverables into the contract is a slow, checklist-driven effort. Knowing which validation and documentation deliverables must be contractually mandated requires deep GMP procurement expertise. Omitted deliverables must be bought later at a premium or self-generated at cost.

rows.source_job: Evaluate used or refurbished machinery options
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of evaluated options lacking sufficient maintenance, refurbishment, and compliance records to verify their history
rows.imp_rat: Evaluated options lacking history records is an evaluation-phase completeness error, remote from the running cartoning job, with modest consequences. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Records are frequently incomplete or unverifiable, so many evaluated options cannot have their history confirmed. Chasing maintenance, refurbishment, and compliance history for used or refurbished options takes significant elapsed time. Interpreting incomplete service and compliance records needs specialist scrutiny that is often unavailable. Missing history forces costly independent inspections or risks buying a poor-history machine.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of loaded cartons with incomplete closures at discharge
rows.plain: Make sure every filled carton is fully closed when it leaves the process. This protects the contents and prevents cartons from opening during handling.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete closures at discharge is a core packaging-integrity defect, but the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Closure inspection reliably flags most incomplete closures at discharge. Incomplete closures at discharge are caught at inspection, adding rework. Complete closure depends on glue/tuck setup skill. Incomplete closures cause rejects, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile units damaged during carton closing
rows.plain: Prevent germ-free products from being damaged while their cartons are closed. This protects the products and avoids waste.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile units damaged during carton closing is a real handling defect, but ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Tuned closing sections reduce damage but it recurs with variation. Units damaged during closing are caught downstream, adding rework. Preventing closing damage depends on correct closure setup. Damaged units are scrapped, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons closed before all required contents are enclosed
rows.plain: Fewer cartons should be closed before all the required items are inside. This helps ensure each customer receives a complete package.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons closed before all contents enclosed is a core defect, but the ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Presence sensors and checkweighers reliably catch most incomplete cartons before closing, though some slip. Cartons closed short of contents are caught at check-weigh, adding rework time. Detection relies on automated checks rather than operator skill. Prematurely closed cartons become rejects, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons torn or deformed during closing
rows.plain: Fewer cartons should tear or become misshapen while they are being closed. This helps keep the product protected and the cartons usable.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons torn during closing affect job quality directly, but the batch ceiling caps the score at 1.0 average; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Tuned closing reduces tearing but deformation recurs with variation. Torn cartons at closing cause rejects and some rework. Preventing tearing depends on closure setup skill. Torn cartons are scrapped, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of rejected cartons remaining in conforming output
rows.plain: Keep fewer rejected boxes mixed in with products that passed inspection. This helps prevent unsuitable products from being shipped or used.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected cartons remaining in conforming output is a quality-escape defect, but the mandatory ceiling caps this batch at 1.0; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Automatic reject diversion is fairly reliable but manual reject handling still allows escapes. Rejects remaining in good output are found late if at all. Keeping rejects out depends on reliable segregation discipline. Escaped rejects risk recalls, a severe cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of conforming cartons incorrectly segregated as rejects
rows.plain: Reduce the number of good boxes that are mistakenly placed with rejected products. This prevents acceptable products from being set aside unnecessarily.
rows.imp_rat: Conforming cartons segregated as rejects affects yield, but the batch ceiling caps it at 1.0; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation logic and criteria reduce false rejects but tuning inconsistency remains. Misclassifying conforming cartons as rejects wastes good output without a time cost. Correct classification depends on calibrated criteria and judgement. Good cartons wrongly segregated are a direct waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejected cartons unaccounted for at reconciliation
rows.plain: Make sure every carton rejected during production is accounted for during the final count. This helps prevent missing or unexplained product from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected cartons unaccounted at reconciliation is a reconciliation defect, peripheral to erecting/inserting, with modest severity; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reject counters help but manual reconciliation still leaves rejects unaccounted. Unaccounted rejects are found at reconciliation, delaying batch close. Full reject accounting depends on disciplined counting. Unaccounted rejects force investigations, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of rejected output with unreadable product or lot identity
