rows.plain: Make sure rejected products still show readable product and batch information. This helps people identify and control them correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected output with unreadable identity is a traceability defect, but the batch ceiling caps it at 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Reject handling often damages identity, so lot readability on rejects is inconsistent. Unreadable reject identity slows disposition. Maintaining reject identity depends on labeling discipline. Unidentifiable rejects force broader scrap, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejected sterile units broken or leaking after segregation
rows.plain: Prevent rejected germ-free product units from being broken or leaking after they have been separated from usable products. This keeps the rejected items contained and makes them easier to handle safely.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected sterile units broken/leaking after segregation is a handling defect, but ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reject chutes are often not gentle, so segregated units still break inconsistently. Broken rejects after segregation aren't a time issue but a handling one. Gentle reject handling depends on operator care. Broken/leaking rejects add cleanup and disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required cartoning activities omitted from the packaging record
rows.plain: Record every required step of putting products into cartons in the packaging record. This creates a complete account of what happened during the work.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted activities from the packaging record is a documentation-compliance issue, peripheral to erecting/inserting (low relevance), consequences administrative (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: EBR prompts reduce omissions but manual records still miss required activities. Omitted activities are found at review, delaying batch close. Complete recording depends on operator discipline. Omitted records force documentation rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of machine settings, checks, or quantities recorded inaccurately
rows.plain: Reduce the number of machine settings, checks, or amounts that are written down incorrectly. Accurate records help show what happened during production and support safe, consistent work.
rows.imp_rat: Settings/checks recorded inaccurately is a data-quality error, indirectly relevant to the CFJ, with modest consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: HMI logging and EBR reduce inaccurate entries, though manual quantities still get mis-recorded. Inaccurate settings/records surface as review delays. Accurate recording depends on operator care. Inaccurate records trigger investigations, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of stoppages, interventions, rejects, or deviations omitted from the packaging record
rows.plain: Record every stop, manual fix, rejected item, and problem during packaging. A complete record shows what really happened during the run.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted stoppages/deviations from the record is a documentation-compliance issue, peripheral to erecting/inserting (low relevance), consequences administrative (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automatic event logging helps where present, but manual capture of interventions is inconsistent. Omitted event records surface at review, delaying close. Capturing every stoppage and deviation depends on sustained operator vigilance that is scarce. Omitted events cause investigations, a documentation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packaging-record entries that are illegible or ambiguous
rows.plain: Reduce packaging records that are hard to read or unclear. Clear records help people know exactly what happened during the run.
rows.imp_rat: Illegible/ambiguous record entries is a documentation-quality error, indirectly relevant to the CFJ, with modest severity; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic records largely eliminate legibility issues where deployed, making this fairly well served. Illegible entries slow later review. Legibility depends on operator writing when using paper records. Ambiguous entries cause rework, a documentation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of packaging-record corrections lacking traceability to the original entry and operator
rows.plain: Make sure every correction to a packaging record shows the original information and who entered it. This preserves a clear history of what was changed and by whom.
rows.imp_rat: Record corrections lacking traceability is a documentation-compliance issue, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance), consequences administrative (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: EBR audit trails reliably enforce correction traceability where deployed, though paper corrections still lack it. Untraceable corrections slow review. Proper correction practice depends on documentation discipline. Untraceable corrections cause documentation investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of erected cartons outside squareness specification
rows.plain: Reduce the number of opened boxes that are not properly square and straight. Properly shaped boxes are more likely to close and handle correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Erected cartons outside squareness spec affects the core erection job, but the mandatory ceiling limits this batch to 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Forming tooling holds squareness fairly consistently on tuned machines. Out-of-square cartons cause insertion issues in-run. Squareness depends on erection tooling setup skill. Non-square cartons cause rejects, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of erected cartons with incorrectly seated flaps or tabs
rows.plain: Reduce the number of opened boxes whose closing flaps or tabs are not fitted into the right position. Correctly placed parts help the boxes stay closed.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly seated flaps/tabs affects the core erection job, but the mandatory ceiling limits this batch to 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Forming guides seat flaps fairly reliably but variation still causes defects. Poorly seated flaps cause closing issues in-run. Correct flap seating depends on forming setup skill. Bad flap seating causes rejects, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons torn, crushed, or permanently creased during erection
rows.plain: Fewer cartons should tear, be crushed, or develop lasting unwanted folds while they are being made. This helps produce cartons that are strong and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons crushed during erection hit the core job, yet the mandatory ceiling limits this batch to 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Forming tooling reduces erection damage but crush/crease still recurs. Cartons crushed during erection cause immediate rejects. Preventing erection damage depends on feeder and forming setup skill. Damaged cartons at erection are scrapped, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton blanks entering erection in the wrong orientation
rows.plain: Fewer flat carton pieces should enter the machine facing the wrong way. This helps the machine build cartons correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Carton blanks entering erection in wrong orientation is a core erection defect, but ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Orientation-keyed magazines and sensors reduce wrong-orientation feeds fairly reliably. Wrong-orientation blanks cause stops, costing time. Correct magazine loading requires operator attention. Misoriented blanks waste material and cause jams.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of carton blanks with score lines or cut features outside specification
rows.plain: Fewer flat carton pieces should have fold marks or cut features in the wrong place or outside the allowed limits. This helps prevent cartons from being formed incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-spec score lines could affect carton erection, but per the batch ceiling relevance and severity are capped; scored at floor as ranked below prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Incoming carton QC reduces out-of-spec blanks, making this fairly consistent though not eliminated. Out-of-spec score lines cause erection issues discovered in-run. This is an incoming-material issue outside operator skill. Bad blanks waste material and cause jams.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons containing a sterile unit from the wrong product or batch
rows.plain: Fewer cartons should contain a sterile product unit from the wrong product or production batch. This helps ensure each carton contains exactly what its label and records say it contains.
rows.imp_rat: Wrong product/batch sterile unit in a carton is a serious mix-up defect, but the ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation and scanning reduce mix-ups but the status quo cannot fully guarantee correct unit-carton matching. Wrong-product units are hard to catch in real time. Prevention depends on rigorous line clearance and staging discipline. Wrong-product cartons risk recalls, a severe cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile units with damaged primary packages after insertion
rows.plain: Prevent the product’s immediate container from being damaged when the product is placed into a carton. This helps keep the product protected and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile units with damaged primary packages after insertion is a defect, but ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Gentle-handling tooling helps but primary-package damage during insertion still occurs inconsistently. Damaged primary packages after insertion are found late. Gentle insertion setup requires skilled tuning. Damaged primary packages scrap sterile units, an expensive loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons containing missing or extra sterile units
rows.plain: Fewer cartons should contain too few or too many sterile units. This helps ensure every carton has the correct contents.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/extra sterile units strikes the core job directly, but the mandatory batch ceiling caps this at 1.0 average; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Checkweighing and count verification reliably catch most missing or extra units. Missing/extra units are caught at check-weigh, adding rework time. Detection is automated rather than skill-dependent. Missing/extra cartons become rejects, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with sterile units outside designated positions
rows.plain: Fewer sterile units should end up outside their assigned places inside the carton. This helps keep the product protected and presented correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile units outside designated positions is a core placement defect, but the mandatory ceiling limits this batch to 1.0; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Positioning guides reduce off-position units but variation still produces them. Mispositioned units cause insertion issues in-run. Correct positioning depends on tooling setup skill. Mispositioned units cause rejects and damage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile units with unreadable identification after insertion
rows.plain: Keep product identification clear and readable after the product is placed into a carton. This helps people tell the products apart and handle them correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Units with unreadable identification after insertion is a defect, but ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vision verification reliably flags unreadable identification on most lines. Unreadable IDs are caught by scanners without much delay. Detection relies on vision systems rather than operator skill. Unreadable IDs cause rejects, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartons crushed, torn, or deformed during unit insertion
rows.plain: Fewer cartons should be crushed, torn, or misshapen while the product unit is being put inside. Undamaged cartons protect the product and present it properly.
rows.imp_rat: Cartons crushed/torn during insertion is a real quality defect, but the ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Tuned insertion tooling reduces crush damage but it recurs with format variation. Crushed cartons during insertion cause immediate rejects. Preventing crush depends on correct insertion setup by techs. Damaged cartons and units are scrapped, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with required inserts missing or misplaced
rows.plain: Fewer cartons should be missing required inserts or have them in the wrong place. This helps ensure every carton is complete and arranged correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/misplaced required inserts affects the core job, yet the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Insert-presence detection helps but misplaced inserts still recur. Missing inserts are found downstream, adding rework. Insert placement depends on feeder setup skill. Missing/misplaced inserts cause rejects, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unexpected machine faults interrupting cartoning
rows.plain: The operator wants machines to fail less often without warning during packaging. This helps products keep moving into cartons without unexpected stops.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected machine faults interrupting cartoning bear on continuity but the ranking places this below batch 3; relevance and severity held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive maintenance reduces faults but unexpected machine failures still interrupt runs. Unexpected faults cause unplanned downtime. Handling unexpected faults depends on troubleshooting experience. Faults waste run time and sometimes material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with missing sterile units or inserts that pass monitoring
rows.plain: Fewer cartons missing sterile units or required inserts should pass through the checking process. This helps prevent incomplete cartons from being accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Missing units/inserts passing monitoring is a core quality failure, but the mandatory batch ceiling caps it at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Checkweigh and presence verification reliably prevent most missing-unit escapes. Missing-unit cartons passing monitoring create downstream rework. Detection is automated rather than skill-dependent. Escaped missing-unit cartons risk recalls, a severe cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartons with incorrect or unreadable identification that pass monitoring
rows.plain: Fewer cartons with wrong or hard-to-read labels should pass through the checking process. This helps ensure people can correctly identify each carton.
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable identification passing monitoring is quality-critical, but the batch ceiling forces a 1.0 cap; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Vision/OCR inspection reliably catches most incorrect or unreadable IDs before pass-through. Bad-ID cartons passing monitoring are found late, adding rework. Detection relies on vision systems, not operator skill. Escaped bad-ID cartons risk costly downstream issues.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of damaged or improperly closed cartons that pass monitoring
rows.plain: Reduce the number of damaged or poorly closed boxes that pass the inspection process. This helps stop defective products from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged/improperly closed cartons passing monitoring is quality-critical, but the mandatory ceiling caps this batch at 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Vision inspection catches most damaged/open cartons but some pass undetected. Damaged cartons passing monitoring cause downstream rework. Detection relies on inspection systems, not operator skill. Escaped damaged cartons risk complaints, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between the first nonconforming carton and operator recognition
rows.plain: Workers should notice the first incorrect carton sooner after it is produced. Faster recognition helps prevent more incorrect cartons from being made.
rows.imp_rat: Time between first nonconforming carton and recognition affects defect containment, but the batch ceiling caps it at 1.0; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Inline inspection shortens recognition where present, but manual monitoring leaves recognition slow and inconsistent. The lag between first nonconforming carton and operator recognition is the core delay and often long. Prompt recognition depends on operator vigilance and monitoring skill. Delayed recognition lets more defects accumulate, wasting material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of conforming cartons incorrectly classified as nonconforming
rows.plain: Reduce the number of good boxes that are mistakenly labeled as failing inspection. This prevents usable products from being treated as defective.
rows.imp_rat: Conforming cartons wrongly classified as nonconforming affects yield, but the mandatory ceiling limits this batch to 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Inspection tolerances cause inconsistent false-reject rates that are hard to tune. Over-rejection isn't a delay but a waste of good cartons. Avoiding false rejects depends on well-tuned inspection judgement and thresholds. Good cartons wrongly rejected are a direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conformance observations assigned to the wrong batch or sample
rows.plain: Reduce the number of test findings attached to the wrong production batch or sample. Correct matching is needed to know which work actually met the requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Conformance observations assigned to the wrong batch is a data-recording error, indirectly relevant to the CFJ, with modest severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Structured forms reduce misassignment but manual entry still allows errors. Misassigned observations are corrected slowly during review. Correct assignment depends on careful documentation practice. Misassignment causes documentation rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of startup cartons incorrectly erected, filled, or closed after changeover
rows.plain: Prevent the first cartons made after a change from being put together, filled, or closed incorrectly. This reduces waste and helps the process start correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Startup cartons wrongly processed after changeover touches the job, but the mandatory ceiling caps this batch at 1.0; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Setup verification catches bad startup cartons but their occurrence is routine. Bad startup cartons after changeover are scrapped quickly during verification. First-good-carton depends on skilled setup, a scarce competence. Startup rejects are a predictable material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required format components after changeover begins
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for the parts needed for the new product or setup after switching begins. This helps the line start making the new product sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for format components after changeover affects uptime, but the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Change-part kitting reduces waiting but missing or misplaced parts still cause delays. Waiting for format components after changeover begins is pure downtime. Locating parts is an organization task needing little skill. Waiting idles the line and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of format components damaged during removal, installation, or adjustment
rows.plain: Reduce damage to the parts used to make different product sizes or types when they are removed, installed, or adjusted.
rows.imp_rat: Format components damaged during changeover is a maintenance/rework defect, but ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Tool-less change parts and guides reduce damage, though careless handling still damages parts. Damaging format parts during changeover delays setup. Careful removal and installation of format parts requires trained technique. Damaged format components are expensive to replace.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of configuration adjustments repeated before startup approval
rows.plain: Reduce how often settings have to be changed again and again before a machine or process can be approved to start. This saves time and lowers the chance of setup mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Repeated configuration adjustments before approval is a rework error peripheral to steady-state cartoning, with modest consequence; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe recall reduces repeats, but fine-tuning still requires iterative adjustment. Repeated setup adjustments before approval prolong changeover. Fewer repeats depend on skilled first-time-right setup, a scarce competence. Each adjustment cycle wastes setup material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterile units cracked or deformed during transfer and insertion
rows.plain: Prevent germ-free product containers from cracking or changing shape while they are moved and inserted. This protects the product and avoids waste.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile units cracked during transfer/insertion strikes the core job and product integrity, but the batch ceiling caps it at 1.0; scored at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Gentle handling reduces cracking but transfer/insertion still deforms units inconsistently. Cracked units during transfer are found late, adding rework. Gentle transfer setup requires skilled tuning. Cracked sterile units are scrapped, an expensive loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile units with scraped, creased, or detached labels after handling
rows.plain: Prevent labels from being scraped, bent, or pulled off while the products are handled. This keeps the product information attached and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Units with scraped/creased/detached labels after handling is a labeling defect, but ranking places this batch below batch 3; both proxies capped at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Smooth transfer paths reduce label damage but scraping still happens with abrasive contact. Scraped or detached labels are found at inspection, adding rework. Gentle handling to protect labels depends on setup care. Damaged labels force relabel or scrap, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed cartons with insufficiently restrained sterile units
rows.plain: Reduce the number of finished boxes in which sterile items are not held securely in place. This helps prevent the items from moving around or being damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Insufficiently restrained sterile units is a core packaging-integrity defect, but the batch ceiling caps it at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Format parts provide restraint but fit variation leaves inconsistent restraint. Insufficient restraint is found downstream if at all. Proper restraint depends on correct format-part fit selected by techs. Poorly restrained units risk damage and rejects.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of replenishments containing materials that do not match the batch specification
rows.plain: Make sure every new supply of packaging materials matches the requirements for the batch being made. This prevents the wrong materials from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Replenishments not matching batch specification is a material-control defect, indirectly relevant to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode verification of materials at loading reduces mismatches fairly reliably. Wrong replenishment materials cause stops when discovered. Verifying replenishment against spec depends on operator checking. Wrong materials waste effort and risk mix-ups.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of replenished components damaged or unusable at the point of use
rows.plain: Make sure replacement packaging supplies are still usable when they reach the people who need them. This prevents delays and wasted materials.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged replenished components at point of use touches operation, but the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0; held at floor.
rows.sat_rat: Careful staging reduces damage but component damage during replenishment still recurs. Damaged replenishment components cause minor delays at point of use. Careful handling during replenishment depends on operator care. Damaged components at point of use are a direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of packaging material staged beyond the remaining run requirement
rows.plain: Avoid putting out more packaging materials than are needed for the rest of the production run. This reduces waste and keeps extra materials from being left over.
rows.imp_rat: Material staged beyond run requirement is inventory/waste efficiency, largely irrelevant to the job; trivial consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Staging to run quantity is standard practice and fairly consistent, with occasional overstaging. Over-staging isn't a time problem but a leftover-handling one. Right-sizing staged material needs planning discipline, not expertise. Excess staged material must be reconciled or scrapped, a direct waste cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the same interruption recurring after cartoning restarts
rows.plain: The operator wants a problem not to return after putting products into cartons starts again. This helps packaging continue without another interruption.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring interruptions after restart touch job continuity but the ranking places this below batch 3; both relevance and severity held at 1 per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without root-cause tools, the same interruption recurs frequently after restart. Recurring interruptions repeatedly consume restart time. Preventing recurrence requires diagnostic skill to fix root cause, not just symptoms. Repeated interruptions repeatedly waste material and time.

rows.source_job: Nothing to Fear in the Record
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of doubt that the packaging record accurately reflects performed actions
rows.plain: Be confident that the packaging record truthfully shows what people actually did. An accurate record protects the person and helps prove the work was completed properly.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt the record reflects actions is a documentation-trust matter, peripheral to erecting/inserting (low relevance, low severity).

rows.source_job: Nothing to Fear in the Record
rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty when justifying a packaging-record entry under GMP scrutiny
rows.plain: Quickly explain why an entry in the packaging record is correct when it is closely reviewed. This helps show that the record is accurate and can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty justifying a record entry under GMP is a documentation-defense matter, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.source_job: Nothing to Fear in the Record
rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting acceptance of carton and sterile-unit reconciliation
rows.plain: They want quick confirmation that the number of cartons and the number of medicine units match. This helps show that nothing is missing or incorrectly recorded.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting reconciliation acceptance is a psychological state, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty when carton flow and sterile-unit arrival diverge
rows.plain: Quickly understand why the movement of cartons does not match the arrival of germ-free units. This helps keep the work moving at the expected pace without losing control of the process.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty when carton flow and unit arrival diverge is a monitoring-state issue, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of losing composure after unexpected changes in cartoning pace
rows.plain: Make it easier to stay calm when the speed of putting products into boxes changes unexpectedly. This helps the worker keep the operation running safely and steadily.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure after pace changes is a psychological state, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while judging whether the current cartoning pace is sustainable
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while deciding whether the current speed of packing items into cartons can continue. This matters because they need to know the work can keep moving at that speed without causing problems.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety judging whether pace is sustainable is a psychological state, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty that each sterile unit matches the current carton configuration
rows.plain: Quickly confirm that every germ-free medicine container is matched with the correct current box design. This helps prevent units from being packed in the wrong boxes.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty each unit matches current configuration touches quality, but the batch ceiling caps this at 1.0; held at floor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety when sterile-unit and carton identity cues conflict
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries when the identifying marks on a sterile product and its package do not match. This matters because every product must be paired with the correct package.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety when identity cues conflict is a psychological state, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of lingering doubt after a sterile unit passes from immediate view
rows.plain: Quickly regain confidence that a sterile item is still correct after it moves out of immediate view. This matters because the worker cannot keep watching it directly.
rows.imp_rat: Lingering doubt after a unit leaves view is a psychological state, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that production leadership attributes a missed run commitment to the operator
rows.plain: The operator wants to avoid production leaders blaming them when a promised production run is missed. This matters because unfair blame could harm the operator’s reputation and future opportunities.
rows.imp_rat: Missed run commitment attributed to the operator is reputational, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost when quality personnel perceive procedural corner-cutting
rows.plain: They want to avoid seeming careless when quality staff think workers skipped or shortened required steps. This matters because following the full process helps keep the work safe and dependable.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost over perceived corner-cutting is reputational, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which shift leadership second-guesses the operator's cartoning decisions
rows.plain: Shift leaders should trust the worker’s decisions about putting products into boxes instead of repeatedly questioning them. This helps the work stay on schedule and finish reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Shift leadership second-guessing operator decisions is reputational, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that shift leadership bypasses the operator for pace-critical cartoning assignments
rows.plain: The operator wants to avoid shift leaders leaving them out when assigning urgent work to pack products into boxes. Being included helps ensure the work is assigned to someone who can complete it reliably and on time.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed for pace-critical assignments is reputational, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality or production leadership attributes a packaging mix-up to the operator
rows.plain: Quality or production leaders should not blame the operator if different products or packages are accidentally mixed up. The work should make such mix-ups unlikely and show who handled each step.
rows.imp_rat: Packaging mix-up attributed to the operator is reputational, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with quality and production leadership after a packaging discrepancy
rows.plain: The person doing the work wants to avoid losing the trust of quality and production leaders after products or packages are mixed up.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost after a packaging discrepancy is reputational, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the operator's account is second-guessed during deviation review
rows.plain: The worker’s explanation of what happened during an unusual event should be questioned less often. This matters because an accurate account helps people understand the problem and decide how to fix it.
rows.imp_rat: Operator's account second-guessed during deviation review is reputational, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of consequential sterile cartoning runs for which the operator is bypassed because of perceived mix-up risk
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important sterile packaging runs where the operator is left out because people fear items could be mixed up. This allows the trained operator to take part while keeping the work reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Operator bypassed on runs due to mix-up risk is reputational, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required change parts or approved packaging materials are unavailable when changeover starts
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that needed replacement parts or approved packing materials are missing when the equipment change begins. Having them ready helps the change happen without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Change parts/materials unavailable at changeover start is a readiness-delay error, indirectly relevant to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Kitting and staging usually ensure availability, but missing parts or materials still stall changeovers. Unavailable parts or materials at changeover start directly delays the whole job. Availability depends on planning, not operator skill. Waiting on missing parts wastes labor and capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of renewed alarm when a prior-format cue appears after line clearance is confirmed
rows.plain: Avoid becoming worried again when an old label, part, or other sign from the previous production run appears after the line has been cleaned and checked. This helps the operator trust that the line is truly ready.
rows.imp_rat: Renewed alarm when a prior-format cue appears is an emotional/perception error only loosely tied to the job output; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while first-run evidence is assessed for setup release
rows.plain: The worker wants less worry while the results from the first use are being checked before the setup is approved for use. This matters because early results can reveal problems before regular work begins.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety duration during first-run evidence assessment is a peripheral emotional-state error with low relevance to physically erecting cartons and inserting units, and mild consequences; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of renewed self-doubt after additional adjustments during first-run release
rows.plain: Avoid losing confidence after making more adjustments while releasing the first production run. This helps the operator trust that the changes were handled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Renewed self-doubt after adjustments is an emotional-state error only loosely tied to the CFJ, with minor consequence; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of alarm about causing personnel injury during cartoning-machine adjustments
rows.plain: Reduce fear that adjusting the carton-packaging machine could injure someone. Safe adjustment protects the people doing the work.
rows.imp_rat: Alarm about causing injury during adjustments is a psychological-safety error only loosely tied to the core cartoning output; capped at 1.0 by ranking.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supervisors or line operators attribute startup rejects to the operator's setup judgment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that supervisors or workers blame the person setting up the equipment when products are rejected at startup. The setup should work correctly without making that person seem at fault.
rows.imp_rat: Startup rejects attributed to operator setup judgment is a reputation error, tangential to physical cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with line personnel when post-startup adjustments are repeatedly required
rows.plain: Avoid losing line workers’ trust when changes are repeatedly needed after the equipment starts operating. This matters because the work should be set up correctly from the beginning and avoid disruption.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost from repeated post-startup adjustments is a reputation error, peripheral to steady-state cartoning; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which line personnel bypass the operator on changeover adjustment decisions
rows.plain: Workers on the production line should go around the operator less often when deciding how to adjust a changeover. This helps the change happen correctly the first time.
rows.imp_rat: Line personnel bypassing the operator on adjustments is an authority error, peripheral to the physical job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a packaging deviation is attributed to the operator's failure to follow the approved changeover procedure
rows.plain: Any packaging problem should not be blamed on the operator for failing to follow the approved steps for changing the setup. This protects the operator’s reputation for doing the work correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation attributed to operator procedure failure is a blame/reputation error, indirectly relevant to the CFJ; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with Quality Assurance when the operator's line-clearance evidence is challenged
rows.plain: They want quality staff to keep trusting the worker when they question the proof that the work area was cleared and ready before starting. This matters because a clean, properly prepared area helps prevent mistakes and contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when line-clearance evidence is challenged is a reputation error remote from the CFJ, with modest stakes; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that blame for a label, carton, leaflet, or sterile-unit mix-up is publicly assigned to the operator
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the operator is publicly blamed when labels, boxes, instruction sheets, or sterile products are mixed up. This helps ensure the source of the mix-up is found before responsibility is assigned.
rows.imp_rat: Public blame for a mix-up assigned to the operator is a reputation/contamination-perception error, tangential to physical cartoning here; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of staffing mismatch after packaging workload changes
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to quickly adjust the number of workers when the packaging workload changes. This helps prevent having too few or too many people for the work.
rows.imp_rat: Staffing mismatch after workload change is a resourcing error with peripheral relevance to the CFJ and modest consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Workload-driven staffing mismatches recur with schedule volatility. Staffing mismatch after workload changes persists until reallocation. Rebalancing staff to workload depends on the overseer's flexibility and cross-training. Mismatch means either idle labor or unmet demand, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoning personnel committed simultaneously to other production activities
rows.plain: Fewer workers assigned to putting products into cartons should also be committed to other production jobs at the same time. This helps ensure enough people are available to finish the cartoning work.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel committed simultaneously to other activities is a resourcing error with peripheral relevance and modest consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Shared personnel create unpredictable availability for cartoning. Personnel split across activities can slow cartoning response. Managing shared commitments requires coordination judgement. Double-committed labor is inefficient use of resource.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of within-shift personnel reassignments that disrupt cartoning continuity
rows.plain: The overseer wants to avoid moving people between tasks during the same work shift when doing so would interrupt packaging. Stable staffing helps cartons keep moving.
rows.imp_rat: Within-shift reassignments disrupting continuity is a staffing-stability error with peripheral relevance and modest consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reassignments are a recurring source of disrupted cartoning continuity. Within-shift reassignments interrupt flow and cost transition time. Managing reassignments needs the overseer's judgement to preserve continuity. Disrupted continuity reduces effective output.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of assigned labor capacity exceeding packaging workload demand
rows.plain: Avoid assigning more worker time than the packaging work actually requires. This helps use staff efficiently and avoid paying for unused capacity.
rows.imp_rat: Labor capacity exceeding workload demand is a utilization/waste error peripheral to the core job, with minor consequence; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Overstaffing is a manageable, low-risk inefficiency. Excess labor capacity has little time impact. Right-sizing labor requires the overseer's planning skill. Labor exceeding demand is direct wasted labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among approved packaging specifications, batch instructions, and market requirements
rows.plain: The supervisor wants the packaging details, work instructions, and market requirements to agree with one another. This matters because conflicting instructions could lead to the wrong packaging being used.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among specifications, instructions, and market requirements is a documentation-ambiguity error tangential to executing cartoning; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts among specs, instructions, and market requirements recur and undermine certainty. Reconciling spec conflicts takes time before a run can proceed. Resolving spec/instruction/market conflicts needs expert interpretation. Unresolved conflicts risk wrong output and rejects.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cartoning stoppages caused by constraints in removing completed secondary packages
rows.plain: Spend less time stopping the box-packing line because finished packages cannot be moved away. Removing completed packages smoothly helps the line keep running.
rows.imp_rat: Stoppages from removing completed packages is a throughput/waste error, indirectly relevant to the CFJ, with modest consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Downstream removal constraints intermittently back up the line. Stoppages from downstream pack-removal constraints add wait time. Little specialized skill involved; a material-flow issue. Backup-driven stoppages waste line capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unused cartoning capacity caused by schedule changes or sequencing gaps
rows.plain: Keep the packaging machine from sitting unused for long periods because of schedule changes or poor timing between jobs. Better use of the machine helps avoid wasted production time.
rows.imp_rat: Unused capacity from schedule gaps is a utilization/waste error remote from the physical job, low-severity here; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Schedule-change gaps recur and are hard to fully avoid. Unused capacity from sequencing gaps is idle time on the line. Reducing gaps needs skilled scheduling and sequencing. Idle capacity from gaps is wasted committed cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cartoning downtime events recorded with missing or incorrect cause classifications
rows.plain: When the cartoning machine stops, its cause should be recorded correctly and completely more often. This helps the team understand and reduce stoppages.
rows.imp_rat: Downtime events recorded with wrong cause classification is a data-quality error peripheral to executing cartoning; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Downtime cause records are frequently incomplete or misclassified. Correcting cause classifications later adds minor review time. Accurate cause coding depends on operator knowledge and diligence. Miscoded downtime mainly costs analytical accuracy, not product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between cartoning disruption onset and notification of the job overseer
rows.plain: The person overseeing the work should be told sooner after a problem starts while products are being put into cartons. Early notice allows the problem to be addressed more quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Time to notify the overseer of a disruption is a communication-delay error, tangential to executing cartoning; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation timing is inconsistent absent automated alerting. Delay in notifying the overseer of disruption extends response time. Notification depends on operator awareness and communication discipline. Late notification prolongs downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from disruption assessment to an authorized recovery decision when recovery direction conflicts
rows.plain: Make it faster to choose an approved way to recover after a disruption, especially when people disagree about what to do. This helps restore work safely and quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Time to authorized recovery decision under conflict is a decision-delay error, indirectly relevant to the CFJ, low-severity here; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting-direction situations produce unpredictable decision delays. Conflicting recovery direction delays the authorized decision. Resolving conflict needs experienced authority and judgement. Decision delay prolongs costly downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the disruption remains unresolved while qualified personnel are unavailable
rows.plain: Make it quicker to solve a production problem when qualified workers are unavailable. This helps reduce the time that work remains disrupted.
rows.imp_rat: Delay while qualified personnel are unavailable is a staffing/logistics error only loosely tied to the core cartoning job, with modest consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified-personnel availability during events is inconsistent. Disruptions stay unresolved longer when qualified staff are unavailable. Resolution hinges on availability of specific qualified troubleshooters. Prolonged unresolved downtime wastes capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt that first-off cartons after changeover match the approved configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the first boxes made after the equipment or setup is changed match the approved design. This helps prevent incorrect products from moving forward.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that first-off cartons match configuration is an assurance-perception error, indirectly relevant to the core job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty when line reports conflict about coding or leaflet correctness
rows.plain: The team needs to quickly resolve conflicting reports about whether the product labels and instruction leaflets are correct.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty from conflicting coding/leaflet reports is an information-resolution issue, peripheral to physical insertion (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety episodes while awaiting confirmation of a suspected configuration discrepancy
rows.plain: A supervisor wants confirmation about a suspected mismatch in the equipment’s settings. This helps ensure the equipment is set up as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety episodes awaiting discrepancy confirmation are psychological, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure while the line team awaits a configuration go-or-hold decision
rows.plain: Stay calm while the line team waits to hear whether the current setup can proceed or must stop. This helps the team avoid starting with an unsafe or unapproved setup.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure awaiting a go-or-hold decision is a psychological state, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty about whether cartoning documentation is inspection-ready
rows.plain: The team needs to know quickly whether the paperwork for putting products into cartons is complete and ready for an inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about documentation inspection-readiness is an administrative-anxiety item, peripheral to cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of composure loss when line-clearance and batch-record status signals conflict
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to stay calm when the cleanup status and the production records give different signals. This matters because conflicting information makes it harder to know whether work is ready to continue.
rows.imp_rat: Composure loss under conflicting status signals is psychological, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when asked to justify documentation readiness without a defensible answer
rows.plain: They want to avoid feeling caught off guard when asked to prove that the records are complete and ready for review. They need a clear, supportable answer.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed on documentation readiness is psychological/administrative, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety episodes while questionable line-clearance or batch-record entries remain unresolved
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to worry less while questionable records about clearing away earlier materials or documenting a production batch remain unresolved. This matters because unclear records may cause problems during an inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over unresolved line-clearance entries is psychological/administrative, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of visible uncertainty about whether cartoning will meet the next batch handoff
rows.plain: The team needs to know quickly whether the carton-packing work will be finished in time for the next handoff.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty about batch handoff is a communication/psychological state, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance) with mild consequences (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of composure loss when cartoning pace departs from plan
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to stay calm when the pace of packing differs from the plan. This matters because they need to respond clearly instead of being unsettled by a delay or sudden increase.
rows.imp_rat: Composure loss when pace departs from plan is psychological, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety episodes while awaiting confirmation of current cartoning status
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to worry less while waiting to learn whether the products are currently being packed into boxes as planned. This matters because knowing the packing status helps them keep the work on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting status confirmation is a psychological state, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of self-doubt under conflicting signals about cartoning pace and handoff readiness
rows.plain: Spend less time doubting decisions when signs about packing speed and readiness to pass work to the next team do not agree. This helps the supervisor decide whether production is truly on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Self-doubt under conflicting pace signals is a psychological state, peripheral to the physical job (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to give stakeholders a defensible cartoning pace call
rows.plain: The person wants to give a clear and well-supported answer about how quickly products can be packed into cartons. This matters because leaders need to know whether work is keeping up with the plan.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed without a defensible pace call is psychological, peripheral to physical cartoning (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of plant leadership attributing a missed cartoning commitment to the overseer's governance
rows.plain: Avoid having plant leaders blame the person overseeing the work when a promised cartoning job is missed. The schedule should be managed reliably so commitments are met.
rows.imp_rat: Missed commitment attributed to the overseer's governance is a blame/reputation error tangential to executing cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of plant leadership publicly second-guessing the overseer's future batch commitments
rows.plain: They want plant leaders to stop publicly questioning whether the overseer can keep future production promises. This helps the team and leaders trust the schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Leadership second-guessing batch commitments is reputational and peripheral to the cartoning function (low relevance, low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cartoning schedule decisions in which plant leadership bypasses the overseer because of perceived delivery unreliability
rows.plain: Fewer decisions about the cartoning schedule should be made without the supervisor because plant leaders do not trust that person to deliver on time. Reliable planning should make leaders comfortable relying on the supervisor.
rows.imp_rat: Schedule decisions bypassing the overseer is an authority error remote from physical cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of inspection findings being attributed to the overseer's compliance governance
rows.plain: They want to avoid inspection problems being blamed on the person responsible for making sure the organization follows the rules. This matters because that person needs confidence that the oversight system is working properly.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of inspection findings to the overseer's governance is administrative/reputational, peripheral to the packaging job (low relevance) with absorbable consequences (low severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility loss with quality assurance when accountable batch records are challenged
rows.plain: Keep quality staff confident when they question records showing who did what during a production run. The records should clearly and truthfully show that the required work was completed.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss over challenged batch records is a reputation error remote from physical cartoning; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of stakeholders bypassing the overseer in line-readiness decisions because they doubt the overseer's compliance assurances
rows.plain: People involved in preparing a production line should not go around the overseer because they doubt the overseer's promises that rules are being followed. This matters because bypassing the overseer can weaken coordination and compliance.
rows.imp_rat: Stakeholders bypassing the overseer over doubted assurances is an authority error remote from physical cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the overseer being blamed for schedule commitments missed after compliance holds
rows.plain: Avoid being blamed when compliance checks stop work and cause promised dates to be missed. This helps protect the person overseeing the work from taking responsibility for delays outside their control.
rows.imp_rat: Overseer blamed for missed commitments after holds is a reputation error remote from physical cartoning; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which operators and shift leaders view line-clearance expectations as inconsistently enforced
rows.plain: Make operators and shift leaders see the rules for checking that an area is completely cleared as being applied consistently. This helps prevent leftover materials or paperwork from causing mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent line-clearance enforcement perception is a process-variability/perception error, tangential to erecting cartons; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety triggered by conflicting reports about the cartoning batch's deviation status
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries when different reports disagree about whether a packaging batch has a known problem. This matters because they need to know the batch’s true status and what action to take.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety from conflicting deviation reports is an emotional-state error only loosely tied to the CFJ; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to explain the cartoning batch's status to unit leaders
rows.plain: The person wants to clearly explain to department leaders whether the batch of products being packed into cartons is acceptable, delayed, or has a problem. This matters because leaders need an accurate status to decide what to do next.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to explain batch status is an interpersonal-reputation error, peripheral to erecting and inserting units, with limited consequence; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety while awaiting confirmation of a suspect carton's disposition
rows.plain: They want fewer sudden worries while waiting to learn what will happen to a possibly faulty package. This matters because they need confidence that the package will not reach a customer by mistake.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting a suspect carton's disposition is an emotional/delay error remote from the core job, low-severity here; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when pressed for a disposition decision without conclusive carton-status evidence
rows.plain: Stay calm when asked to decide what to do with a package even though there is not enough clear evidence about its condition. This helps prevent possibly affected packages from being wrongly accepted or released.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure under pressure for a disposition is an emotional-state error remote from physical cartoning, with limited stakes; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inconsistent packaging-governance judgments cause quality leadership to second-guess the overseer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that different decisions about packaging rules make quality leaders question the overseer’s judgment. This matters because packaging decisions need to be consistent and clearly supported.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent governance judgments causing second-guessing is a reputation error, tangential to physical cartoning, held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the overseer publicly misses a first-pass batch commitment during unit reviews
rows.plain: The person overseeing the work wants to meet promises to complete a group of products correctly the first time and not be shown as failing during team reviews. This protects trust in their performance.
rows.imp_rat: Publicly missing a first-pass batch commitment is a reputation/delay error, tangential to physical cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recurring cartoning rework and holds define the overseer's reputation with quality and site leadership
rows.plain: The overseer wants repeated packaging repairs and production stoppages to be seen as problems being managed, not as a sign of poor leadership. Consistent, correct work helps protect their reputation.
rows.imp_rat: Rework/holds defining the overseer's reputation is a reputation error, tangential to erecting and inserting units; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of carton material exceeding protection and machinability needs
rows.plain: Avoid using more cardboard or other carton material than needed to protect the product and work properly with the packing machines. This reduces waste without weakening the package.
rows.imp_rat: Excess carton material beyond protection needs is efficiency waste, largely irrelevant to whether cartons erect and units insert; trivial consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Reducing material must be balanced against protection, and over-spec is common but rarely causes failures. Optimizing material use requires design iteration time but is not urgent. Right-sizing material draws on value-engineering skill that is reasonably available. Excess material is a direct, ongoing overspend that trimming reduces.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of internal carton volume left unused after sterile-unit insertion
rows.plain: Use as much of the space inside each carton as practical after the sterile item is placed inside. This reduces wasted packaging space and material.
rows.imp_rat: Unused internal carton volume is an efficiency/waste metric, largely irrelevant to whether units insert successfully; trivial consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Volume optimization must respect protection needs, so some slack is intentionally retained and stable. Assessing unused internal volume is a quick design calculation. Right-sizing carton volume is routine packaging-design work. Unused volume wastes material and shipping space, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of printed carton stock rendered obsolete by artwork or configuration changes
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of printed carton packaging that becomes unusable when its design or settings change. This saves materials and avoids throwing away cartons that can no longer be used.
rows.imp_rat: Printed stock obsoleted by artwork changes is a waste/inventory issue, unrelated to whether cartons erect; low consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Just-in-time and version controls limit obsolescence but changes still strand some printed stock. Managing print obsolescence requires inventory and change coordination time. Minimizing obsolete stock needs planning skill rather than rare expertise. Obsolete printed stock is a direct write-off cost driven by artwork changes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt that the cartoning specification can withstand traceability, labeling, and validation scrutiny
rows.plain: Make sure the box-making requirements can stand up to close checks of product tracking, labels, and proof that the process works correctly. This helps the purchase remain acceptable during reviews.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that the specification withstands scrutiny is an assurance-perception error, tangential to executing cartoning, with low stakes here; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty when explaining the packaging rationale to auditors
rows.plain: Make it quick and easy to explain why the product was packaged in a particular way when auditors ask. This reassures them that the packaging choice was sensible and properly supported.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty explaining rationale to auditors is a confidence error peripheral to physical cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing calm when reviewers challenge validation evidence for the cartoning specification
rows.plain: They want to avoid becoming unsettled when reviewers question the proof that the box-packing requirements were properly tested. This matters because they need to stay confident that the packaging choice is supported by evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Losing calm when validation evidence is challenged is an emotional-state error peripheral to executing cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when decision-makers challenge packaging cost, qualification, change-control, or scale-up trade-offs
rows.plain: Stay calm when decision-makers question the costs, testing, future changes, or growth plans for the packaging choice. This helps the person explain why the recommendation is a safe and practical option.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over trade-off challenges is an emotional-state error peripheral to executing cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty when committing to the cartoning-machine recommendation
rows.plain: The recommender needs to quickly feel certain about choosing the proposed box-packing machine. This helps make the decision without unnecessary delay or fear of avoidable risk.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty when committing to the recommendation is a confidence/reputation error, tangential to the CFJ; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of post-commitment doubt when new packaging evidence conflicts with the recommendation rationale
rows.plain: Quickly resolve doubts after committing to a recommendation if new information about the packaging conflicts with the reasons behind it. This helps the recommender know whether the decision still stands.
rows.imp_rat: Post-commitment doubt from conflicting evidence is a decision-confidence error, peripheral to physical cartoning; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unresolved uncertainty when approved specifications and sterile-quality controls conflict
rows.plain: Quickly resolve a conflict between the approved product requirements and the controls needed to keep it germ-free. This helps the right people approve specifications that protect product quality.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved uncertainty when specs and quality controls conflict is a governance-timing error, peripheral to the physical job; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of visible hesitation when stakeholders challenge packaging requirements during sign-off
rows.plain: The buyer wants to stay confident and composed when people question packaging requirements during final approval. This makes it easier to explain and defend the requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Visible hesitation when requirements are challenged is a confidence error, indirectly relevant to the core job, low-severity here; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of feeling exposed when specification assumptions lack documentary support during sign-off
rows.plain: Avoid feeling vulnerable when the assumptions behind a requirement have no written support during approval. Documented reasons make the requirement easier to review and sign off.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over unsupported assumptions at sign-off is an emotional/documentation error, peripheral to the job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which quality and regulatory leaders bypass the specifier during packaging approval
rows.plain: Make sure quality and regulatory leaders include the person who sets packaging requirements when approving packaging. This helps ensure the packaging meets safety and legal needs.
rows.imp_rat: Leaders bypassing the specifier during approval is an organizational-authority error, peripheral to the core job; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that line-output shortfalls are publicly attributed to the specifier's configuration judgment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that leaders publicly blame the person who set up the production line when the line makes less than expected. The setup should be judged fairly rather than treated as the automatic cause of poor output.
rows.imp_rat: Output shortfalls publicly attributed to the specifier is a reputation error, peripheral to physical cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which production leaders second-guess the specifier's configuration criteria
rows.plain: Production leaders should question the specialist’s rules for setting up equipment less often. Clear, trusted setup rules help the production line run reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Production leaders second-guessing configuration criteria is a reputation error, peripheral to the core job; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement across quality, engineering, production, and supplier assessments about observed cartoning failures
rows.plain: Make sure quality, engineering, production, and the supplier reach the same conclusion about problems seen when products are put into boxes. This helps the team judge the risk of those failures consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Cross-functional disagreement about observed failures is a coordination error with indirect relevance and modest severity here; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Stakeholder disagreement about observed failures is common and hard to resolve objectively. Cross-functional disagreement extends decision cycles. Aligning quality, engineering, production, and supplier views requires facilitation and expert consensus-building. Prolonged disputes cost labor and schedule.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for complete machine, material, quality, and qualification information after a deviation
rows.plain: Make it quicker to collect all the needed information about the machine, materials, quality, and testing after something goes wrong. Complete information helps people find the real cause of the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for complete information after a deviation is an information-delay error, tangential to executing cartoning, with modest stakes; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Fragmented systems make complete post-deviation information slow to assemble, a persistent pain. Waiting for complete information after a deviation is a well-known, chronic delay. Assembling the information needs coordination more than deep skill. Idle waiting time carries schedule and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from completion of cartoning evaluation activities to issuance of the acceptance recommendation
rows.plain: The recommendation about whether to accept the carton-packing solution should be issued sooner after all evaluation work is finished. This helps the final decision happen without unnecessary delay.
rows.imp_rat: Time from evaluation completion to recommendation issuance is a process-delay error remote from the physical job, low-severity here; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Review and approval workflows are established, so issuance eventually happens predictably despite the lag. The lag from evaluation completion to recommendation issuance is a recognized bottleneck. Report synthesis and sign-off require experienced authors, adding to the delay. Delay defers decisions but has limited direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality reviewers overturn the evaluator's cartoning qualification recommendation because supporting evidence cannot be reproduced
rows.plain: Quality reviewers should not reject the evaluator’s recommendation that a box-packing process was properly tested just because the supporting proof cannot be recreated. The test evidence should be complete and repeatable.
rows.imp_rat: Reviewers overturning a recommendation over irreproducible evidence is a documentation error, indirectly relevant and low-severity per ranking; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality reviewers perceive inconsistent application of criteria to carton erection and sterile-unit insertion evidence
rows.plain: The reviewer wants the evidence about putting cartons together and placing sterile units inside them to be judged by the same rules each time. This helps prevent the review from looking unfair or unreliable.
rows.imp_rat: Perceived inconsistent criteria application is a review-perception error remote from the physical job, with modest stakes; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which decision makers bypass the evaluator in subsequent cartoning qualifications after a recommendation is successfully challenged
rows.plain: After a recommendation has been successfully questioned, decision-makers should still involve the evaluator in later checks of whether cartons and their process meet the requirements. This preserves useful expertise in future decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Bypassed after a challenged recommendation is an authority error remote from the physical job; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the evaluator loses credibility with quality and leadership reviewers because the cartoning qualification rationale is not scrutiny-ready
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the evaluator loses the trust of quality and senior management reviewers because the explanation for checking the carton-packaging equipment is not strong enough to withstand questions. This matters because approval may become harder to obtain.
rows.imp_rat: Losing credibility over non-scrutiny-ready rationale is a reputation error remote from the job; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the launch team attributes a late-discovered cartoning integration risk to incomplete qualification criteria
rows.plain: The launch team should not later blame incomplete testing requirements for a packaging connection problem that was discovered late. The risk should be identified and assigned fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Integration risk attributed to incomplete criteria is a blame/analysis error, indirectly relevant to the core job; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which production and quality leaders attribute sterile fill-finish continuity disruptions during carton-format changeovers to the evaluator's recommendation
rows.plain: Production and quality leaders should blame the evaluator’s recommendation less often when germ-free product filling and final packaging are interrupted during a carton change. This helps the team assess the recommendation fairly instead of assigning blame without clear evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Continuity disruptions attributed to the recommendation is a blame/reputation error remote from physical cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of evaluator credibility loss with procurement and production leaders when supplier-support limitations emerge after machine selection
rows.plain: Avoid losing credibility with purchasing and production leaders when limits in the supplier’s support appear after the machine has been chosen. This means identifying support problems before they affect the decision.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility loss when supplier-support limits emerge is a reputation error remote from physical cartoning; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that engineering and operations bypass the evaluator in cartoning-machine decisions after judging the early-risk guidance incomplete
rows.plain: Provide enough early information about possible problems with the box-packing machine so engineering and operations continue to involve the evaluator in the decision. This helps prevent important risks from being missed.
rows.imp_rat: Engineering/operations bypassing the evaluator is an organizational-authority error remote from erecting cartons; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that launch stakeholders characterize the evaluator as an obstacle because cartoning-risk warnings lack clear decision relevance
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that people involved in starting the work see the evaluator as slowing things down because warnings about carton-packing risks do not clearly help them decide what to do. The warnings should be tied to practical choices.
rows.imp_rat: Being characterized as an obstacle over unclear warnings is a reputation error, tangential to executing cartoning; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of recurring changeover labor and material expense excluded from configuration comparison
rows.plain: Include all recurring worker time and material costs for switching between product setups when comparing equipment options. This gives a fairer comparison.
rows.imp_rat: Changeover labor/material expense excluded from comparison is a costing-analysis error peripheral to the job with minor consequences; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration comparisons commonly omit these recurring costs, making them consistently incomplete. Compiling changeover labor and material expense is not a time issue but is frequently deferred. Capturing recurring changeover costs into configuration comparisons requires costing discipline seldom applied. Recurring changeover labor and material expense is routinely excluded from comparisons, hiding real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty while defending specified serialization requirements for cartoned sterile units during inspection
rows.plain: Quickly explain and defend why each packaged germ-free unit needs a unique identifying number during an inspection. This helps show that the units can be tracked and that the requirement is justified.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty defending serialization requirements during inspection is an assurance-timing error, indirectly relevant, low-severity per ranking; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of doubt that unit-to-carton traceability can be explained across permitted secondary packaging configurations during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure each unit can be linked to the box it goes into, and that this link can be clearly explained for every allowed box-and-label arrangement during an inspection. This helps show where products came from and how they were packed.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about explaining traceability during inspection is an assurance-perception error, indirectly relevant and low-severity per the ranking; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when serialization exceptions lack a defensible rationale before the inspection team
rows.plain: The person wants a clear, well-supported reason for any exceptions in the system that tracks products through production and delivery. This matters because inspectors may question why those exceptions were allowed.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when serialization exceptions lack rationale is an emotional-state error remote from physical cartoning; held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty that rejection logic prevents mix-ups without corrupting aggregation during final recommendation review
rows.plain: Know sooner that the rules for rejecting items will stop products from being mixed up while still correctly combining the final results. This supports a reliable final recommendation.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about rejection logic during recommendation review is an analysis-timing error, tangential to cartoning execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt caused by conflicting interpretations of rejection and aggregation evidence while finalizing the recommendation
rows.plain: The buyer needs clear proof of which results were rejected and how the remaining results were combined. Confusion could cause incorrect information to be used in the final recommendation.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting interpretations of rejection/aggregation evidence is an analysis-ambiguity error, tangential to cartoning execution; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting resolution of an ambiguous rejection exception before recommendation approval
rows.plain: The buyer wants a confusing exception about a rejected item resolved before approving the recommendation. This matters so the wrong item is not accepted by mistake.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting rejection-exception resolution is an emotional/delay error, indirectly relevant to the CFJ, with modest stakes; capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether proposed unit-to-carton relationships are defensible while stakeholders await a recommendation
rows.plain: Know quickly whether the proposed number of items in each box can be clearly supported while others wait for a recommendation. This helps the recommender explain why the proposed arrangement makes sense.
